guides.title: Choosing the Right Emergency Shelter Type

guideData.title: Choosing the Right Emergency Shelter Type
guideData.summary: Three main shelter types — tarp, natural lean-to, and debris hut — have very different trade-offs in speed, warmth, and required materials. Choosing the wrong type wastes critical time or leaves you cold overnight.
guideData.whenToUse: You are deciding which shelter to build and have multiple options available
guideData.preferredOption: If you have a tarp: rig it immediately (fastest rain protection). If no tarp and temperatures are dangerous: build a debris hut. If no tarp and you have fire: build a natural lean-to.
guideData.fallbackOption: In arid, alpine, or snow-covered terrain with no leaf litter: use a lean-to with emergency blanket insulation or seek natural windbreak (rock, fallen log) rather than attempting a debris hut.
warnings: No shelter type compensates for inadequate ground insulation — sleeping on bare ground causes rapid conductive heat loss regardless of the shelter overhead.
A lean-to without fire is inadequate for survival in temperatures below 50°F.
A debris hut requires abundant dry leaf litter; attempting to build one in arid or alpine terrain without suitable materials wastes time that could be spent on fire-making or signaling.
Building any shelter larger than you need reduces its ability to retain body heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not spend time on a debris hut if you cannot find at least arm's-length (3 feet) depth of dry leaves nearby — the material requirement is non-negotiable.
Do not orient a lean-to into the prevailing wind — it will funnel cold air and rain into the shelter.
Do not assume a tarp alone provides warmth — it sheds rain but does not insulate; pair it with ground debris or sleeping gear in cold conditions.
redFlags: You chose a lean-to but cannot build or maintain a fire — switch to debris hut construction or seek better natural windbreak immediately.
Night is approaching and your shelter is still incomplete — prioritize finishing the floor insulation above all else; lying on bare ground will cause hypothermia even in mild temperatures.
preparednessTips: Carry a tarp (at minimum 8x10 feet) and 20 feet of paracord — they provide the fastest shelter setup in almost any environment.
Know how to identify leaf-litter-rich terrain (deciduous forests) vs. environments where a debris hut won't work (desert, tundra, alpine above treeline).
Practice both a lean-to and a debris hut in good conditions so you can execute either under stress.
An emergency mylar blanket (2 oz) dramatically improves the warmth of any improvised shelter when used as a ground layer or wrapped around your body inside.

guides.title: Build a Debris Hut for Overnight Warmth

guideData.title: Build a Debris Hut for Overnight Warmth
guideData.summary: Build a debris hut — a compact, fully enclosed natural shelter — that uses body heat and thick leaf insulation to stay warm down to 0°F without fire. It takes several hours to build but is the most thermally effective no-equipment shelter in forested environments.
guideData.whenToUse: You must survive overnight without fire in cold conditions
guideData.preferredOption: Build a full debris hut with ridgepole, ribbed frame, 3-foot exterior debris layer, insulated floor, and a stuffed door plug to fully seal body heat inside.
guideData.fallbackOption: If time or debris is limited, build a smaller debris cocoon — dig into a leaf pile 2–3 feet deep, burrow in feet-first, and pull debris over yourself to trap body heat.
steps: Choose a site with abundant dry leaf litter, grasses, or dry moss nearby. Avoid areas under widow-maker branches, near ant or wasp nests, and away from creek banks or low ground where water pools.
Find a ridgepole longer than your body (at least 9–10 feet). Prop one end on a stump, forked branch, or rock about waist height, with the other end on the ground to create a sloped spine.

Lean shorter 'rib' branches closely and evenly against both sides of the ridgepole to form a tent-like frame. Then weave a tight lattice of thinner branches and vines through the ribs to hold debris in place.
Size the interior just large enough for your body. An oversized shelter will stay cold because body heat cannot warm the excess space.

Pile dry leaves, grasses, or moss on the outside, starting at the bottom and working upward. Build the debris layer to at least 3 feet thick (arms-length depth) on all sides. Conifer needles (pine, spruce) are poor insulators — use them only as a last resort.
Pack the interior floor with 1–2 feet of compressed leaves or grass as a sleeping mat. This ground layer is as important as the outer insulation — cold ground pulls heat away through conduction.

Leave a small opening at the high end of the ridgepole just large enough to crawl through. Create a door plug by stuffing a shirt, bag, or a large armful of leaves to fully seal the entrance from inside.
Enter feet-first to avoid disturbing the debris walls. Immediately seal the entrance with the door plug behind you.
warnings: A debris hut requires abundant dry leaf litter; it is not feasible in arid, alpine, or desert environments where this material is scarce.
Skimping on interior floor insulation is the most common failure — sleeping on inadequately insulated ground causes rapid conductive heat loss even inside a well-built hut.
An oversized interior cannot be warmed by body heat alone; build it snug around your body size.
Wet or damp debris provides almost no insulation; if debris is wet, look for deeper, sheltered leaf litter or consider a lean-to with a fire instead.
whatNotToDo: Do not use conifer needles (pine, spruce) as the primary insulation layer — they trap less air than broad leaves and are significantly less effective.
Do not build the debris layer thinner than 3 feet — a shallow layer will not retain body heat in cold overnight conditions.
Do not enter the shelter head-first — you risk dislodging the debris walls and reducing insulation integrity.
Do not leave the entrance unsealed — even a small opening will allow cold air to displace trapped body heat.
redFlags: You are still shivering 20–30 minutes after entering the sealed shelter — insulation is insufficient; add more debris if possible or exit and build a fire.
Water is seeping into the floor of the shelter — the site is in a low-lying drainage; abandon and rebuild on higher, drier ground.
The debris pile is collapsing or thinning — the lattice framework was too loose; reinforce the frame before re-entering.
preparednessTips: Practice building a debris hut at least once in good conditions — the technique is physically demanding and full-sized construction takes 2–4 hours of effort.
In cold seasons, scout for suitable debris hut sites (forested areas with deep leaf litter) when planning backcountry travel.
Carrying a small emergency mylar blanket adds significant warmth with minimal weight and can supplement a debris hut's interior insulation.

guides.title: Build a Natural Lean-To Frame Shelter

guideData.title: Build a Natural Lean-To Frame Shelter
guideData.summary: Build a wind-and-rain shelter using branches and natural materials — no tarp needed. A lean-to can be constructed in under an hour and provides immediate overhead cover, but requires a fire on its open side for cold-weather warmth.
guideData.whenToUse: You are caught outdoors without a tarp or tent and need shelter quickly
guideData.preferredOption: Find two trees 6–8 feet apart, lash a ridgepole between them, lean rafters against it at 45°, and layer natural debris as shingles starting from the bottom.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no suitable trees exist, use two forked sticks as upright supports lashed together at the top to hold the ridgepole.
steps: Choose a flat, dry site on elevated ground, away from flood zones and low points; check overhead for dead branches ('widow makers') that could fall.
Find two trees 6–8 feet apart (or set two forked sticks as uprights). Lash a sturdy horizontal ridgepole between them at waist-to-shoulder height using paracord, vines, bark strips, or shoelaces.

Lean long branches ('rafters') at a 45-degree angle against the ridgepole on one side to form the sloped roof. Space them 6–8 inches apart — closer in heavy snow conditions to prevent collapse under weight.
Weave smaller branches horizontally through the rafters to create a lattice that will hold insulation in place.
Starting from the bottom, layer pine boughs, leafy branches, bark pieces, or leaf clusters over the frame — overlap each row upward like shingles so rain runs off rather than in.

Pile floor debris (pine needles, dry leaves, soft bark) 4–6 inches thick to create a thermal break between your body and the cold ground. Ground conduction causes rapid heat loss even in mild weather.
Orient the open face of the lean-to away from the prevailing wind. Optionally add vertical branch bundles on the sides of the entrance to block crosswind.
If temperatures are cold, build a fire on the open side of the lean-to to reflect heat into the shelter — the lean-to's open design does not retain body heat well without supplemental warmth.
warnings: A lean-to provides wind and rain shelter but does not trap body heat — without a fire, it is inadequate for overnight survival in cold temperatures.
Do not position the shelter under dead or leaning trees that could fall in wind.
In expected heavy snowfall, space rafter branches very close together — snow load on widely spaced rafters can collapse the structure.
A lean-to facing the wrong direction (into wind) will funnel rain and cold air into the shelter instead of deflecting them.
whatNotToDo: Do not sleep directly on bare ground — even a few degrees of cold ground will sap body heat through conduction.
Do not build the lean-to larger than you need — extra interior volume is wasted space you cannot warm.
Do not rely on the lean-to alone for warmth in temperatures below 50°F without a fire or sleeping insulation.
Do not skip lashing the ridgepole — an unlashed pole can roll or fall under the weight of branches and debris.
redFlags: You are shivering inside the shelter — the lean-to is not retaining enough warmth; build a fire or find additional insulation immediately.
The shelter frame shifts or sags under wind or debris weight — reinforce before sleeping in it.
Rain is running under the bottom edge of the debris layer into the shelter — add more shingled layers at the base or reorient.
preparednessTips: Practice building a lean-to frame in good weather so you know the technique under stress.
Carry 20 feet of paracord in your go-bag — it makes ridgepole lashing much faster than relying on natural cordage.
Know how to recognize good ridgepole trees: straight, living (not rotted), with a first branch or fork at waist-to-shoulder height.

sourceReferences.title: U.S. Army Survival Manual FM 21-76

guides.title: Edible Plant Foraging in an Emergency

guideData.title: Edible Plant Foraging in an Emergency
guideData.summary: A structured walkthrough for identifying and safely consuming wild plants when conventional food is unavailable. Plant misidentification is a potentially fatal risk — this guide covers the identification process, three high-confidence starter edibles, toxic lookalike warnings, and what to do if poisoning is suspected.
guideData.whenToUse: You are in a prolonged survival situation with no conventional food access
guideData.preferredOption: Positively identify a plant using a regional field guide and at least three independent identification points before consuming any part of it.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no field guide is available and you cannot make a positive ID, use the U.S. Army Universal Edibility Test as a last resort — but never use it on mushrooms.
steps: Step 1 — Assess the situation: Forage only when the caloric benefit outweighs the risk. In a short-term (24–48 hour) survival situation, water is more critical than food. Prioritize hydration first.

Step 2 — Choose your location carefully: Avoid foraging near roads, industrial areas, golf courses, city parks, farmland edges, or anywhere that may have been sprayed with pesticides or herbicides. Move off-trail into undisturbed areas.
Step 3 — Only harvest healthy plants: Skip any plants that are diseased, discolored, wilted, infested with insects, or growing in contaminated or stagnant water.

Step 4 — Apply the three-point identification rule: Before consuming any plant, confirm its identity using at least three independent characteristics — leaf shape, stem structure, flower color, root type, fragrance, habitat, and season. Do not rely on a single feature or a common name alone.

Step 5 — Check for danger signs and do NOT eat plants with: milky or discolored sap (use as a warning, not absolute rule — verify further), fine hairs or spines, umbrella-shaped flower clusters (the carrot/parsley family includes deadly hemlock), an almond or pear scent in woody parts or leaves, green/white/yellow berries, or a three-leaved growth pattern.

Step 6 — Start with the highest-confidence beginner edibles: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) — bright yellow blooms, toothed leaves from base, entire plant is edible raw or cooked. Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) — oval leaves with parallel veins, central flower spike, edible raw or boiled. Cattail (Typha latifolia) — wetland plant with distinctive cigar-shaped brown tip, young shoots and rhizomes edible raw or cooked.

Step 7 — Test one plant at a time: Never introduce multiple new plants simultaneously. Eat a small amount of a single confirmed edible and wait at least 30–60 minutes before eating more to check for allergic or adverse reactions.

Step 8 — If you cannot positively identify a plant and must eat it (true survival emergency only), use the Universal Edibility Test: (a) separate the plant into components — leaves, stems, roots, seeds; (b) smell each part for acidic or almond-like odors; (c) fast for 8 hours; (d) press a piece against your inner elbow for 15 minutes — stop if there is burning, rash, or itching; (e) touch a small pinch to your outer lip for 3 minutes — stop if burning; (f) hold a small amount on your tongue for 15 minutes without chewing — stop if burning; (g) chew a small portion and hold for 15 minutes without swallowing — spit out if any irritation; (h) swallow a small amount and wait 8 hours; (i) if no reaction, eat a quarter-cup portion and wait another 8 hours. Test only one plant part at a time. This process takes approximately 24 hours per part.

Step 9 — Cook when possible: Cooking reduces bitterness in many edible plants, kills surface bacteria and parasites, and can neutralize some mild irritants. Do not eat raw mushrooms under any circumstances.

Step 10 — If you experience burning throat, uncontrollable salivation, nausea, vomiting, rapid heartbeat, difficulty breathing, confusion, or seizures after eating a wild plant: call Poison Control at 1-800-222-1222 or call 911 immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless specifically directed by Poison Control. Bring the plant or a photo to the emergency room.
warnings: Misidentification of toxic plants can be fatal. The carrot/parsley family (Apiaceae) includes deadly poison hemlock and water hemlock that closely resemble edible wild carrot and parsnip — many experienced foragers refuse to harvest from this family entirely.
Foraging near roads, industrial sites, farmland edges, or treated lawns risks consuming plants contaminated with pesticides, herbicides, or heavy metals that are invisible and odorless.
Some plants are partially edible: elderberries are edible when cooked but toxic raw; rhubarb fruit is edible but leaves are toxic; the distinction is not obvious in the field.
Toxic plant symptoms can onset very quickly — poison hemlock can cause respiratory failure within 15 minutes of ingestion. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen before calling for help.

The Universal Edibility Test is not a guaranteed safety check. It does not detect slow-onset or cumulative toxins, and it does not protect against plants that cause organ damage without immediate symptoms.
Do NOT use the Universal Edibility Test on mushrooms. Many deadly mushrooms (e.g., Amanita phalloides — death cap) cause no immediate discomfort and destroy the liver over 24–72 hours.
Children are at higher risk — toxic plant doses that cause mild symptoms in adults can be lethal in children. Children should never forage independently.
Even plants considered safe edibles can cause allergic reactions in some individuals. Individual sensitivity varies.
whatNotToDo: Do not eat any plant based solely on a common name match — common names overlap between edible and poisonous species.

Do not consume any plant from the carrot/parsley/celery family (Apiaceae — umbrella-shaped flower clusters) unless you have extensive specific training in that family, as the deadly lookalikes are extremely difficult to distinguish.
Do not eat green, white, or yellow berries from unknown plants.
Do not apply the Universal Edibility Test to mushrooms — it does not work for fungi.
Do not induce vomiting after suspected toxic plant ingestion unless specifically directed by Poison Control or a physician.
Do not eat raw mushrooms, including wild varieties you believe you recognize — always cook them thoroughly.
Do not assume that because an animal or bird is eating a plant it is safe for humans — many toxic plants are tolerated by wildlife but harmful to people.
Do not forage multiple unknown plants simultaneously — test one at a time so you can identify the source if a reaction occurs.
Do not trust plant identification apps as your only identification source — they have meaningful error rates with toxic lookalikes.
Do not harvest plants from water that appears stagnant, discolored, or near industrial runoff.
redFlags: Burning or tingling sensation in mouth, throat, or lips immediately after tasting or swallowing any plant part — spit it out and call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222)
Nausea or vomiting within minutes to hours after eating a wild plant — call Poison Control immediately
Rapid or irregular heartbeat after plant ingestion — call 911
Difficulty breathing, tightness in throat, or shortness of breath after plant ingestion — call 911
Seizures or muscle spasms after plant ingestion — call 911 immediately
Confusion, disorientation, altered mental status, or sudden drowsiness after plant ingestion — call 911
Collapse or loss of consciousness — call 911 immediately
Excessive salivation or drooling after eating a plant — call Poison Control
Skin rash, hives, or swelling of face/lips/throat after plant contact or ingestion — treat as potential anaphylaxis, call 911
Any suspicious plant that has been eaten by a child — call Poison Control at 1-800-222-1222 immediately even with no symptoms yet
preparednessTips: Before any wilderness trip, obtain a region-specific field guide to edible and toxic plants — regional accuracy matters because species and lookalikes vary by geography.
Save Poison Control's number in your phone before heading outdoors: 1-800-222-1222 (US, 24/7, free).
Practice identifying three beginner edibles (dandelion, cattail, plantain) in your local area before relying on this knowledge in an emergency.
Take a local foraging walk led by a certified instructor or experienced naturalist — hands-on learning with expert verification is far more reliable than book learning alone.
Reference the USDA Plants Database (plants.usda.gov) for regional identification and range maps for plants in your area.
Consider the BSA Pocket Tutor Guide to Edible Wild Plants or Sam Thayer's Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants of Eastern and Central North America as your carry reference.
Learn to identify at least 5–10 toxic plants in your region (especially hemlock, water hemlock, nightshade, pokeweed, and foxglove) so you can exclude them with confidence.
Include a compact regional plant identification laminate or booklet in your emergency kit.
Understand seasonal availability: spring and early summer favor leafy greens; late summer and fall favor fruits, nuts, and seeds.

guides.title: Primitive Fishing in a Survival Situation

guideData.title: Primitive Fishing in a Survival Situation
guideData.summary: When stranded without fishing gear for multiple days, improvised hooks, passive fish traps, and fish weirs can secure critical protein and calories. Passive methods (weirs, traps) are preferred because they work unsupervised while you handle other survival priorities.
guideData.whenToUse: You are in a multi-day survival situation and need food
guideData.preferredOption: Build a passive fish weir (V-shaped rock or stick funnel in a stream) so fish are captured automatically without ongoing effort
guideData.fallbackOption: Improvise a hook-and-line rig using a bent safety pin, carved wood, or thorn with plant-fiber or paracord line; fish actively at dawn and dusk
steps: ASSESS YOUR WATER: Scan for a stream, river, or shallow shoreline. Look for signs of fish: ripples on the surface, jumping fish, diving birds, or baitfish activity near shore. Check undercut banks, submerged logs, and where a shallow riffle meets a deeper pool — fish congregate in these spots.

BUILD A FISH WEIR (passive method — do this first): Choose a narrow point in a shallow stream. Using rocks or sturdy sticks driven into the streambed, build two walls forming a V-shape with the open end facing upstream (into the current). The narrow downstream end should funnel into a small enclosed pocket. Make the gap just large enough for one fish to enter at a time. Check every few hours. This trap works while you sleep.

IMPROVISE A HOOK if active fishing is needed: Bend a safety pin into a hook shape, or whittle a gorge hook — a 1-to-2-inch straight piece of hardwood or bone sharpened at both ends with a notch in the middle for the line. Tie the line through the notch. A gorge hook turns sideways inside the fish's mouth when swallowed, securing it without a barb.

SOURCE LINE: Use paracord inner strands, shoelaces, strips of cut clothing, or twisted plant-fiber cordage from inner bark (basswood, stinging nettle). A few feet is enough for fixed-line fishing from shore.

FIND BAIT: Flip rocks and logs near the water's edge to find earthworms and grubs. Grasshoppers, beetles, and other insects work well. Small crayfish are excellent bait. Bright fragments of cloth or foil can attract fish as improvised lures. Plan on needing several attempts per fish with improvised gear.

FISH AT DAWN AND DUSK: Fish move into shallow water to feed at first light (approximately 5–9 AM) and again at dusk (approximately 6–9 PM). Midday bright sun drives fish deep — do not waste energy fishing during peak sun hours. Cloudy, overcast conditions and light rain also trigger feeding; use these windows.

PLACE YOUR LINE: Lower your baited hook near structure — along a submerged log, under an overcut bank, at the edge of shade. Keep the line taut enough to feel a strike. Because improvised hooks have no barb, flip the fish immediately onto the bank the moment you feel resistance — do not play it on the line.

FIELD-DRESS IMMEDIATELY: Kill the fish with a quick strike to the back of the head. Insert a knife or sharpened stick at the vent and slit toward the head. Remove innards completely. Dispose of all innards at least 200 feet from your camp and away from the water source to avoid attracting predators and contaminating the water.

COOK THOROUGHLY: Cook all wild-caught fish to an internal temperature of 145°F (63°C). The flesh should be opaque and flake easily. Over a fire, rotate the fish until no pink flesh remains at the thickest part. Never eat raw wild-caught fish — doing so can introduce parasites and waterborne pathogens that will worsen your situation.

PRESERVE SURPLUS: If you catch more fish than you can eat immediately, smoke or sun-dry the excess. Smoking: hang cleaned fish over a low fire with green leafy branches producing smoke for several hours. Sun-drying: slice fish thin, hang on branches or rocks in direct sun and wind. Consume preserved fish within 1–2 days in warm weather.
warnings: Passive weirs and traps are illegal for recreational use in most U.S. jurisdictions — these techniques are for genuine survival emergencies only
Waterborne parasites such as Giardia and Cryptosporidium are present in most natural water sources; always cook fish thoroughly and treat any water used for drinking
Raw or undercooked wild fish can carry parasites; foodborne illness in a survival scenario can rapidly become life-threatening
Fishing requires significant caloric expenditure — active fishing that fails to yield a catch costs more calories than it produces; prioritize passive methods
Freshwater fish in some regions can carry heavy metal contamination; in urban or industrial areas near waterways, catch and cook multiple small fish rather than relying on one large specimen
Light refraction makes fish appear closer to the surface than they are when spearing — aim below the apparent position
Fish innards disposed of near camp or water can attract bears, coyotes, and other wildlife; carry waste well away from camp
Smoked or dried fish spoils faster in humid, warm conditions; when in doubt, discard fish that smells strongly sour or has visible mold
whatNotToDo: Do not eat raw wild-caught fish — parasites and bacteria are destroyed only by thorough cooking
Do not waste energy fishing during bright midday hours when fish are deepest and least active
Do not attempt noodling (hand-fishing in underwater holes) without experience — snapping turtles and other animals share those holes and can cause serious injury
Do not ignore your weir for more than a few hours — fish that enter may escape or die and spoil
Do not dispose of fish scraps near your camp or water source — attract unwanted wildlife and contaminate the water
Do not attempt to eat fish that has a pungent ammonia smell, unusual coloring, or slimy texture — discard it
Do not set your weir where it could be swept away in fast current — check the walls are stable before leaving them unattended
Do not rely solely on fishing as your food strategy in the first 24 hours — shelter, fire, and signaling take priority over food procurement in most survival scenarios
redFlags: You develop severe stomach cramps, diarrhea, or vomiting after eating — stop eating and prioritize hydration; you may have consumed undercooked or spoiled fish
You have been unable to catch any fish after two full days of effort — consider shifting to plant foraging, trapping small mammals, or an alternate food procurement strategy

The water body you are fishing shows signs of algal bloom (green or blue-green scum, strong musty smell) — avoid eating fish from this water and do not drink it; cyanobacterial toxins can cause serious illness even in cooked fish
You are showing symptoms of dehydration alongside hunger — water is more urgent than food; address dehydration first before expending energy on fishing
Fish in the area have unusual lesions, growths, or abnormal coloring — these may indicate contaminated water; do not eat them
preparednessTips: Pack a small survival fishing kit: 50 feet of 20-lb monofilament, 10 small hooks (size 8–12), 5 split-shot sinkers, a small bobber, and 5 swivels — fits in a film canister or Altoids tin
Learn to identify your local fish-bearing water bodies on a map before an emergency
Practice making improvised cordage from plant fibers so you can produce fishing line from natural materials
Know your local game and fish regulations — in some states, fish traps and weirs are permitted for survival use
Store paracord in your emergency kit; its inner strands make effective improvised fishing line
A multi-tool with a knife blade dramatically speeds up hook carving, fish cleaning, and trap construction

guides.title: Signal for Rescue: Be Seen and Be Heard

guideData.title: Signal for Rescue: Be Seen and Be Heard
guideData.summary: When lost or stranded on land, systematic signaling — using the Rule of Three, a whistle, mirror, and ground symbols — dramatically improves your chances of being found. Staying put and signaling from a visible location is almost always more effective than trying to self-rescue.
guideData.whenToUse: You are lost or stranded on land and need to signal for rescue
guideData.preferredOption: Move to an open, visible location (clearing, ridgetop, riverbank) and signal continuously using the Rule of Three — three whistle blasts, repeated every 1–3 minutes.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no whistle is available, use any reflective surface as a signal mirror, create ground-to-air symbols, or build a signal fire when you see or hear aircraft.
steps: Stop moving and stay calm — decide to stay put unless an immediate hazard forces you to move. Rescuers search from your last known location and planned route.
Move to the most open, visible location you can safely reach: a clearing, ridgetop, riverbank, open rock face, or beach. Avoid dense trees or valleys that block line-of-sight.
Signal using the Rule of Three — the universal distress signal: three whistle blasts, three mirror flashes, or three fires in a triangle. Pause, then repeat.
Use a whistle to signal: three short sharp blasts, pause 30–60 seconds, then repeat. A 120 dB whistle can be heard up to 1 mile in forest. Continue this pattern until rescuers respond.

On a sunny day, use a signal mirror or any reflective surface (space blanket, belt buckle, polished metal, smartphone screen): aim the reflected sunbeam toward aircraft by sighting the target between two outstretched V-shaped fingers and flashing in bursts of three.

Create a ground-to-air signal in any open area: stamp or build an 'X' or 'SOS' using rocks, logs, or dug trenches. Make each line at least 10 feet wide and aim for a 5:1 length-to-width ratio to be visible from aircraft.

If you hear or see an aircraft or ground searchers, light a signal fire and add green vegetation (pine boughs, fresh leaves) to create dense smoke visible from the air. Only do this when rescuers are potentially watching — an uncontrolled fire creates danger.
Balance signaling with your other survival needs — maintain shelter, conserve water, and protect yourself from exposure. You need to be alive and in place when rescuers arrive.
warnings: Moving away from your last known route drastically enlarges the search area and can delay rescue by hours or days — stay put once you begin signaling
Signal fires must be carefully controlled — never start a fire in dry brush, high wind, or conditions where it can spread; extinguish it if searchers do not appear
Shouting exhausts your voice quickly — a whistle carries 10 times farther and lasts indefinitely
whatNotToDo: Do not abandon your signaling location to search for help — this works against rescue
Do not rely solely on a phone for signaling — cell signal may be absent and battery may be low
Do not assume rescuers can hear or see you just because you can see them — active signaling is required
redFlags: Signals have gone unanswered for an extended period while weather is worsening or injuries are deteriorating — if you have a PLB or satellite messenger, activate it now
Medical emergency or rapidly worsening condition requires urgent intervention — activate emergency electronic beacon immediately if available
preparednessTips: Always carry a high-decibel emergency whistle (120 dB) when hiking or in the backcountry — they weigh less than an ounce
Pack a small signal mirror or keep a reflective emergency space blanket accessible at the top of your pack
Tell someone your planned route and expected return time before entering the backcountry — this is the single biggest factor in how quickly SAR can locate you

Consider a Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) for remote trips — register it with NOAA at beaconregistration.noaa.gov; it works without cell signal and transmits your GPS coordinates directly to rescue coordination

guides.title: Stay Put vs Move When Lost on Land

guideData.title: Stay Put vs Move When Lost on Land
guideData.summary: If you are truly lost on land but not in immediate danger, staying near your last known location is almost always safer and makes you far easier to find. Move only to escape an active hazard or reach clearly safer ground nearby.
guideData.whenToUse: You realize you are lost and cannot find your way back
guideData.preferredOption: Stay near your last known location, improve your shelter and visibility, and begin signaling for rescue.
guideData.fallbackOption: If an immediate hazard forces movement, move conservatively to the nearest safer ground — slightly higher, to a clearing, or to a trail edge — then stop and begin signaling.
steps: Stop moving immediately. Take a breath and assess your situation: injuries, weather, available water, remaining daylight, and any communication options.
Identify any immediate dangers at your current location — rising water, unstable slopes, active fire, lightning exposure, or falling hazards.
If no immediate danger exists, stay near your last known location. Rescuers will begin searching along your planned route and from the last known point.
Improve your shelter and visibility from where you are: find or build wind and rain protection, and make yourself as visible as possible from the air and ground.

If you must move to escape a hazard, choose the most conservative option — slightly higher ground above flood level, a nearby clearing, or a forest edge. Never attempt steep terrain, fast water crossings, or routes through dense vegetation.
Move slowly and deliberately, avoiding steep drop-offs, fast water crossings, avalanche paths, and dense terrain that obscures your location.
Once in a safer spot, stop again. Mark your location if possible and begin signaling for rescue using any available means — whistle, mirror, bright clothing, or fire smoke.
warnings: Wandering far from your last known route can drastically enlarge the search area and delay rescue by hours or days
Self-rescue over difficult terrain without skills or equipment is a leading cause of wilderness injuries and fatalities
whatNotToDo: Do not move just because staying feels passive — movement that enlarges the search area is worse than waiting
Do not attempt to navigate by memory alone in unfamiliar terrain; disorientation increases with fatigue and stress
Do not send the group in multiple directions to search — stay together
redFlags: Severe weather is imminent and your current location provides no shelter options
Active fire, unstable ground, or rising water threatens your current location
A severely injured person in your group cannot survive without rapid professional medical care — you must weigh the risk of moving vs. calling for help
preparednessTips: Always tell someone your planned route and expected return time before going into the backcountry — this is the single most important factor in fast SAR response
Carry a whistle and a signal mirror or space blanket — they weigh almost nothing and can be seen or heard from a mile away
Download offline maps for your area before departing so you have navigation if cell service is lost

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NPS guidance on staying near last known location and conserving energy when lost in a national park or wilderness area

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear guidance to stay put once genuinely lost, with limited circumstances where movement is warranted

guides.title: Food Safety After Power Loss: What to Keep and What to Toss

guideData.title: Food Safety After Power Loss: What to Keep and What to Toss
guideData.summary: When power goes out, two rules govern food safety: keep the doors closed as long as possible to preserve cold, and never taste food to judge whether it's safe. This card gives you the exact time windows and decision rules for refrigerator, freezer, and cooler scenarios.
guideData.whenToUse: Power has just gone out and you need to know what to do immediately
guideData.preferredOption: Keep both the refrigerator and freezer doors closed and track the time since power was lost to determine when to act.
guideData.fallbackOption: If outage will exceed 4 hours, transfer refrigerated perishables to a cooler packed with ice or frozen gel packs and keep it at 40°F or below.
steps: Keep the refrigerator door closed — every opening costs 20–30 minutes of safe storage time. Food stays safe for up to 4 hours with the door closed.
Keep the freezer door closed — a full freezer stays safe for 48 hours; a half-full freezer stays safe for 24 hours.
If the outage is expected to last more than 4 hours, transfer refrigerated perishables (meat, poultry, fish, eggs, milk, and leftovers) to a cooler packed with ice. Keep the cooler at 40°F or below.

When power is restored (or after 4+ hours without power), check refrigerated food: discard any raw or cooked meat, poultry, fish, seafood, eggs, and leftovers that have been above 40°F for more than 2 hours.

Check freezer food when power returns: if items still contain ice crystals or register 40°F or below, they may be safely refrozen (quality may suffer). If fully thawed and above 40°F, discard meat, poultry, seafood, and egg-based items.
Never taste food to test whether it is safe — bacteria that cause food poisoning have no odor, color, or taste. A food can look, smell, and taste normal and still cause serious illness.
When in doubt, throw it out — the cost of replacing food is far less than the cost of foodborne illness.
warnings: Food poisoning bacteria are odorless, colorless, and tasteless — safe-looking food can be dangerous after temperature abuse
Opened condiments (mayonnaise, tartar sauce, horseradish) must be discarded if held above 50°F for more than 8 hours
Bread, salad greens, or ready-to-eat foods contaminated by dripped raw meat juices must be discarded regardless of time or temperature history
whatNotToDo: Do not taste food to determine if it is safe — never rely on smell, color, or taste
Do not refreeze meat, poultry, or seafood that has fully thawed above 40°F
Do not open the refrigerator or freezer unnecessarily — every opening shortens the safe window
redFlags: Power has been out more than 4 hours and you have no cooler with ice for perishables
Freezer contents have fully thawed and meat or fish has reached room temperature
You are unsure how long the power was out — treat all perishables as potentially unsafe
preparednessTips: Keep an appliance thermometer in your refrigerator (target: 35–38°F) and freezer (target: 0°F) — it lets you verify safe temperatures after an outage without guessing
Freeze containers of water and keep them in the freezer to increase thermal mass — they help maintain safe temperatures longer during an outage and can supply drinking water if needed
Stock shelf-stable foods (canned goods, dried beans, peanut butter, crackers) so you can eat without opening the refrigerator during a short outage

guides.title: Wildfire Preparedness: Go Bag, Evacuation Plan, and Defensible Space

guideData.title: Wildfire Preparedness: Go Bag, Evacuation Plan, and Defensible Space
guideData.summary: Surviving a wildfire requires preparation done well before fire season: a ready go-bag, clear evacuation routes, and maintained defensible space around your home are the three pillars of wildfire preparedness. The single most important action is leaving early.
guideData.whenToUse: You live in a wildland-urban interface (WUI) or fire-prone region
guideData.preferredOption: Leave early when a wildfire warning order is issued for your zone — do not wait for a mandatory evacuation order.
guideData.fallbackOption: If evacuation routes are cut off by fire, go inside the most fire-resistant building available, close all windows and vents, call 911, and wait for rescue.
warnings: Wildfires can move faster than a person can run — last-minute evacuation is far more dangerous than early departure
Smoke inhalation from wildfire can be life-threatening long before flames reach your area — an N95 mask is essential
Evacuating at night in heavy smoke with low visibility dramatically increases accident risk — know your routes by daylight
Gas stations and roads may be gridlocked or closed during mass evacuation — a half-tank of fuel can be the difference
whatNotToDo: Do not wait for a mandatory evacuation order if you feel unsafe — early voluntary departure dramatically improves survival odds
Do not stop to gather non-essential belongings once an evacuation order is issued — possessions can be replaced
Do not shelter inside a structure once fire reaches it — a burning building is more dangerous than the open; this is a last resort only
Do not return to an evacuated area until local authorities give the all-clear — fire damage weakens structures and air quality may remain hazardous
redFlags: A mandatory evacuation ORDER has been issued for your zone — leave immediately without delay
You can see, smell, or hear an active fire or heavy smoke approaching your area
Your primary evacuation route has been blocked by fire or emergency vehicles
Embers or spot fires are appearing near your home from wind-driven sparks
preparednessTips: Find your evacuation zone before fire season. Many counties use a letter (A-B-C) or number system. Go to your county's Office of Emergency Services website to find your zone and learn what each alert level means.

Follow the Ready-Set-Go model: READY (year-round preparation), SET (when fire is in your general area, be ready to leave at a moment's notice), GO (leave immediately when a warning or order is issued for your zone).

Build and maintain a go-bag with at least 72 hours of supplies: water, non-perishable food, N95 or P100 respirator mask for wildfire smoke, essential medications (7-day supply), phone charger and portable battery, cash, copies of critical documents (ID, insurance), a change of clothing, and a battery-powered radio.
Keep at least half a tank of gas in your vehicle at all times during fire season — gas stations may be closed or gridlocked during an evacuation.

Create defensible space around your home: Zone 1 (0–30 feet from structures) — remove dead vegetation, dried leaves, woodpiles, and flammable debris; Zone 2 (30–100 feet) — space plants at least three times their diameter apart so fire cannot jump from plant to plant.
Identify and drive at least two different evacuation routes out of your neighborhood — practice them so you know them under stress and poor visibility conditions.

Sign up for your county's emergency alert system. Most counties use a local system (Nixle, Everbridge, or county-specific app) plus Wireless Emergency Alerts sent automatically to your phone. Confirm WEA alerts are enabled in your phone's notification settings.
Pre-select a family meeting place and designate an out-of-area contact so family members can find each other if separated during evacuation.
Keep cellphones charged every night during fire season — a dead phone during evacuation is a serious hazard.

Consider home-hardening improvements: ember-resistant vents, metal roof, and non-combustible eaves and soffits reduce ignition risk from wind-driven embers, which start most home fires during wildfires.

guides.title: Flood Preparedness: Know Your Risk and Plan Your Escape

guideData.title: Flood Preparedness: Know Your Risk and Plan Your Escape
guideData.summary: Flooding is the most common and deadliest natural disaster in the U.S. Preparation done before a flood — knowing your zone, planning your escape routes, and building an emergency kit — can be the difference between safety and tragedy.
guideData.whenToUse: You live near a river, creek, coastline, or low-lying area prone to flooding
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate to pre-identified higher ground before floodwaters rise and cut off your escape routes.
guideData.fallbackOption: If evacuation routes are already cut off, move to the highest floor of your building, call 911, and wait for rescue — do not enter floodwater.
warnings: Flash floods can occur with little to no warning — do not wait for an official order if water is actively rising near you
Floodwater is often contaminated with sewage, chemicals, agricultural runoff, and concealed debris
Downed power lines in standing water create a lethal electrocution hazard — never approach them
Evacuating after routes are flooded is extremely dangerous; early departure is always safer
whatNotToDo: Never drive through flooded roadways — Turn Around, Don't Drown; 12 inches of water can sweep away a vehicle
Never walk through moving floodwater — 6 inches of fast-moving water can knock an adult off their feet
Do not assume standard homeowner's insurance covers flood damage — it does not; NFIP flood policies take 30 days to go into effect
Do not return home after a flood until local authorities confirm it is safe — hidden structural damage, gas leaks, and contamination are common
redFlags: A flash flood WARNING has been issued for your area — move to high ground immediately
You see water rising visibly toward doorways, vents, or your first floor
Evacuation order issued by local authorities — do not delay
Hearing rushing water or seeing debris-filled water moving rapidly nearby
preparednessTips: Find your flood zone using FEMA's Flood Map Service Center (msc.fema.gov) — search your address to know whether you are in a Special Flood Hazard Area. Remember: flooding doesn't follow lines on a map.

Know the three alert levels: flood WATCH = flooding is possible, stay alert; flood WARNING = flooding is occurring or imminent, take action; flash flood WARNING = life-threatening rapid flooding, move to high ground immediately.

Identify and practice at least two evacuation routes to higher ground — drive both routes so you know them well before an emergency. Designate a destination: a relative's home, a hotel on high ground, or an official emergency shelter.
Sign up for your county's emergency alert system (most are free) and confirm that Wireless Emergency Alerts are enabled on your smartphone.
Build a 3-day emergency kit: 1 gallon of water per person per day, non-perishable food, battery-powered radio, flashlight and extra batteries, first aid kit, medications, and cash.
Store copies of critical documents — ID, insurance policies, medical records, property deeds — in a waterproof, portable container. Create secure digital backups in cloud storage.

Protect your home in flood-prone areas: install check valves in plumbing pipes to prevent sewage backflow, consider a sump pump with battery backup, and move valuables to upper floors before flood season.
In a flood-prone neighborhood, sandbags and flood barriers can slow water entry — obtain them from your local emergency management office in advance, not during a flood warning.

Note that standard homeowners and renters insurance does NOT cover flood damage. Flood insurance through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) must be purchased at least 30 days before a flood to be active.
Create a family plan: designate a meeting place on high ground and identify an out-of-area contact in case local communication is disrupted.

guides.title: Tornado Preparedness: Safe Place, Kit, and Family Plan

guideData.title: Tornado Preparedness: Safe Place, Kit, and Family Plan
guideData.summary: Surviving a tornado depends almost entirely on preparation done before the storm. Identify your safe shelter now, build an emergency kit, and make sure every household member knows exactly where to go when a tornado warning is issued.
guideData.whenToUse: You live in a tornado-prone region and want to prepare before severe weather season
guideData.preferredOption: Go to your basement or designated interior room on the lowest floor immediately when a tornado warning is issued.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no basement is available, go to the lowest floor interior room with no windows, cover your head and neck with your arms, and shelter under a heavy table or mattress if available.
warnings: Tornadoes can form faster than warning systems can respond — do not treat a tornado watch as all-clear
Mobile homes and manufactured homes offer no meaningful protection in a tornado, even if anchored
Seeking shelter in a car or under a highway overpass is extremely dangerous and offers no protection
Upper-floor interior rooms are not safe — always go to the lowest floor
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter in a car during a tornado — abandon it and find a sturdy building
Do not shelter under a highway overpass — it creates dangerous wind tunnels and offers no protection
Do not go outside to watch the storm approach
Do not assume a tornado warning is for someone else's area — take every warning seriously
redFlags: A tornado WARNING (not just watch) has been issued for your area — seek shelter immediately
You see a visible funnel cloud, debris cloud, or rotating wall cloud overhead
You hear a loud continuous roar like a freight train with no visible storm
Dark greenish sky with large hail — signs of a supercell capable of producing tornadoes
preparednessTips: Identify your safe shelter now: the best option is a basement or storm cellar; second-best is an interior room on the lowest floor with no windows (bathroom, closet, or hallway). For multi-story buildings, go to a central hallway on the lowest floor you can reach in time.

If you live in a mobile home or manufactured home, identify the nearest sturdy building now — no mobile home is safe in a tornado, even if tied down. Plan to leave for that building when a tornado WATCH is issued.
Know the difference: tornado WATCH means conditions are favorable for tornadoes — stay alert; tornado WARNING means a tornado has been confirmed by radar or sighting — seek shelter immediately.
Install a NOAA Weather Radio All Hazards with battery backup in your home — it will alert you even at night when you cannot check your phone.
Enable Wireless Emergency Alerts on your smartphone and check that they are turned on in your phone's settings.

Build a 3-day emergency kit: 1 gallon of water per person per day, non-perishable food, battery-powered or hand-crank radio, flashlight and extra batteries, first aid kit, at least a 7-day supply of essential medications, and copies of important documents.
Create a family emergency plan: designate two meeting places (one near home, one outside your neighborhood), identify an out-of-area contact, and make sure all household members know the plan.
Practice your tornado drill at least once a year — time how fast each family member can reach the safe place from every room in the house.
Before severe weather season, secure or bring indoors any outdoor furniture, decorations, tools, or garden items that could become windborne projectiles.
After a tornado, wait for the official all-clear before leaving your shelter; watch for downed power lines, structural damage, and gas leaks when exiting.

guides.title: CPR: How It Works and When to Use It

guideData.title: CPR: How It Works and When to Use It
guideData.summary: CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) manually circulates blood to the brain and vital organs when the heart has stopped. Bystander CPR can double or triple survival odds — but fewer than half of all cardiac arrests in the US receive it. Understanding how CPR works, how it differs for adults, children, and infants, and when to use an AED helps you act confidently when seconds count.
guideData.whenToUse: You want to understand how CPR works before taking a formal training class
guideData.preferredOption: For step-by-step instructions during an active emergency, use the Cardiac Arrest: Start CPR Immediately action card. This guide is for understanding and preparedness.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are unsure of technique during an emergency, compression-only CPR (no rescue breaths) is always better than no CPR — push hard and fast in the center of the chest and call 911.
warnings: CPR can cause rib fractures in approximately 1 in 3 cases, especially in older adults. This is an expected complication and not a reason to stop — broken ribs can heal, brain death from no compressions cannot.
Rescue breathing carries a very low risk of disease transmission. The risk of not acting is far greater. If you are concerned, compression-only CPR is a highly effective alternative for adults.
AED shocks are safe for the rescuer as long as no one is touching the person when the shock is delivered. The AED will tell you to clear before analyzing or shocking.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait to see if the person recovers on their own — every minute without CPR reduces survival odds by 7–10%.
Do not assume CPR is only for people with heart conditions — cardiac arrest can happen to anyone at any age.
Do not avoid starting CPR because you are afraid of doing it wrong — imperfect CPR is always better than no CPR.
preparednessTips: Take a CPR and AED course from the American Heart Association (cpr.heart.org) or American Red Cross (redcross.org) — hands-on training takes about 2 hours and builds the muscle memory to act under stress.
Compression rate memory trick: the Bee Gees song 'Stayin' Alive' is exactly 100 BPM. Use it as a mental metronome.

Know where the nearest AED is in your workplace, gym, school, and community spaces. Most are wall-mounted in a red or green cabinet. AEDs in public locations have saved thousands of lives — but only 12.6% of bystanders use them.
Children aged 12 and older can be taught effective CPR and AED use per 2025 AHA guidelines. Consider teaching CPR to older children at home.
If you have a family member with a known heart condition, ask their cardiologist whether a home AED is appropriate for your household.

sourceReferences.title: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary authoritative source for adult bystander CPR: compression rate, depth, hands-only vs 30:2, AED use, and updated 2025 recommendations.

guides.title: Sprains and Suspected Fractures

guideData.title: Sprains and Suspected Fractures
guideData.summary: Most limb injuries after a fall, twist, or impact look the same — you cannot reliably tell a sprain from a fracture without an X-ray. This guide walks through how to assess the injury, when to call emergency services, how to immobilize and cool the limb, and when it is safe to wait for an urgent care appointment versus going to an emergency room.
guideData.whenToUse: After a fall, twist, or impact with pain, swelling, bruising, or difficulty bearing weight on a limb
guideData.preferredOption: Stop the person from moving the injured area. Check circulation below the injury (warmth, color, sensation in fingers/toes). Splint in position found, apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for up to 20 minutes, and elevate. Arrange medical evaluation — even an apparent sprain warrants an X-ray if the person cannot bear weight.
guideData.fallbackOption: If splinting materials are unavailable, support the injured limb with a pillow or folded blanket and keep the person still. For an injured leg, bind it loosely to the uninjured leg with cloth strips. Do not attempt to walk the person out on an injured lower limb without immobilization.
steps: Ask what happened and where it hurts. Look for deformity, swelling, bruising, or bone visible through skin. Do not ask the person to move or test the limb.
Call 911 immediately for: bone through skin, severe deformity, numbness or coldness below the injury, suspected spine or hip injury, signs of shock, or a child with significant limb pain.
Check circulation: feel for warmth below the injury, look at skin color (pink vs. pale or blue), and ask if they can feel your touch on their fingers or toes. Record what you find.

Immobilize the limb in the exact position found. Splint with any rigid material (rolled magazine, board, trekking pole) padded with soft cloth, extending one joint above and one joint below the injury.
Re-check circulation after splinting. If the limb is colder, paler, or more numb, loosen the splint ties immediately.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for up to 20 minutes. Never apply ice directly to skin and never leave ice on for more than 20 consecutive minutes.
Elevate the limb above heart level if it does not cause additional pain — this slows swelling and reduces internal bleeding around the injury.
Monitor for shock (pale, cold, clammy, faint, rapid shallow breathing), especially with hip, pelvis, or upper leg injuries where internal blood loss can be large.
Arrange medical evaluation: urgent care or an emergency room depending on severity. Any suspected fracture or inability to bear weight needs imaging to rule out a break.
warnings: You cannot tell a sprain from a fracture without an X-ray. Always treat as a fracture when in doubt — over-treatment is far safer than missing a break.
Large-bone fractures (femur, hip, pelvis) can cause severe internal bleeding even with no visible wound. Signs of shock after a leg or hip injury are a medical emergency.
Never move a person with a suspected spine, neck, or head injury — immobilize them where they are and call 911.
Applying ice directly to skin or for longer than 20 minutes can cause frostbite. Always use a cloth barrier.
A splint that is too tight can cut off blood flow to the limb below the injury. Check color, warmth, and sensation before and after splinting and every 10–15 minutes.
whatNotToDo: Do not ask the person to move, flex, or bear weight to 'see how bad it is' — this can turn a crack into a full break.
Do not try to straighten, reduce, or push back any bone that appears out of place.
Do not push a bone visible through the skin back in — cover it loosely with a clean cloth and call 911.
Do not apply tight compression directly over a suspected fracture or open wound.
Do not give the person food or drink if surgery may be needed.
redFlags: Bone visible through the skin — open fracture, call 911
Numbness, tingling, pale or blue fingers or toes below the injury — nerve or blood vessel involvement, call 911
Severe deformity or limb at an unnatural angle — call 911
Suspected hip, pelvis, or femur (upper thigh) fracture — major internal bleeding risk, call 911
Signs of shock: pale, cold, clammy, faint, rapid shallow breathing — call 911
Child with significant limb pain after trauma — fractures in children can be subtle; get imaging
preparednessTips: Keep a SAM splint, triangular bandage (sling), and elastic wrap in your first aid kit — these three items handle most limb injuries.
Know the Ottawa Ankle Rules: an ankle X-ray is needed if there is pain near the ankle bone and inability to bear weight for four steps. This helps decide urgent care vs. ER.
In the backcountry, a trekking pole, tent stake, or sturdy branch padded with clothing makes an effective improvised splint.

sourceReferences.title: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

sourceReferences.title: Fractures: Types, Symptoms, and Treatment

guides.title: Sprain or Suspected Fracture: Immobilize and Cool

guideData.title: Sprain or Suspected Fracture: Immobilize and Cool
guideData.summary: If you cannot tell whether an injury is a sprain or fracture, treat it as a fracture. Stop the person from moving the limb, apply an improvised splint in the position found, cool with a wrapped ice pack for 20 minutes, and elevate. Check circulation below the injury before and after splinting.
guideData.whenToUse: After a fall, twist, or impact with pain, swelling, or difficulty moving or bearing weight on a limb
guideData.preferredOption: Stop the person from moving or testing the injured limb. Splint it in the exact position you find it — do not try to straighten it. Immobilize the joint above and below the injury. Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth for up to 20 minutes, and gently elevate if it does not cause more pain.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no splinting materials are available, bind an injured leg to the uninjured leg with cloth strips, or use a folded blanket or pillow to support an injured ankle/foot. Keep the person still and call 911.
steps: Tell the person to stop moving the injured area. Do not ask them to move or test the limb to assess the injury.

Call 911 if you see: bone protruding through skin, gross deformity, numbness or coldness below the injury, suspected head/neck/spine injury, signs of shock (pale, cold, faint, rapid breathing), or a large-bone injury (hip, femur/thigh, pelvis).
Check circulation below the injury: feel for warmth, look at skin color, and ask if they have normal sensation in fingers or toes. Note what you find.

Build or find a splint: use a rigid object (rolled magazine, piece of wood, umbrella, folded cardboard) or a soft support (pillow, folded blanket for ankle/foot). Pad it with clothing or cloth to prevent pressure.

Position the splint to immobilize the joint one level above AND one level below the injury. For a forearm fracture, the splint runs from above the elbow to below the wrist. For a lower leg fracture, from above the knee to below the ankle.
Secure the splint with cloth strips, bandages, or tape. Tie firmly but not so tight that it cuts off circulation — you should be able to slip a finger under the tie.
Re-check circulation after splinting: warmth, color, and sensation below the injury. If the limb becomes colder, paler, or more numb after splinting, loosen the ties immediately.
Apply a cold pack wrapped in a cloth (never directly on skin) to the injured area for up to 20 minutes. Remove it after 20 minutes — do not leave ice on longer.
Gently elevate the injured limb above the level of the heart if this does not increase pain. This reduces swelling and internal bleeding.
Keep the person still, warm, and calm. Monitor for signs of shock and reassess circulation every 10–15 minutes until medical help arrives.
warnings: You cannot reliably tell a sprain from a fracture without an X-ray. When in doubt, treat it as a fracture.

Large-bone fractures (femur/thigh, hip, pelvis) can cause life-threatening internal bleeding — 1–2 liters of blood can collect around the thigh alone. These are emergencies even if no bone is visible.
Suspected spinal, neck, or head injury: do not move the person. Leave them in position found, tell them to stay still, and call 911 immediately.
Never apply ice directly to skin or for more than 20 consecutive minutes — this can cause frostbite and skin damage.
A splint that is too tight can cut off circulation to the limb. Check warmth, color, and sensation below the splint frequently.
whatNotToDo: Do not ask the person to move, wiggle, or bear weight on the injured area to 'test' it — this can worsen a fracture.
Do not try to straighten, realign, or reduce a deformed limb — splint it in the position you find it.
Do not push a bone protruding through the skin back in — cover the wound loosely with a clean cloth and call 911.
Do not apply tight compression directly over a suspected fracture or open wound.
Do not remove a splint once applied unless circulation below the limb is being cut off.
Do not give food or drink — the person may need surgery.
redFlags: Bone visible through the skin (open/compound fracture) — call 911 immediately
Numbness, tingling, pale or blue skin, or coldness below the injury — possible nerve or blood vessel damage, call 911
Signs of shock: pale or ashen skin, cold clamminess, faintness, rapid shallow breathing — call 911
Severe deformity or limb at an unnatural angle — call 911
Suspected hip, pelvis, or upper thigh (femur) fracture — call 911, do not attempt to move the person
Limb becomes colder or more numb after splinting — loosen ties and reassess immediately
preparednessTips: Include triangular bandages (slings), elastic wraps, and SAM splints in your first aid kit — they are lightweight and work for most limb injuries.
Practice identifying splint materials: a rolled-up magazine, a trekking pole, or a sturdy stick all work. The key is rigidity and padding.
Know the circulation check: warmth (feel the skin), color (pink vs. pale/blue), sensation (can they feel you touch their fingers/toes). Take 5 seconds to check before and after splinting.

sourceReferences.title: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for RICE steps, splinting technique, circulation checks, and what not to do for bystander management of sprains and suspected fractures.

sourceReferences.title: Fractures: Types, Symptoms, and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Source for fracture recognition signs, 911 call criteria, shock risk from large-bone fractures, and open fracture handling.

sourceReferences.title: 2024 American Heart Association and American Red Cross Guidelines for First Aid
sourceReferences.organization: American Heart Association / American Red Cross
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Most recent comprehensive update of authoritative first aid guidelines (2024), co-authored by AHA and Red Cross, covering musculoskeletal injury management.

guides.title: Cardiac Arrest: Start CPR Immediately

guideData.title: Cardiac Arrest: Start CPR Immediately
guideData.summary: If someone is unresponsive and not breathing normally, they are likely in cardiac arrest. Start CPR immediately — every minute without compressions reduces survival odds by 7–10%. You do not need formal training to help: push hard and fast in the center of the chest until emergency services arrive.
guideData.whenToUse: A person is unresponsive and not breathing normally (gasping or no breaths at all)
guideData.preferredOption: Call 911 immediately (or tell someone else to), then begin Hands-Only CPR: place the heel of your hand on the center of the chest, stack your other hand on top, and push straight down hard and fast at 100–120 compressions per minute. Do not stop until EMS arrives.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are trained in CPR: after every 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths (pinch nose, seal mouth, 1 breath per second, watch for chest rise). If alone and untrained, continue compression-only — do not attempt rescue breaths.
steps: Check the scene is safe. Tap the person's shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you OK?' If no response, they are unresponsive.
Call 911 immediately or shout for someone nearby to call. Ask them to bring an AED if one is available. Put 911 on speaker.
Check for breathing for no more than 10 seconds. Look for chest rise. Gasping, gurgling, or no movement all count as not breathing normally — start CPR.
Lay the person on their back on a firm, flat surface. Kneel beside their chest.
Place the heel of one hand on the lower half of the sternum — the center of the chest, between the nipples. Place your other hand on top and interlace your fingers. Keep fingers off the ribs.
Lock your elbows, position your shoulders directly over your hands, and compress straight down at least 2 inches (5 cm) but no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm). Push hard.
Push at a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute — roughly the beat of 'Stayin' Alive.' Let the chest fully rise back up after each compression. Do not lean on the chest between pushes.

If untrained: continue compressions without stopping. Do not attempt rescue breaths. If trained: after every 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths — tilt the head back, lift the chin, pinch the nose shut, seal your mouth over theirs, and give 1 breath per second. Each breath should make the chest visibly rise. Avoid blowing hard.

If an AED arrives: turn it on and follow the voice prompts. Attach pads to bare, dry skin. Clear everyone away from the person during analysis and before any shock. Resume compressions immediately after the shock, or immediately if no shock is advised.
Continue CPR until EMS takes over, the person begins breathing normally on their own, or you are physically unable to continue.
warnings: Gasping, gurgling, or irregular breathing is NOT normal breathing. Do not wait to see if they breathe on their own — start CPR immediately.
If you hear or feel cracking during compressions, rib fractures occur in roughly 1 in 3 CPR cases. Do not stop — broken ribs can be repaired; brain death from no compressions cannot. Keep going.
Every minute without CPR reduces survival odds by 7–10%. Hesitation is the most dangerous mistake. Imperfect CPR is always better than no CPR.
If you cannot reach 911, use any available means to get help — flag down someone, shout, send a bystander. Do not leave the person alone to go find help unless you are truly the only person present.

For children (age 1 to puberty): compress to about 2 inches or one-third of the chest depth. Rescue breaths are more important for children because their cardiac arrests are usually caused by breathing failure, not heart rhythm problems. Give 30 compressions then 2 breaths even if untrained.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait for the person to stop gasping before starting — gasping is not normal breathing and CPR should begin immediately.
Do not compress too deep (more than 2.4 inches for adults) or at the wrong location — place hands on the lower half of the sternum, not on the lower tip or upper chest.
Do not bend your elbows during compressions — locked arms and body weight over the hands produce the correct depth and reduce fatigue.
Do not lean on the chest between compressions — the chest must fully recoil after each push to let the heart refill with blood.
Do not pause compressions for more than 10 seconds for any reason, including rescue breaths or AED setup.
Do not blow too hard during rescue breaths — over-inflation pushes air into the stomach, causing vomiting and airway obstruction. Give just enough air to make the chest rise.
redFlags: No movement, coughing, or normal breathing after several cycles of CPR and an AED shock — continue CPR, do not stop
Person vomits during CPR — quickly turn them on their side, clear the mouth with your fingers or cloth, and resume compressions
AED advises 'no shock recommended' — continue CPR immediately; this means the rhythm may be non-shockable but compressions are still needed
Person shows signs of returning to life (normal breathing, movement, opening eyes) — stop compressions, place in recovery position if breathing, and monitor closely until EMS arrives
preparednessTips: Take a CPR/AED course from the American Heart Association or American Red Cross — hands-on training builds the muscle memory and confidence to act in an emergency.
Know where the nearest AED is in your workplace, gym, school, or community center. Many are wall-mounted in public spaces with clear signage.
Practice the compression rate: download a metronome app or find 'Stayin' Alive' on a playlist. 100–120 BPM is the correct range.
Teach other household members where your home AED is (if you have one) and how to call 911 — having a second person on scene dramatically improves outcomes.
Cardiac arrest can happen to anyone. You cannot make a person worse by attempting CPR — you can only help.

sourceReferences.title: Part 7: Adult Basic Life Support: 2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for CPR and ECC
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary authoritative source for adult bystander CPR: compression rate, depth, hands-only vs 30:2, AED use, and survival statistics.

sourceReferences.title: Part 6: Pediatric Basic Life Support: 2025 AHA/AAP Guidelines for CPR and ECC
sourceReferences.organization: American Heart Association / American Academy of Pediatrics
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Source for child CPR differences: compression depth, rescue breath importance, and two-rescuer ratios.

sourceReferences.title: CPR Steps: How to Perform CPR
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Step-by-step lay-rescuer CPR sequence, chest recoil guidance, rescue breath technique, and AED integration.

sourceReferences.title: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) complications encountered in forensic autopsy cases
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Peer-reviewed source establishing that rib fractures occur in approximately 1 in 3 CPR cases and are an expected complication, supporting the warning not to stop CPR when cracking is felt.

guides.title: Preparing for a Nuclear or Radiological Emergency

guideData.title: Preparing for a Nuclear or Radiological Emergency
guideData.summary: Nuclear and radiological emergencies require specific advance preparation beyond a standard emergency kit. Identifying your best shelter room, stocking enough food and water, keeping a hand-crank radio, and understanding shelter-in-place and decontamination basics are the core pillars. Potassium iodide (KI) plays a limited, specific supporting role — it protects only the thyroid from radioactive iodine and should only be used if directed by authorities.
guideData.whenToUse: You want to prepare your home and family for a nuclear or radiological emergency before one occurs
guideData.preferredOption: Identify the most protected room in your home (basement or interior room with the most mass around it), stock 3+ days of supplies there, and designate a family communication plan with a pre-agreed meeting point for after a shelter-in-place period.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot prepare a dedicated shelter room, at minimum keep a hand-crank radio, 3-day water supply, and sealed food in your home. Know where the nearest basement or large solid building is within walking distance.
warnings: Potassium iodide (KI) tablets only protect the thyroid from one specific type of radiation — radioactive iodine (I-131). They do not protect against blast injury, general fallout radiation, or any other radionuclides. In a nuclear detonation scenario, KI is unlikely to be the right intervention for most people.
Taking KI without medical guidance, or taking incorrect doses, can cause serious harm. Only use KI if directed by public health authorities or a healthcare provider.
A nuclear emergency may require you to shelter in place for 24 hours to a month, not just a few hours. Prepare for extended shelter-in-place, not just a quick event.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume your regular 72-hour kit is sufficient for a nuclear event — nuclear preparedness requires specific additions (battery/crank radio, plastic sheeting, duct tape, extra sealed water and food, change of clothing in sealed bags).
Do not stockpile KI tablets without understanding when and how to use them — and never take them unless directed by authorities.
Do not plan to drive away from a nuclear detonation — if you are in the fallout zone, sheltering in place is safer than driving through the zone.
preparednessTips: Identify the most protected room in your home: a basement is best. If no basement, choose an interior room on the lowest floor, surrounded by as much mass (concrete, brick, earth) as possible and with no windows.
Walk through your home and identify the quickest route to that room so every family member knows where to go without thinking.

Pre-position emergency supplies inside or near that shelter room: water (1 gallon/person/day for at least 3 days), sealed non-perishable food, battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio, prescription medications (30-day supply), first aid kit, and extra clothing sealed in plastic bags.
Store heavy-duty plastic sheeting (4 mil or thicker) and duct tape in your kit — these can quickly seal windows and doors to reduce outside air infiltration during sheltering.
Keep a supply of large resealable plastic bags for bagging contaminated outer clothing during decontamination.
Create a family emergency communication plan: designate an out-of-area contact that all family members can check in with, and agree on a meeting point for after a shelter-in-place period ends.
Enroll in your community's emergency alert system and download the FEMA app so you receive official instructions immediately when an event occurs.
Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank radio operational and charged — cell service and internet may fail after a nuclear event. This is your most reliable source of official instructions.

Learn where the nearest publicly designated fallout shelter is in your community — check with your local emergency management agency. If none are designated, identify the nearest large brick or concrete multi-story building.

About potassium iodide (KI): KI only protects the thyroid gland from radioactive iodine (I-131) — it does not protect against blast, general fallout, or other radiation types. The NRC and FDA provide KI to residents within 10 miles of nuclear power plants. Talk to your doctor about whether KI is appropriate for your household, and only take it if directed by public health authorities.

sourceReferences.title: Radiation Emergencies — Be Ready
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for nuclear-specific preparedness steps: shelter room identification, emergency kit contents, and family communication plan.

sourceReferences.title: Frequently Asked Questions on Potassium Iodide (KI)

sourceReferences.title: Potassium Iodide (KI)
