guides.title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis Warning Signs

guideData.title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis Warning Signs
guideData.summary: Anaphylaxis is a severe, rapidly progressing allergic reaction that can cause airway, breathing, or circulation failure within minutes. Epinephrine is the only effective first-line treatment — antihistamines alone cannot reverse anaphylaxis.
guideData.whenToUse: After exposure to a known or suspected allergen (food, insect sting, medication, latex) with any systemic symptoms
guideData.preferredOption: Recognize anaphylaxis early, call emergency services, and help administer prescribed epinephrine at the first signs of airway or circulation involvement.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no epinephrine is available, call emergency services immediately, keep the person calm and lying down (or sitting up if breathing is easier), and monitor breathing closely — do not leave them alone.
steps: Ask about known allergies and what the person was recently exposed to.
Look for hives, widespread itching, flushing, or facial swelling.
Check for airway symptoms: throat tightness, hoarse voice, trouble swallowing, stridor (high-pitched breathing noise).
Check for breathing symptoms: wheeze, shortness of breath, chest tightness.
Check for circulation symptoms: dizziness, feeling faint, pale or clammy skin, rapid weak pulse.
If any airway, breathing, or circulation symptom is present — call emergency services immediately.
Help use an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen or equivalent) if prescribed and available: inject into the outer mid-thigh through clothing if needed.
Position the person based on symptoms: lying flat with legs raised for faintness; sitting up for breathing difficulty.
Even after epinephrine, call emergency services — anaphylaxis can return (biphasic reaction) hours later.
Monitor closely until emergency responders arrive and be prepared to give a second epinephrine dose if available and symptoms return or worsen.
warnings: Anaphylaxis can progress from mild symptoms to airway closure or cardiac arrest in minutes — do not wait to see if it gets worse.
Antihistamines (diphenhydramine/Benadryl) do not treat anaphylaxis — they are too slow and do not reverse airway or circulation collapse.
A biphasic reaction can occur hours after the initial episode resolves — all anaphylaxis cases should be evaluated in an emergency setting.
Epinephrine is safe to administer even if anaphylaxis is suspected but not certain — the risks of delay outweigh the risks of epinephrine injection.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait for all symptoms to appear before acting — early epinephrine and emergency services are critical.
Do not rely on antihistamines alone for suspected anaphylaxis.
Do not let the person stand up or walk if they feel faint — they can collapse suddenly.
Do not leave the person alone once anaphylaxis is suspected.
redFlags: Throat tightness, stridor, or hoarse voice — airway swelling can close the airway completely within minutes.
Difficulty breathing, wheeze, or chest tightness after allergen exposure.
Dizziness, fainting, or sudden rapid drop in blood pressure.
Symptoms rapidly spreading or progressing despite initial treatment.
preparednessTips: People with known severe allergies should carry TWO epinephrine auto-injectors at all times — AAAAI/ACAAI 2023 guidelines recommend two in case a second dose is needed.
Store epinephrine at room temperature (59–77°F / 15–25°C) — do not freeze or leave in a hot car.
Wear a medical alert bracelet if you have a history of severe allergic reactions.
Know the most common triggers: foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, milk, eggs), insect stings (bee, wasp), medications (penicillin-type antibiotics, NSAIDs), and latex.
Even mild allergic reactions can escalate to anaphylaxis — monitor for 2–4 hours after trigger exposure.

sourceReferences.title: Anaphylaxis: A 2023 Practice Parameter Update
sourceReferences.organization: AAAAI / ACAAI Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Most current (2023) authoritative guidelines from AAAAI and ACAAI covering epinephrine as first-line treatment, when to call EMS, two auto-injector recommendation, and biphasic reaction risk.

sourceReferences.title: Anaphylaxis Recognition and First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing recognition and first-aid guidance for identifying and responding to anaphylaxis in lay rescuer settings.

guides.title: Choking: Unresponsive Adult or Child

guideData.title: Choking: Unresponsive Adult or Child
guideData.summary: When a choking person becomes unresponsive, CPR with chest compressions becomes the primary intervention — each compression may help dislodge the obstruction while also circulating blood. Look for the object in the mouth only if it is clearly visible.
guideData.whenToUse: When a choking person loses consciousness or becomes unresponsive during back blows and abdominal thrusts
guideData.preferredOption: Start CPR immediately — call for an AED and emergency services, then begin 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths (if trained).
guideData.fallbackOption: If you do not know full CPR, follow emergency dispatcher instructions and perform continuous chest compressions until help arrives.
steps: Call or have someone call emergency services and request an AED.
Lower the person carefully to a firm, flat surface.
Check for breathing — if absent or only gasping, begin CPR.
Give 30 chest compressions: place hands on the center of the chest and compress hard and fast at least 2 inches deep.
Before giving rescue breaths, look into the mouth — if you can clearly see the object and can remove it easily, do so.
Do not perform blind deep finger sweeps — this can push the object deeper.
Give 2 rescue breaths if trained; if not trained or unsure, continue compressions-only CPR.
Continue cycles of 30 compressions (and mouth check before breaths) until emergency responders arrive, an AED is attached, or the person recovers.
warnings: Chest compressions take priority over airway checks — do not pause compressions for prolonged mouth searches.
A person may survive even prolonged CPR in a choking emergency — do not stop until emergency services arrive.
AHA guidelines recommend checking the mouth for a visible object before each set of rescue breaths, not before every compression.
whatNotToDo: Do not perform blind or deep finger sweeps — you cannot feel what is there and may push the object deeper.
Do not stop CPR unless the person recovers, you are physically unable to continue, or trained responders take over.
Do not delay compressions to search extensively for the object.
redFlags: No return of spontaneous breathing or pulse after several CPR cycles — continue until EMS arrives.
Rescue breaths not going in (chest not rising) — reposition the head, check for visible obstruction, and continue compressions.
Object removed but person remains unresponsive — continue CPR, as cardiac arrest may have occurred.
preparednessTips: Learn CPR — the American Heart Association and Red Cross offer in-person and online courses.
Know where the nearest AED is located at your workplace, gym, and community center.
After any choking episode requiring CPR or abdominal thrusts, seek emergency medical evaluation even if the person appears recovered — internal injuries are possible.
CPR for infants under 1 year uses 2-finger chest compressions and a different depth — take a course that covers infant CPR separately.

sourceReferences.title: Choking (Unconscious Adult or Child)
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NLM guidance on transitioning from responsive to unresponsive choking management and the role of CPR.

sourceReferences.title: 2020 AHA Guidelines for CPR and ECC: Adult Basic and Advanced Life Support
sourceReferences.whyUseful: AHA guidelines on CPR sequence for unresponsive choking victims, mouth checks before rescue breaths, and compression-only CPR for untrained rescuers.

guides.title: Choking: Responsive Adult or Child

guideData.title: Choking: Responsive Adult or Child
guideData.summary: Choking is a life-threatening emergency when a person cannot breathe, speak, or cough effectively due to an object blocking the airway. Immediate back blows and abdominal thrusts can dislodge the object and restore breathing in minutes.
guideData.whenToUse: When a responsive person shows signs of severe choking: cannot speak, cannot cough effectively, or cannot breathe
guideData.preferredOption: If severe choking is confirmed, start back blows and abdominal thrusts immediately and have someone call emergency services.
guideData.fallbackOption: For pregnant individuals or those where abdominal thrusts are not appropriate, use chest thrusts: same position as abdominal thrusts but hands on the center of the chest.
steps: Ask clearly: 'Are you choking?' If they can speak or cough strongly, encourage coughing — do not intervene yet.
If severe choking is confirmed (cannot speak, breathe, or cough), call for someone to call emergency services.
Lean the person forward with their head lower than their chest.
Give up to 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
Check after each blow — if the object is dislodged, stop.
If back blows don't clear it, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts: stand behind the person, place one fist just above the navel, grab it with your other hand, and thrust sharply inward and upward.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object comes out, the person can breathe, or they become unresponsive.
If the person becomes unresponsive, lower them carefully to the floor and switch to the unresponsive choking guide (CPR with visual mouth checks).
warnings: Use appropriate force — too gentle is ineffective, but for small children use appropriately scaled force.
For children under 1 year (infants), back blows and chest thrusts are used, not abdominal thrusts — infant choking is a separate procedure.
Even after successful clearance, seek medical evaluation if the person had severe distress or the maneuver was performed forcefully.
whatNotToDo: Do not perform blind finger sweeps in a responsive person — this can push the object deeper.
Do not interrupt an effective cough — a person who can cough forcefully is clearing their own airway.
Do not perform abdominal thrusts on infants under 1 year or on someone who is pregnant — use chest thrusts instead.
redFlags: Object not clearing after multiple cycles of back blows and abdominal thrusts.
Persistent breathing difficulty, wheezing, or coughing after the object appears removed — object may be partially lodged.
Person becomes unresponsive — immediately transition to CPR and the unresponsive choking guide.
preparednessTips: Take a Red Cross or AHA Heartsaver first-aid course — choking response is a core skill that takes 10 minutes to learn properly.
For young children: cut food into small pieces (under 1 inch), supervise mealtime, and keep small objects out of reach.
If you are alone and choking: call 911 if possible (stay on the line), then self-administer abdominal thrusts against the back of a chair or a counter edge.
Know that for pregnancy or obesity, chest thrusts replace abdominal thrusts — hands go on center of chest, not abdomen.

sourceReferences.title: Choking First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public-facing choking response steps including back blows, abdominal thrusts, and when to escalate.

sourceReferences.title: Choking in Adults and Children
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NLM guidance reflecting current consensus resuscitation practice for responsive choking.

guides.title: Bleeding Control: Direct Pressure First

guideData.title: Bleeding Control: Direct Pressure First
guideData.summary: Direct pressure is the first and most important action for controlling serious external bleeding. Severe blood loss can cause death within minutes — apply firm, uninterrupted pressure immediately and call emergency services.
guideData.whenToUse: When a wound is bleeding heavily, bleeding does not slow with light pressure, or a dressing soaks through quickly
guideData.preferredOption: Call emergency services early and maintain firm continuous direct pressure on the wound without releasing.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no bandages are available, use any clean cloth, clothing, or your bare hands directly on the wound — imperfect pressure is better than no pressure.
steps: Ensure the scene is safe before approaching.
Put on gloves if available to protect yourself from bloodborne pathogens.
Expose the wound by cutting or removing clothing if needed — do not waste time undressing fully.
Press firmly and directly on the wound with both hands using a clean cloth, bandage, or dressing.
Maintain constant firm pressure without lifting to check — checking restarts the clotting process.
If blood soaks through, add more dressings on top; do not remove soaked materials.
Keep the injured person lying flat and warm to reduce shock risk.
For life-threatening limb bleeding not controlled by pressure: apply a commercial tourniquet 2–3 inches above the wound (not over a joint) and note the time of application.
Stay with the person and continue pressure until emergency responders take over.
warnings: Severe bleeding can be rapidly fatal — do not hesitate to call emergency services immediately.
Once a tourniquet is applied in the field, do not remove or loosen it — that decision belongs to medical professionals.
Blood loss shock signs (pale, cold, clammy skin; rapid weak pulse; confusion) indicate a medical emergency requiring immediate transport.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep lifting the dressing to check if bleeding has stopped — this disrupts clot formation.
Do not remove soaked dressings — add more material on top.
Do not delay calling emergency services to try home measures first.
Do not apply a tourniquet to the neck, torso, or over a joint.
Do not use a tourniquet for minor wounds — direct pressure is the correct first response.
redFlags: Spurting blood (arterial bleed), rapidly expanding pool of blood, or soaked dressings within 1–2 minutes despite firm pressure.
Signs of shock: pale or ashen skin, cold and clammy, rapid weak pulse, confusion, or loss of consciousness.
Amputation, major crush injury, or impalement — all require immediate emergency services.
preparednessTips: Take a Stop the Bleed course — a free 2-hour community training that teaches direct pressure, wound packing, and tourniquet use (stopthebleed.org).
Stock your first aid kit with: exam gloves, sterile gauze pads, a pressure bandage or Israeli bandage, and a commercial tourniquet (CAT or SOFT-T Wide).
Know how to use a tourniquet before you need one — practice applying it to yourself on your arm with one hand.
Know the location of the nearest trauma center — for severe bleeding, every minute of transport time matters.

sourceReferences.title: Life-Threatening External Bleeding
sourceReferences.whyUseful: ARC protocol for severe bleeding control: direct pressure, wound packing, and tourniquet guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Stop the Bleed: Bleeding Control Basics
sourceReferences.organization: American College of Surgeons / Stop the Bleed Campaign
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based public training program for layperson bleeding control — covers pressure, packing, and tourniquet technique.

guides.title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help

guideData.title: Hypothermia: Warm the Core and Get Help
guideData.summary: Hypothermia occurs when the body's core temperature drops dangerously low from cold or wet exposure. Gentle rewarming of the core is critical — rough handling or rapid external rewarming of the limbs can trigger fatal cardiac arrest in moderate to severe cases.
guideData.whenToUse: When someone is shivering, cold, and mildly confused after cold or wet exposure
guideData.preferredOption: Move to warm shelter, replace wet clothes with dry ones, insulate the person, and apply warmth to the chest, neck, and groin first — not the arms and legs.
guideData.fallbackOption: If full shelter is not available, use any windbreak, dry layers, blankets, and body-to-body contact under loose dry coverings to slow further heat loss.
steps: Move the person to a warm room, heated vehicle, or sheltered area out of wind and precipitation.
Gently remove wet clothing and replace with dry clothing, blankets, or a sleeping bag.
Warm the center of the body first — apply warm dry compresses or heat packs to the chest, neck, head, and groin.
Offer warm, sweet, non-alcoholic drinks only if the person is fully awake and can swallow safely.
Handle the person gently and minimize movement — rough handling of someone with severe hypothermia can cause cardiac arrest.
Seek urgent medical care if symptoms are not improving, the person is confused, stops shivering without warming up, or becomes unconscious.
warnings: Moderate to severe hypothermia can be fatal without medical care — symptoms of stopped shivering and extreme drowsiness indicate dangerous deterioration.
Rough handling or aggressive rubbing of limbs can send cold blood rushing to the heart and trigger cardiac arrest.
A person with hypothermia may appear dead (very slow breathing, weak pulse) but may still survive with prolonged CPR and rewarming — do not assume death without warming first.
whatNotToDo: Do not rewarm limbs or extremities first — warming arms and legs drives cold blood back to the core and can worsen cardiac risk.
Do not use very hot water, heating lamps, or direct high heat for rapid rewarming.
Do not rub or massage the arms and legs vigorously.
Do not give alcohol — it causes blood vessels to dilate, increasing heat loss.
Do not give fluids if the person is not fully conscious and cannot swallow safely.
redFlags: Person stops shivering but is still cold — shivering cessation without rewarming signals dangerous core temperature drop.
Severe confusion, inability to walk, slowed or very shallow breathing, or loss of consciousness.
Bluish skin, very weak or absent pulse in a cold environment.
preparednessTips: Carry emergency space blankets in your hiking kit, vehicle, and go-bag — they trap body heat with minimal weight and bulk.
Dress in moisture-wicking base layers in cold environments — cotton holds moisture and accelerates heat loss ('cotton kills').
Know the early signs of hypothermia in yourself: fumbling hands, unusual clumsiness, poor decisions, and unusual tiredness in the cold.
On any cold-weather trip, pack extra dry clothing in a waterproof bag as a hypothermia backup.
Tell someone your route and return time so a search can be launched quickly if you are overdue.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official CDC guidance on hypothermia recognition, first-aid rewarming steps, and what not to do — primary authoritative source.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia: First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed first-aid steps, core rewarming technique, and red flags for when to seek emergency care.

guides.title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help

guideData.title: Heat Stroke: Cool and Call for Help
guideData.summary: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency in which the body can no longer regulate its temperature. Permanent organ damage or death can occur within minutes — immediate cooling and emergency services are both required.
guideData.whenToUse: When someone exposed to heat has very high body temperature, confusion, altered consciousness, seizure, or collapse
guideData.preferredOption: Activate emergency medical services at once and start aggressive cooling with cool water, wet sheets, and airflow while waiting.
guideData.fallbackOption: If EMS is delayed, continue cooling with any safe method available (cool bath, ice packs to neck/armpits/groin, fanning wet skin) and arrange urgent transport to a medical facility.
steps: Call emergency services as soon as heat stroke is suspected — do not wait to see if cooling alone resolves it.
Move the person to the coolest accessible place: indoors with air conditioning, shade, or a cool vehicle.
Remove excess clothing quickly to allow body heat to escape.
Cool the body rapidly: apply cool water to skin, use wet sheets or towels, direct a fan to increase evaporation.
Apply ice packs or cold packs to the neck, armpits, and groin where large blood vessels run close to the surface.
Fan the person while keeping the skin wet — evaporation is one of the most effective rapid-cooling methods.
If the person is vomiting or unconscious, place on their side to protect the airway (recovery position).
Continue active cooling until emergency responders arrive or the person is confirmed under medical supervision.
warnings: Heat stroke can cause permanent brain, kidney, and muscle damage or death — it is not the same as heat exhaustion.
Rapid cooling and emergency care are critical — every minute of high body temperature increases risk of organ damage.
Do not give fluids to a person who is confused, vomiting, or unconscious — aspiration risk is high.
Even after apparent recovery, heat stroke survivors are at elevated risk of recurrence for weeks.
whatNotToDo: Do not give fluids by mouth if the person is confused, vomiting, or unconscious.
Do not delay calling emergency services while trying home cooling measures first.
Do not use alcohol rubs — alcohol is absorbed through skin and can cause other harm.
Do not apply ice directly over large body areas for extended periods — this can cause shivering which generates heat.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, seizure, or loss of consciousness with heat exposure — these confirm heat stroke, not heat exhaustion.
Very hot body with red, dry, or flushed skin and no sweating.
Collapse or inability to respond normally after time in the heat.
preparednessTips: Know the difference: heat exhaustion (sweating, pale, nauseous, weak) is less severe; heat stroke (hot dry skin, confusion, no sweating) is immediately life-threatening.
Know your local emergency number before working or exercising in high heat.
Use buddy checks when working in high heat — check on partners every 15–20 minutes.
During heat waves, check on elderly neighbors, young children, and people without air conditioning.
Never leave a person, child, or pet in a parked car — interior temperatures rise to dangerous levels in minutes even in mild weather.
Stay hydrated before and during heat exposure — do not wait until thirsty to drink water.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative description of heat stroke, rapid cooling steps, and the critical distinction from heat exhaustion.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Cramps, Exhaustion, Stroke
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NWS public guidance on heat stroke first-aid priorities and recognition criteria.

sourceReferences.title: Signs and Symptoms of Heat Illness

guides.title: Send an SOS Signal Using Morse Code

guideData.title: Send an SOS Signal Using Morse Code
guideData.summary: How to send the internationally recognized SOS distress signal (· · · − − − · · ·) using a flashlight, whistle, tapping, blinking, or mirror — step by step, regardless of what tools you have available.
guideData.whenToUse: You are lost, stranded, injured, or in danger and need rescue
guideData.preferredOption: Use a flashlight or signal mirror to flash SOS toward rescuers or aircraft — light signals carry the farthest and work at night.
guideData.fallbackOption: Use a whistle to blast SOS — three short blasts, three long blasts, three short blasts. A whistle carries further than voice and can be heard through debris or dense foliage.
steps: Remember the SOS pattern: SHORT SHORT SHORT — LONG LONG LONG — SHORT SHORT SHORT. Short = brief, Long = three times the duration of a short.
Choose your signaling method based on what you have: flashlight → flash the pattern; whistle → blast the pattern; hard surface → tap the pattern; mirror → reflect the pattern.
FLASHLIGHT: Flash three short flashes (~0.5 sec each), then three long flashes (~1.5 sec each), then three short flashes. Aim toward rescuers, vehicles, or aircraft — not straight up.
WHISTLE: Blow three short blasts, then three long blasts (~1.5–2 sec each), then three short blasts. Blow into wind so the sound carries toward potential rescuers.

TAPPING: On a pipe, wall, floor, or window, tap three times quickly, pause, then three times slowly (hold contact ~3x longer), pause, then three times quickly. Works in confined spaces, collapsed structures, or vehicles.

MIRROR or REFLECTIVE SURFACE: Hold the mirror or phone screen to catch sunlight. Flash three short reflections, three long reflections, three short. A 2-inch mirror can be seen 10+ miles on a clear day.
EYE BLINK (last resort / immobile): Blink three times fast, pause, three times slow and deliberate, pause, three times fast. Use toward anyone watching you.
After the full pattern, pause 3–5 seconds, then repeat. Keep repeating until you get a response or are rescued.
Conserve energy and battery — signal in bursts. Rest between sequences. Do not signal continuously without breaks.
warnings: Do not insert pauses between the S, O, and S groups — SOS is one continuous signal sequence, not three separate letters. Pausing between groups may confuse rescuers.
Signaling continuously without rest or shelter can worsen exposure, exhaustion, and battery drain.
A flashlight aimed upward into the sky is less effective than aiming at a known rescuer position, road, or aircraft.
Whistle blasts carry further in cool, calm air — wind, rain, and hot air reduce range significantly.
Mirror signaling requires sunlight and a clear sightline to be effective.
whatNotToDo: Do not use random irregular flashing or tapping — an unrecognized pattern will not be interpreted as a distress call.
Do not stop signaling after one round — rescuers may not be looking in your direction on the first pass.
Do not use your only light source for extended continuous signaling if your battery is low — pace yourself.
Do not shout instead of using the Morse pattern — voice carries much shorter distances and fatigues you faster.
redFlags: Signals going completely unanswered as weather worsens or darkness falls with no shelter.
Battery or light source depleted before rescue arrives with no backup signaling method.
Injury or deteriorating condition that prevents you from maintaining a signaling pattern — prioritize getting to visible ground or marking your position instead.
preparednessTips: Memorize SHORT SHORT SHORT — LONG LONG LONG — SHORT SHORT SHORT before any trip into the wilderness, backcountry, or remote areas.
Carry a signaling mirror and whistle in your emergency kit — both are lightweight, battery-free, and effective over long distances.
Many modern flashlights have a built-in SOS mode — learn how to activate it before you need it.
Practice sending SOS with a flashlight before any trip so the pattern is automatic under stress.
Tell someone your route and expected return time — a search will be launched sooner if you are reported overdue.

sourceReferences.title: Recommendation ITU-R M.1677-1: International Morse Code
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Defines the SOS prosign (· · · − − − · · ·) and the 1:3 dot-to-dash timing ratio used in all SOS sending methods.

sourceReferences.title: How to Signal SOS to Get Yourself to Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Covers flashlight, whistle, tapping, and blinking SOS methods with practical timing and technique guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Army Field Manual FM 21-76: Survival
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Identifies SOS as the primary survivor distress call and covers mirror/reflective signaling range and technique in field conditions.

guides.title: EMP (Electromagnetic Pulse): What Stops Working and What to Do

guideData.title: EMP (Electromagnetic Pulse): What Stops Working and What to Do
guideData.summary: A high-altitude nuclear detonation can produce an electromagnetic pulse (EMP) that disables or damages unprotected electronics and grid infrastructure across a large area — potentially a region or more. EMP from a nuclear event means the grid failure is part of a broader nuclear emergency, not a standalone power outage.
guideData.whenToUse: Electronics and power suddenly stop working with no warning across a wide area
guideData.preferredOption: Treat a nuclear EMP as a nuclear emergency first — follow Get Inside/Stay Inside/Stay Tuned protocols immediately. Then manage the resulting extended grid-down situation using non-electronic supplies and battery/hand-crank radio.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no nuclear event is confirmed and only an unexplained widespread power and electronics outage has occurred, shelter in place, use battery-powered radio for information, and treat the situation as a potential nuclear threat until confirmed otherwise by official sources.
steps: If a nuclear EMP has occurred alongside or following a nuclear detonation: follow nuclear explosion immediate response guidance first (Get Inside, Stay Inside, Stay Tuned). The EMP is secondary to blast and fallout threats.

Assume that most unprotected electronics are non-functional: cell phones, internet, computers, most vehicles manufactured after the late 1970s, and grid power may all be disrupted or permanently damaged.

Switch to battery-powered or hand-crank radio immediately. FEMA has tested and hardened the National Public Warning System specifically for EMP survival — official emergency broadcasts should still be available on battery radios.
Do not rely on electronic navigation (GPS), digital communications, or any grid-dependent systems for planning or communication. Use paper maps if available.

Manage food and water carefully: refrigerators and freezers will stop working. Apply standard power outage food safety rules — food in a closed refrigerator is safe for about 4 hours; a closed freezer for 24–48 hours.

If your vehicle starts (older vehicles with minimal electronics may be unaffected; newer vehicles may be disabled), only use it for evacuation if officially directed and if roads are clear. An EMP that affects vehicles may disable traffic signals and create dangerous road conditions.

Prepare for an extended outage — EMP damage to the power grid can take months to years to fully repair, far longer than a typical storm-related outage. Prioritize water supply (municipal water pumps may be offline), food, shelter, and warmth based on season.
warnings: An EMP originating from a nuclear detonation means you are in a nuclear emergency — radiation from fallout may be present. Do not treat it as only a power outage. Follow nuclear detonation shelter-in-place protocols.
EMP effects are unpredictable — some electronics may function, others may not. Do not assume any electronic device is working correctly after an EMP event.
Grid restoration after a significant EMP event may take far longer than any normal power outage — months to years, not hours or days. Prepare for extended self-sufficiency.

Battery-powered devices may survive a nuclear EMP if they were not connected to the power grid or to long antenna/wire runs at the time of the pulse. Portable battery radios stored in a drawer (not plugged in) have a reasonable chance of functioning.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume the situation is only a power outage — if the outage is sudden, widespread, and all electronics fail simultaneously, treat it as a potential nuclear emergency.

Do not attempt to drive long distances immediately after a suspected EMP event — roads may be gridlocked with stalled vehicles, traffic signals will be out, and fuel pumps (electronic) may be non-functional.
Do not try to recharge devices using damaged grid power — power surges from grid restoration after an EMP can damage equipment.
Do not dismiss EMP as science fiction — U.S. government agencies including DHS, CISA, and FEMA have published serious preparedness guidance on EMP threats.
redFlags: All electronic devices — including those on battery — suddenly stop working simultaneously: this is a potential indicator of a very high-intensity EMP.
You hear or receive any indication of a nuclear detonation alongside the power failure — treat as a nuclear emergency immediately.
Water stops flowing from taps shortly after the event — municipal water pumps may be offline, making stored water critical.
preparednessTips: Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank NOAA weather radio as a primary emergency communication device. Handheld battery radios stored in metal containers (improvised Faraday cage) have a higher chance of surviving a high-altitude EMP.
Maintain a supply of non-electronic tools and reference materials: paper maps, printed emergency contact lists, analog thermometers, manual can opener, and paper-based reference guides.

Store water beyond your normal emergency supply: if municipal water pumps fail, tap water will stop within hours to days. FEMA recommends 1 gallon per person per day minimum; for EMP scenarios, store 2 weeks or more if possible.
Keep non-perishable food that requires no cooking (or can be cooked over a non-electric heat source) for an extended period.
Know how to manually open your garage door — most electric garage door openers will not work without power.

guides.title: Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS): Signs, Stages, and Care

guideData.title: Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS): Signs, Stages, and Care
guideData.summary: Acute radiation syndrome (ARS) occurs when the body receives a high dose of penetrating radiation in a short time. The deceptive latent phase — where a person feels well for hours to weeks — is the most dangerous misconception. Anyone who may have received a significant radiation dose should seek medical evaluation even if they feel fine.
guideData.whenToUse: You want to understand the signs and stages of acute radiation syndrome after a nuclear or radiological event
warnings: The latent phase of ARS is medically deceptive — a person may feel completely normal for hours to days after a significant radiation dose, then rapidly deteriorate. Do not assume someone is fine because they feel fine shortly after exposure.

ARS requires a very high whole-body radiation dose to develop. Most people caught in a nuclear event who sheltered promptly will not develop ARS. The risk is highest for those in or near the blast zone or those who were outside during fallout without shelter.
Nausea and vomiting alone are not diagnostic of ARS — many common conditions cause these symptoms. The key factor is confirmed or suspected high radiation exposure combined with these symptoms.
ARS treatment is highly specialized and requires hospital-level care. There is no effective treatment you can administer at home.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume radiation exposure is harmless because the person feels well in the hours after — the latent phase can last days before manifest illness.
Do not attempt to treat ARS at home — it requires expert hematology and radiation medicine care.
Do not delay seeking medical evaluation for someone with suspected significant exposure, even if they are currently asymptomatic.
redFlags: Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea beginning within minutes to hours of a suspected high-dose radiation exposure — this is the prodromal stage of ARS and requires medical evaluation.
Erythema (redness), itching, or burning of the skin after radiation exposure — may indicate cutaneous radiation injury (CRI), which can develop separately from or alongside ARS.
Rapid onset of fatigue, high fever, hair loss (typically 2–3 weeks post-exposure), or bruising/bleeding without clear cause — signs of the manifest illness phase of ARS.
Loss of consciousness, seizures, or sudden severe deterioration in anyone who was exposed to high-dose radiation.
preparednessTips: In a nuclear event, record when someone first experienced nausea or vomiting after the event — time of onset is used clinically to estimate radiation dose when dosimetry is unavailable.
The REAC/TS (Radiation Emergency Assistance Center/Training Site) hotline is available for medical providers: (865) 576-3131 (weekdays) or (865) 576-1005 (after hours).
Potassium iodide (KI) does NOT prevent or treat ARS — it only protects the thyroid from radioactive iodine. It has no role in treating ARS from general radiation exposure.

sourceReferences.title: Acute Radiation Syndrome: Signs and Symptoms
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary public-facing CDC source on ARS definition, four stages, and key warning signs.

sourceReferences.title: Acute Radiation Syndrome: Information for Clinicians
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clinical guidance on ARS diagnosis, dose estimation, treatment considerations, and the REAC/TS hotline.

sourceReferences.title: Time Phases of Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS)
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed clinical timeline for ARS stages including the latent phase duration and dose-response relationships.

sourceReferences.title: Managing Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS)
sourceReferences.whyUseful: REMM clinical management guidance including the 4 ARS subsyndromes (hematologic, GI, cutaneous, neurovascular) and treatment approach.

guides.title: Understanding Nuclear and Radiological Threats

guideData.title: Understanding Nuclear and Radiological Threats
guideData.summary: Nuclear weapons, dirty bombs, and nuclear power plant accidents are distinct threats with different effects, different scales of damage, and different protective actions. This reference explains blast zones, fallout patterns, radiation dose concepts, and what protection factor means — so you understand why sheltering actions work.
guideData.whenToUse: You want to understand the difference between a nuclear weapon, dirty bomb, and nuclear plant accident
warnings: This is background reference material — in an actual emergency, follow the action guides for immediate response. Do not read reference content during an active event.

Radiation dose and risk depend heavily on distance from the blast, whether you sheltered, how long you were exposed, and whether decontamination occurred. General dose tables cannot accurately predict individual risk without specific exposure information.
whatNotToDo: Do not confuse a dirty bomb with a nuclear weapon — the response actions differ significantly. A dirty bomb has no nuclear blast; evacuation from the explosion site (upwind) is typically appropriate, whereas a nuclear detonation requires immediate sheltering in the nearest building.

Do not assume that an air burst (no ground contact, limited fallout) is less dangerous than a ground burst in terms of blast and thermal damage — air bursts can actually extend the blast damage radius significantly compared to a ground burst.
preparednessTips: Understand which type of nuclear or radiological threat is most relevant to your location — proximity to military targets, nuclear power plants, or major cities all affect risk profile.

Learn the 7-10 rule: every 7-fold increase in time after a nuclear detonation results in a 10-fold reduction in radiation levels. At 7 hours post-detonation, radiation is 10x lower than at 1 hour. At 49 hours, it is 100x lower. This is why sheltering for 24 hours is so effective.
Know your building's protection factor: a basement of a wood-frame home provides PF ~10 (10x less radiation than outside). A basement or center of a large concrete building provides PF ~100+.

sourceReferences.title: Planning Guidance for Response to a Nuclear Detonation (3rd Edition)

sourceReferences.whyUseful: REMM clinical overview of nuclear detonation effects, blast damage zones, and the five primary effects of a nuclear explosion.

guides.title: Dirty Bomb (Radiological Dispersal Device): Immediate Response

guideData.title: Dirty Bomb (Radiological Dispersal Device): Immediate Response
guideData.summary: A dirty bomb uses conventional explosives to disperse radioactive material — it is NOT a nuclear weapon and does not produce a nuclear blast or mushroom cloud. The explosion itself is the immediate danger. After the blast, moving away from the site and decontaminating are the priorities.
guideData.whenToUse: A conventional explosion has occurred and authorities have indicated radioactive material may have been released
guideData.preferredOption: Move away from the explosion area upwind if outdoors, then get inside the nearest intact, undamaged building. Remove outer clothing and shelter in place until official instructions are received.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot immediately get inside, cover your nose and mouth with a dry cloth and move rapidly away from the explosion site. Seek any indoor shelter as soon as possible.
steps: Move away from the immediate explosion area. Unlike a nuclear detonation, a dirty bomb does not produce a massive blast wave — the conventional explosion has already occurred. Your priority is to put distance between yourself and the source of radioactive material.

If outdoors, move upwind from the explosion and toward the nearest intact building. If you do not know wind direction, simply move away from the site. Cover your nose and mouth with a dry cloth or clothing to reduce inhalation of particles.
Get inside the nearest intact, undamaged building. Avoid buildings that were structurally damaged by the blast. Close all windows and doors. Turn off HVAC, fans, and forced-air systems.
Remove your outer clothing as soon as you are inside. This removes up to 90% of radioactive surface contamination. Bag the clothing in plastic and place it away from people.
Wash exposed skin and hair with soap and water. Do not use conditioner. If no shower is available, wipe exposed skin with a wet cloth.

Stay inside and tune in to a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for official instructions. Authorities will determine the affected zone, levels of radiation, and whether evacuation or continued shelter-in-place is appropriate.
Do not return to the incident area for any reason — the zone may remain off-limits for months during cleanup.
warnings: The presence of radioactive material from a dirty bomb will NOT be detectable by sight, smell, or sensation — only trained personnel with radiation detection equipment can confirm contamination levels.

A dirty bomb explosion is primarily a psychological and conventional weapon. Radiation levels from most RDDs are not high enough to cause acute radiation syndrome, but contamination should be treated seriously.
The affected area may be placed off-limits for months during decontamination and cleanup. Do not return until officially cleared.
Emergency alerts may be slow or incomplete immediately after the event. Wait for confirmed official guidance before deciding to evacuate.

The critical difference from a nuclear detonation: for a dirty bomb, moving away from the site is typically appropriate. For a nuclear detonation, sheltering in the nearest building is typically the priority. Follow official instructions for the specific event.
whatNotToDo: Do not return to or enter the explosion site for any reason — not to help others, retrieve belongings, or investigate.
Do not assume you are safe just because you feel fine — radiation contamination has no immediate symptoms at most RDD exposure levels.
Do not use hand sanitizer to decontaminate — only soap and water (or wet cloth) are effective.
Do not ignore official evacuation orders — even if you feel fine, the long-term contamination risk depends on leaving the affected zone when directed.
Do not assume this is the same as a nuclear detonation — do not shelter in place if authorities are directing evacuation of the area.
redFlags: Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea within hours of exposure — though unusual for dirty bomb levels, these may indicate unexpectedly high radiation exposure requiring medical attention.
Skin redness, burning, or blistering from proximity to the explosion.
Blast injuries from the conventional explosion: lacerations, burns, hearing loss, or trauma from debris — these are the most likely immediate injuries and require standard emergency medical care.
preparednessTips: Know the difference between a dirty bomb (radiological dispersal device) and a nuclear weapon — response actions differ significantly.
Include a change of sealed clothing in your emergency kit for post-event decontamination.
Keep a battery or hand-crank radio in your kit — official guidance will direct whether to shelter-in-place or evacuate after a radiological event.
Store large resealable plastic bags for quickly sealing contaminated clothing.

sourceReferences.title: Radiological Dispersion Device
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary FEMA source for dirty bomb immediate response: move away from site, shelter in nearest building, decontaminate, do not return to site.

sourceReferences.title: Radiological Dispersal Device (RDD) Response Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: FEMA planning guidance covering RDD threat characterization, the absence of nuclear blast, off-limits cleanup duration, and upwind evacuation guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Radiological Dispersal Devices (RDDs)
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clinical and public health characterization of RDDs: radiation levels typically insufficient for ARS, contamination vs. exposure distinction.

guides.title: Radiation Decontamination: Remove Fallout from Your Body

guideData.title: Radiation Decontamination: Remove Fallout from Your Body
guideData.summary: If you were outdoors during or after a nuclear detonation or radiological release, removing your outer clothing and washing exposed skin can eliminate up to 90% of radioactive contamination. Act quickly — early decontamination dramatically reduces total radiation dose.
guideData.whenToUse: You were outside when a nuclear detonation occurred or when radioactive fallout arrived
guideData.preferredOption: Remove outer clothing and bag it immediately, then shower with soap and water (not conditioner). Do this before or immediately after entering shelter.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no shower is available, wipe exposed skin and hair with a wet cloth or moist wipe. Blow your nose and gently wipe around eyes and ears with a damp cloth.
steps: Remove your outer clothing as quickly as possible. Be careful not to shake it — shake as little as possible to prevent inhaling dislodged particles. Removing your outermost layer eliminates approximately 80–90% of radioactive surface contamination.
Place removed clothing in a plastic bag. Seal the bag tightly and set it far away from people and pets — outside the shelter if possible, or in a corner well away from living areas.

If shower is available: shower with warm water and soap. Wash gently — do not scrub or scratch skin harshly. Shampoo your hair with soap or shampoo. Do NOT use conditioner — conditioner binds radioactive particles to hair strands.

If no shower is available: use a wet cloth, wet wipes, or water from a sink to wash all exposed skin. Wash hands, face, neck, and any skin that was uncovered. Blow your nose to clear any inhaled particles. Gently wipe eyelids, eyelashes, and ears with a damp cloth.
Tap water from indoor pipes is safe to use for decontamination even if outdoor water may be contaminated. Any radioactive material that gets into surface water is diluted to very low levels.
Put on clean clothing from inside the building. Do not put contaminated clothing back on.
Keep cuts and abrasions covered when washing — use waterproof dressings or tape if available to prevent radioactive material from entering open wounds.
Hand sanitizer does NOT neutralize or remove radioactive material. Only water and soap (or wet cloth) are effective.
warnings: Early decontamination matters — radioactive fallout decay happens fastest in the first hours. Gross decontamination done immediately, even imperfectly, is far better than waiting for a formal decontamination team.

Being contaminated (radioactive material on or in the body) is different from radiation exposure. Contamination can be reduced by decontamination. External radiation exposure from fallout cannot be reversed, but further exposure can be stopped by getting inside and decontaminating.
Children and pregnant individuals should be prioritized for decontamination assistance.
Do not vigorously scrub skin — it can create small abrasions that allow radioactive particles to enter the body.
If your eyes, nose, or mouth feel irritated, flush with clean water immediately.
whatNotToDo: Do not use conditioner on hair — it binds radioactive particles to hair and makes decontamination harder.
Do not shake or brush contaminated clothing vigorously — this releases radioactive dust into the air where it can be inhaled.
Do not use hand sanitizer as a substitute for washing — it does not remove radioactive material.
Do not delay decontamination to find a 'perfect' location — a wet cloth wipe is far better than waiting.
Do not put contaminated clothes back on after washing.
Do not cover your entire body in plastic sheeting — this traps contaminated air and sweat against the skin.
redFlags: Skin redness, burning, or blistering that was not caused by thermal (heat/flash) exposure — this may indicate a high-level radiation skin injury (cutaneous radiation injury) requiring medical attention.
Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea beginning within hours of exposure — these are early symptoms of acute radiation syndrome. Record the time of onset and seek medical care when safely possible.
Eye irritation, redness, or vision changes after exposure — flush immediately with clean water and seek medical care.
preparednessTips: Keep spare sets of clean clothing sealed in plastic bags inside your emergency kit — you may need to change clothing after decontamination.
Store large resealable plastic bags in your kit for quickly bagging contaminated clothing.
Keep moist wipes in your emergency kit — they allow basic decontamination without running water.
Know where the shower or nearest sink is in your shelter location before an emergency occurs.

sourceReferences.title: How to Self-Decontaminate after a Radiation Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for clothing removal (90% effectiveness), shower protocol, no-conditioner rule, wet-cloth fallback, and no-conditioner warning.

sourceReferences.title: Decontamination for Yourself and Others

guides.title: Nuclear Fallout: Shelter-in-Place for 24 Hours

guideData.title: Nuclear Fallout: Shelter-in-Place for 24 Hours
guideData.summary: Once inside a building after a nuclear detonation, staying sheltered for at least 24 hours is the most important survival action. Radioactive fallout is most dangerous in the first hours and declines rapidly — but only if you stay inside and minimize outside air.
guideData.whenToUse: You are inside a building after a nuclear detonation or radiation emergency
guideData.preferredOption: Stay in the basement or center of a middle floor, away from exterior walls and windows. Keep HVAC off and seal any gaps. Remain for at least 24 hours or until official instructions say it is safe to move.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are in a wood-frame building with no basement, move to an interior room on the ground floor and seal it as well as possible. Any interior shelter is significantly safer than being outside.
steps: Move to the most protected part of the building: a basement is best. In a multi-story building, go to the center of a middle floor — not the top floor (fallout accumulates on the roof) and not the ground floor (fallout accumulates on the ground outside). Stay away from all exterior walls and windows.

Turn off all HVAC systems, fans, and air conditioners that draw air from outside. Close all windows, doors, and fireplace dampers. Use towels, tape, or plastic sheeting to seal gaps in doors and windows if available.

If you were outside before reaching shelter: remove your outer clothing immediately. This eliminates up to 90% of radioactive contamination. Bag the clothing in plastic, seal it, and place it away from people. Shower with soap and water (no conditioner) or wipe exposed skin with a wet cloth if no shower is available.

Take stock of food and water in the building. Food and drinks that were inside before the detonation are safe to consume. Food in sealed containers that were outside is safe after wiping the outside of the container with a damp cloth. Do not eat or drink anything that was openly exposed outside.
Tap water that was already in pipes and water heaters inside the building is generally safe. Do not use outdoor water sources, open cisterns, or water that may have been contaminated by fallout.

Stay inside for a minimum of 24 hours. Radiation levels fall very rapidly — by 7x time increase, radiation drops 10x. After 24 hours, radiation from fallout has typically fallen to about 10% of its initial level. Depending on your location and proximity to the blast, officials may direct you to shelter for up to a month.

Monitor a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for official instructions. Cell, internet, and TV broadcast may not function. Follow official guidance on when to evacuate, where to go, and whether additional sheltering is needed.

If anyone develops symptoms of acute radiation syndrome — severe nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea within hours of possible exposure — note the time of onset and dose estimate if known, and seek medical care as soon as officials indicate it is safe to travel.
warnings: Shelter-in-place for nuclear fallout may last much longer than 24 hours — local officials may direct you to remain inside for several days to a month depending on the blast location and fallout pattern.
Radioactive fallout is invisible and odorless. The absence of visible dust or any physical sensation does not mean it is safe to go outside.
Opening doors or windows to check conditions, even briefly, introduces contaminated outside air and particles into your shelter.

Sheltering in a car, mobile home, or tent provides minimal protection compared to a solid building. Do not attempt to drive away from the area until officially directed — a moving car provides essentially no fallout protection.
Pets and service animals should also be brought inside and kept inside. Wipe down pets that were outdoors with a damp cloth.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave shelter to reunite with family members until official instructions say it is safe to travel. The radiation exposure risk during transit is significantly higher than staying sheltered.
Do not use HVAC, fans, or forced-air ventilation — these circulate outside contaminated air throughout the building.
Do not eat or drink food or water that was openly exposed outside after the detonation.
Do not use conditioner on hair when decontaminating — it binds radioactive particles to hair strands.
Do not evacuate on your own unless facing an immediate physical danger (fire, gas leak, structural collapse). Self-evacuation through a fallout zone dramatically increases radiation exposure.
Do not rely on social media or unverified information for guidance on when to leave — wait for official instructions.
redFlags: Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea beginning within hours of the blast — these are early symptoms of acute radiation syndrome. Note when symptoms started and seek medical care as soon as safely possible.
Skin redness, burning sensation, or blistering from thermal exposure that is worsening.
Fire, gas leak, structural damage, or flooding in your shelter that makes it unsafe to remain — these are the only valid reasons to leave before receiving official clearance.
Loss of consciousness or seizures in anyone who was exposed.
preparednessTips: Identify the most protected room in your home in advance — a basement or interior room with as much mass around it as possible. Know where you would go before an emergency.

Pre-position emergency supplies inside your home: water (1 gallon/person/day for 3+ days), sealed food, battery or hand-crank radio, first aid kit, medications, and extra plastic sheeting and duct tape.
Keep an emergency supply kit at your workplace too — you may not be able to return home for the first 24 hours.
Inform all family members where to shelter if a nuclear event occurs while they are at home and you are elsewhere. Have a pre-agreed family communication plan for after the shelter-in-place period.

guides.title: Nuclear Explosion: Immediate Response

guideData.title: Nuclear Explosion: Immediate Response
guideData.summary: A nuclear detonation requires fast, decisive action in the first minutes. Getting inside a solid building immediately and staying inside for 24 hours dramatically reduces radiation exposure from fallout — the most survivable threat for people outside the immediate blast zone.
guideData.whenToUse: A nuclear detonation has occurred nearby
guideData.preferredOption: Get inside the nearest solid building — preferably a basement or the center of a multi-story brick or concrete structure — within 10 minutes of the blast, then stay inside for at least 24 hours.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no solid building is accessible, any interior space (even a wood-frame house) is far safer than remaining outside. Use whatever shelter is reachable within the next few minutes.
steps: If outdoors when the flash occurs: immediately drop face-down on the ground with hands clasped behind your neck. Keep your face down and eyes closed until the shockwave passes (typically 30–60 seconds). The flash can cause temporary blindness — do not look at it.
After the shockwave passes: stand up and move toward the nearest solid building without delay. You have approximately 10 minutes before radioactive fallout begins to arrive.

Choose the best shelter you can reach within a few minutes: a basement is ideal; the center of a large multi-story brick or concrete building is excellent; underground garages and subway stations also provide good protection. Being inside any building is far safer than being outside — do not waste time seeking a 'perfect' shelter.

Inside a multi-story building: go to the basement or the center of a middle floor. Avoid the top floor (fallout accumulates on the roof) and the ground floor (fallout accumulates outside). Move away from all exterior walls and windows.

Seal your space: close all windows, doors, and fireplace dampers. Turn off fans, air conditioners, and forced-air heating that draw outside air in. Use towels, tape, or plastic sheeting to seal gaps if available.

If you were outside during or after the blast: remove and bag your outer clothing immediately — this eliminates roughly 80–90% of surface radioactive contamination. Seal the bag and place it away from people. Shower with soap and water (do not use conditioner), or wipe exposed skin and hair with a wet cloth if no shower is available.

Stay inside for a full 24 hours unless you are facing an immediate physical danger such as fire, active gas leak, building structural collapse, or a life-threatening injury. Radiation levels drop rapidly — leaving early dramatically increases your exposure.

Tune in to a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for official instructions. Cell service, internet, and TV may be disrupted. Only act on information from official sources — misinformation will spread rapidly.
warnings: Radioactive fallout is invisible and odorless — you will not be able to see, smell, or feel it arriving. Do not rely on your senses to judge when it is safe to go outside.
Radiation levels are highest in the first few hours after a detonation and decline rapidly. The instinct to leave and find family members can be fatal — wait for official instructions.
Cars and mobile homes do NOT provide meaningful protection from radioactive fallout. Do not remain in or shelter in a vehicle.

Potassium iodide (KI) tablets protect only against thyroid uptake of radioactive iodine. They do not protect against blast injury, fallout radiation exposure, or other radionuclides. Most people will not need KI — only use it if directed by public health authorities.
Hand sanitizer does not remove or neutralize radioactive material. Water and soap (or a wet cloth) are required for decontamination.
Fallout that arrives after the blast looks like dust, sand, or ash. Avoid touching it with bare hands and do not consume any food or water that was openly exposed outside.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay in or near a car — get out and into the nearest building immediately.

Do not drive toward family members in another location during the shelter-in-place period. The radiation exposure risk during transit outweighs the benefit of reunion. Pre-plan a family meeting point to use after the shelter-in-place period ends.
Do not look at the flash — it can cause temporary or permanent blindness.
Do not open windows or doors to check conditions outside during the shelter-in-place period.
Do not use conditioner when decontaminating hair — it binds radioactive particles to hair strands.
Do not eat or drink food or water that was openly exposed outside after the detonation.
Do not take KI tablets unless directed by public health authorities — they are not a general radiation antidote.
Do not rely on unofficial social media or internet sources for instructions — stick to official emergency broadcasts.
redFlags: You develop a severe headache, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea within hours of exposure — these may be early signs of acute radiation syndrome and require medical attention as soon as it is safe to travel.
Signs of skin burns, redness, or blistering from thermal exposure (from the initial flash) that are getting worse.
Visible fire, gas leak smell, or structural damage to your shelter that makes it unsafe to remain — these are the only reasons to leave shelter before receiving official clearance.
Loss of consciousness or seizures in anyone who was exposed during or after the blast.
preparednessTips: Identify the most protective room in your home or workplace in advance: a basement or an interior room surrounded by as much mass as possible.
Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank emergency radio in your emergency kit — cell and internet service may fail after a nuclear event.
Store a 3-day supply of water and food inside your home so you can shelter in place without needing to go outside.
Have a family emergency communication plan that includes a designated out-of-area contact and a pre-agreed meeting point for after a shelter-in-place period.
Keep heavy-duty garbage bags and duct tape in your emergency kit — these can be used to quickly seal gaps in doors and windows.
Discuss KI tablets with your doctor or local health department before an event — know whether they are recommended for your area and who should or should not take them.

guides.title: Purify Water with a Portable Filter

guideData.title: Purify Water with a Portable Filter
guideData.summary: Portable hollow fiber membrane filters (LifeStraw, Sawyer Squeeze) remove bacteria and protozoa from water without heat or chemicals. They do not remove viruses. In the US and Canada backcountry, the viral risk from natural water sources is generally low — but in flood water, international travel, or areas with sewage contamination, follow filtration with chemical treatment to cover viruses.
guideData.whenToUse: You cannot boil water and have a portable hollow fiber filter (LifeStraw, Sawyer Squeeze, or similar) available
guideData.preferredOption: Draw or pump source water through your portable hollow fiber filter directly into a clean container or your mouth per the filter's design. For LifeStraw personal filters, drink directly from the source through the filter. For Sawyer Squeeze, fill the included pouch, attach the filter, and squeeze filtered water out.
guideData.fallbackOption: If your filter is unavailable, frozen, or damaged, boil water if a heat source is available. If neither is possible, use chlorine dioxide tablets — they cover bacteria, viruses, and protozoa when used correctly.
steps: Inspect your filter before use. Check for cracks in the housing, any unusual odors coming from the output end, or signs of freeze damage (if the filter was stored in freezing conditions, assume it is compromised and do not rely on it).

If the water is visibly turbid (murky, brown, silty), pre-filter it through a clean cloth or coffee filter to remove large particles. While hollow fiber filters can handle some turbidity, coarse particles reduce the filter's lifespan and may slow flow to a trickle.
Fill your source water container (or use the filter's included pouch or bag) with untreated water. Do not let the output end of the filter touch untreated water or dirty surfaces.

Filter the water according to your product's method: drink through (LifeStraw personal), squeeze the pouch (Sawyer Squeeze), pump (pump filters), or hang and let gravity feed (gravity filters). Keep the clean-side output away from the dirty-side intake at all times.

If you are treating flood water, water from an area with sewage contamination, or water in an international setting where viruses in water are a known risk, treat the filtered water with chlorine dioxide tablets after filtering. Filtering removes the protozoa and most bacteria; the tablets kill remaining viruses.
After filtering, store treated water in a clean, covered container. Do not touch the output end of the filter to anything unclean.

After use, backflush the filter per the manufacturer's instructions (Sawyer Squeeze requires periodic backflushing with the included syringe to restore flow rate). Store the filter dry — do not store it wet in conditions where it could freeze.
warnings: Standard portable hollow fiber filters (0.1–0.2 micron pore size) do NOT remove viruses. Norovirus, hepatitis A, and other waterborne viruses pass through and can cause serious illness. In most US and Canadian backcountry settings this is acceptable (viral contamination of natural water is rare), but not in flood water, international travel, or sewage-contaminated sources.

A frozen filter is a failed filter. If hollow fiber membranes freeze, the fibers can crack and the filter will no longer block pathogens — and it will not look different or smell different. If your filter may have frozen, do not use it. Follow manufacturer instructions for cold-weather storage.
Filters do not remove chemical contamination — fuel, pesticides, heavy metals, or industrial chemicals. If water smells of fuel or chemicals, a filter will not make it safe to drink.

Older or poorly maintained filters may allow small amounts of Cryptosporidium to pass through due to manufacturing variation or wear. Follow filter replacement schedules and look for NSF/ANSI Standard 53 or 58 certification when purchasing.
whatNotToDo: Do not use a filter that has been frozen or that you suspect has been frozen — the hollow fiber membranes may have cracked silently. The filter will appear to work but may not protect you.
Do not let the clean output end of the filter touch untreated water, the rim of a dirty container, or your unwashed hands — this cross-contaminates the filter and defeats the purpose.

Do not skip post-filter chemical treatment in flood water, sewage-affected areas, or when traveling internationally in areas with poor sanitation. Filtering removes protozoa and bacteria but viruses remain.
Do not use a filter past its rated capacity without inspecting or replacing filter elements. Flow rate slowing significantly is a sign the filter is clogged — backflush or replace.
Do not store filters wet in freezing conditions. Drain and air-dry filters thoroughly before storing in cold weather.
redFlags: Water has an oily sheen, chemical smell, or comes from an area with industrial contamination — a filter will not make it safe. Do not drink it.
Your filter has been exposed to freezing temperatures — assume it is compromised unless the manufacturer has explicitly rated it for that temperature. Use an alternative purification method.
Filter output water has an unusual color, smell, or taste — something is wrong with the filter or the source water contains chemicals the filter cannot remove. Stop using it.

You are in a flood-affected area, near a sewage spill, or in an area with documented waterborne viral illness and you have only a hollow fiber filter — this filter alone is not sufficient. Add chemical treatment or find another source.
Vomiting, diarrhea, or fever appearing 1–3 days after drinking filtered water may indicate viral contamination the filter did not remove, or filter failure. Seek medical care.
preparednessTips: Keep a portable hollow fiber filter (LifeStraw, Sawyer Squeeze, or similar) in your go-bag or emergency kit. They are lightweight, long-lasting, and require no fuel or chemicals.
Look for NSF/ANSI Standard 53 (cyst reduction) or Standard 58 certification on the filter packaging when purchasing — this confirms independent testing against Cryptosporidium and Giardia.
Always store filters dry between uses. After each use, shake out excess water and allow the filter to air-dry completely before sealing in a bag or storage case.
In cold climates, keep your filter inside your sleeping bag or clothing layers during overnight camping to prevent freezing. Know the minimum operating temperature stated in your filter's manual.
Stock chlorine dioxide tablets alongside your filter so you can follow filtration with chemical treatment in any situation where viruses are a concern.

sourceReferences.title: About Choosing Home Water Filters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Defines filter pore size requirements for bacteria, protozoa, and virus removal; explains NSF/ANSI certification standards (53, 58); confirms hollow fiber filters do not remove viruses.

sourceReferences.title: About Water Treatment Options When Hiking, Camping, or Traveling
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Guidance on combining filtration with chemical treatment for full-spectrum protection; confirms most filters remove parasites but not viruses.

guides.title: Purify Water with Chemical Tablets

guideData.title: Purify Water with Chemical Tablets
guideData.summary: Chemical purification tablets — especially chlorine dioxide — can disinfect water when boiling is not possible. Chlorine dioxide kills the broadest range of pathogens including Cryptosporidium. Iodine tablets work for bacteria and viruses but not Cryptosporidium, and must not be used by pregnant people or those with thyroid conditions.
guideData.whenToUse: You cannot boil water — no heat source, fuel, or fire-safe container is available
guideData.preferredOption: Use chlorine dioxide (ClO₂) tablets — they are the only chemical tablet type that reliably kills Cryptosporidium. Add the number of tablets specified on the label per the volume of water, wait the full contact time stated on the label (typically several hours for clear water, longer for cold or turbid water), then drink.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you only have iodine tablets and the water source is unlikely to contain Cryptosporidium (not flood water, not surface water in high-risk areas), use iodine tablets — but never if you are pregnant, have thyroid disease, or have an iodine allergy. Wait at least 30 minutes for clear water before drinking.
steps: Look at the water. If it is visibly cloudy, murky, or has particles, pre-filter it first: pour it through a clean cloth, paper towel, or coffee filter into a clean container, or let it settle and pour off the clearer water from the top. Turbid water neutralizes disinfectants — skipping this step makes tablets far less effective.
Identify what tablet type you have: chlorine dioxide (ClO₂), iodine, or chlorine. Read the label now — different products have different dosages and wait times. Do not guess the dosage.

Add the correct number of tablets to your container of water per the label. For a standard 1-liter container, most products specify 1–2 tablets. Screw the lid on loosely or cover the container, then shake or stir to mix. Let a small amount of the treated water rinse the lip and threads of the container before tightening.

Wait the full contact time printed on the label before drinking. Chlorine dioxide tablets require several hours (typically 4 hours for clear water at room temperature; longer in cold water or turbid conditions). Iodine tablets require at least 30 minutes for clear water; double the time if water is cold or turbid.

After waiting, check for a faint disinfectant odor in the water — this is normal and indicates treatment is working. If there is no odor at all, the water may have neutralized the tablets; add a second dose and wait again.
Pour treated water into a clean, covered storage container. Do not let it sit uncovered — treated water has no residual protection against recontamination from containers, hands, or air.

Check the tablet package for expiration date and storage conditions. Expired or improperly stored tablets may not disinfect effectively. Chlorine dioxide tablets are sensitive to light and air — reseal packages immediately after use.
warnings: Chemical tablets are backup to boiling — they do not work as reliably as boiling, particularly against Cryptosporidium (iodine and chlorine tablets) and in turbid, cold, or chemically contaminated water.

Tablets do NOT remove chemical contamination (fuel, pesticides, heavy metals). If water smells of fuel or chemicals, or is from a flood in an industrial area, do not use tablets — the water is not safe to drink.
Chlorine dioxide provides no residual protection. Once treated water sits open or is transferred to a contaminated container, it can become contaminated again.

Cold water significantly reduces tablet effectiveness — most disinfection tables are based on room-temperature water (20°C / 68°F). If water is cold, extend contact time per the label or warm it before treating.
Iodine has limited shelf life once opened and can lose potency if stored improperly (heat, moisture, air exposure). Check expiration dates before relying on them in an emergency.
whatNotToDo: Do not use iodine tablets if you are pregnant, have thyroid disease, or have an allergy or hypersensitivity to iodine — it is physiologically active and can cause harm in these populations.
Do not use iodine tablets for more than a few weeks continuously without medical guidance. Long-term iodine ingestion can affect thyroid function.

Do not rely on iodine or chlorine tablets alone if Cryptosporidium is a known risk (flood water, surface water in outbreak areas) — these tablet types do not reliably kill Crypto. Use chlorine dioxide instead.
Do not skip the pre-filtering step for turbid water. Turbidity physically blocks disinfectants from reaching pathogens — tablets will not compensate for it.
Do not use expired tablets or tablets that have been exposed to air, heat, or moisture for extended periods. Their potency may be insufficient to disinfect safely.
Do not shorten the contact time. The wait time on the label is the minimum for the treatment to work — drinking early means the water may still contain active pathogens.
redFlags: Water has an oily sheen, chemical smell, or comes from an area with known industrial or fuel contamination — tablets will not make it safe. Do not drink it.
You are pregnant, have thyroid disease, or have an iodine allergy and only have iodine tablets available — seek another purification method.

Vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, or fever appearing 1–3 days after drinking tablet-treated water may indicate treatment failure or Cryptosporidium infection (if iodine/chlorine tablets were used). Seek medical care.
No faint disinfectant odor after the full wait time — the tablets may have been neutralized by turbidity or were expired. Re-treat with a fresh dose and wait again.
Symptoms of waterborne illness in infants, young children, pregnant people, or immunocompromised individuals after drinking treated water require immediate medical attention.
preparednessTips: Stock chlorine dioxide tablets (such as Aquatabs or Potable Aqua CIO₂) in your emergency kit and go-bag — they offer the broadest pathogen coverage of any tablet type, including Cryptosporidium.
Keep tablets sealed in their original packaging in a cool, dark, dry place. Check expiration dates annually and replace as needed.
Include a bandana or pre-cut coffee filters in your kit for pre-filtering turbid water before tablet treatment.
Practice reading and following a tablet label before an emergency so you know the dosage and wait times for your specific product.
Consider stocking multiple tablet types (both chlorine dioxide and iodine) to have a backup if one type is depleted or unavailable.

guides.title: Disinfect Water with Bleach

guideData.title: Disinfect Water with Bleach
guideData.summary: If you cannot boil water, emergency disinfection with the correct unscented household bleach and dosage may be a backup option.
guideData.whenToUse: Use only when boiling is not possible and you have the right unscented bleach and official dosage guidance for the strength.
guideData.preferredOption: Use official emergency disinfection instructions exactly, including product type, amount, mixing, and wait time.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are unsure of the bleach type or strength, use a different safer source or treatment method rather than guessing.
steps: Pre-filter cloudy water if possible.
Check that the bleach is unscented and appropriate for emergency water disinfection.
Follow official dosage guidance for the bleach strength and water amount.
Mix well and wait the recommended time before use.
warnings: Wrong bleach type or wrong amount can be unsafe.
Bleach treatment does not remove chemicals or fuel contamination.
whatNotToDo: Do not use scented or specialty bleach products.
Do not mix bleach with other cleaners.
redFlags: Signs of waterborne illness after consuming bleach-treated water.
Bleach concentration unknown or container lacks clear labeling.
