guides.title: Purify Water by Boiling

guideData.title: Purify Water by Boiling
guideData.summary: Boiling is the most reliable way to kill bacteria, viruses, and parasites in water during an emergency. Bring water to a full rolling boil for at least 1 minute — or 3 minutes above 5,000 feet elevation — then cool and store it covered.
guideData.whenToUse: You do not have access to safe tap water and have a heat source available
guideData.preferredOption: Bring water to a full rolling boil — large bubbles continuously breaking the surface — for 1 minute (3 minutes above 5,000 ft / 1,500 m). Cool in the same container, then store covered in a clean vessel.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot boil water, treat with unscented household bleach or chemical purification tablets. See the 'Disinfect Water with Bleach' guide.
steps: If the water is visibly cloudy or has particles, let it settle for a few minutes, then pour it through a clean cloth, paper towel, or coffee filter into your boiling pot. Sediment reduces treatment effectiveness.
Use a metal pot, heat-safe glass container, or purpose-designed camping vessel. Do not boil in thin plastic bottles or single-use plastic containers — heat can cause them to leach chemicals.
Heat the water until you see large bubbles continuously rising and breaking the surface. This is a rolling boil — not just steam or small bubbles forming at the bottom.

Once at a rolling boil, start timing: boil for at least 1 minute at elevations below 5,000 feet (1,500 meters). If you are above 5,000 feet, boil for at least 3 minutes. If unsure of your elevation, boil for 3 minutes.
Turn off the heat and leave the water in the same pot to cool naturally. Do not add ice or cold water to speed cooling — ice may be contaminated and could undo the treatment.

Once cool, pour into a clean storage container with a tight-fitting lid. If possible, sanitize the container first: wash with soap, rinse, swirl 1 teaspoon of unscented bleach per quart of water inside, then rinse and air-dry.
Label the container with the date and time it was boiled. Use boiled water within 24 hours for drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, and wound cleaning.
warnings: Boiling does NOT remove chemical contaminants: fuel, pesticides, heavy metals (lead, arsenic), PFAS ('forever chemicals'), nitrates, or radioactive materials. If water smells like fuel, has an oily sheen, or is from a chemically contaminated source, boiling is the wrong response — it can actually concentrate dissolved chemicals.

If authorities have issued a do-not-drink advisory specifically for chemical or heavy metal contamination (not just biological), boiling will not fix it. Do not drink the water — find a safe alternative.
Prolonged or repeated boiling of chemically contaminated water can make it more dangerous by evaporating the water and concentrating the contaminants in what remains.
Hot water handling can cause serious burns. Keep children away from the heat source and carry hot water carefully.
whatNotToDo: Do not boil in thin plastic bottles, single-use containers, or any container not rated for heat — they may leach chemicals into the water.
Do not add ice to cool boiled water — ice from an unknown source may be contaminated.
Do not mix treated (boiled) water back into untreated water in the same container.
Do not rely on boiling to fix chemical, fuel, or heavy metal contamination. Boiling only treats biological hazards.
Do not boil water that smells of fuel or chemicals — it will not become safe and the fumes can be hazardous.
redFlags: Water has an oily sheen, strong chemical smell, or unusual color — do not boil this water for drinking. Chemical or fuel contamination cannot be removed by boiling.
A do-not-drink advisory is in effect for chemical or heavy metal contamination — boiling is not sufficient.
Vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, or fever appearing 1–3 days after drinking boiled water may indicate post-boil contamination (unclean container, recontamination) or a chemical source issue.
Boiled water stored uncovered or for more than 24 hours should be re-boiled before use.
Symptoms of waterborne illness in children, infants, pregnant people, or immunocompromised individuals warrant immediate medical attention.
preparednessTips: Keep a metal pot or camp kettle in your emergency supplies specifically for water treatment.
Know your approximate elevation — if you live or travel above 5,000 feet, default to 3-minute boil times.
Store extra fuel (camp stove canisters, propane) for extended water treatment needs.
Pre-designate a clean container with a tight lid for storing boiled water. Label it clearly.
Keep unscented household bleach in your emergency kit as a no-heat backup for when boiling isn't possible.

guides.title: During a Landslide

guideData.title: During a Landslide
guideData.summary: Immediate actions to take when a landslide is actively occurring or imminent, whether you are indoors, outdoors, or in a vehicle.
guideData.whenToUse: A landslide is actively occurring near your location
guideData.preferredOption: If indoors, get under a sturdy desk or table, move to the downhill side of the building, and stay inside until the slide passes.
guideData.fallbackOption: If outdoors, run to the left or right — perpendicular to the slide path. Do not attempt to outrun the slide uphill or downhill.
steps: If you have been told to evacuate or feel it is unsafe to remain, leave immediately — save yourself, not your belongings.
If indoors in a single-story building, get under a sturdy piece of furniture such as a desk or table and hold on.
If a second story is available, go upstairs to put distance between you and the ground-level debris.
Move to the downhill side of the building — the landslide could push through windows and walls on the uphill side.
Do NOT evacuate the building during an active landslide — the moving debris can carry you away. Stay inside until it passes.
If outdoors, run to the right or left away from the path of the slide. Do not try to go uphill or downhill — landslides travel downhill much faster than a person can run.
After the slide stops, do not assume it is safe — landslides may occur in pulses and the first slide may not be the last.
Check for injured and trapped persons near the slide area but do not enter the slide zone itself. Direct rescuers to their locations.
Report broken utility lines and damaged roadways to authorities so utilities can be shut off quickly, preventing further hazards.
warnings: Landslides may occur in pulses — the first slide may not be the last. Do not assume it is safe after the initial slide.
If near a wildfire burn area, pay special attention during rain — burn scars are extremely susceptible to debris flows.
A sudden change in stream water levels, especially during or right after an intense storm, may indicate upstream landslide activity.
The landslide can push through windows and walls on the uphill side of a building — stay on the downhill side.
whatNotToDo: Do not evacuate a building during an active landslide — the moving debris can carry you away.
Do not try to outrun a landslide uphill or downhill — move to the side instead.
Do not enter the slide zone after the slide to search for people — direct professional rescuers to their locations instead.
Do not delay evacuation if told to leave before a slide — save yourself, not your belongings.
redFlags: Unusual sounds such as trees cracking, boulders knocking together, or a roaring sound like a freight train indicate moving debris.
New cracks or unusual bulges in the ground, sidewalks, or pavement are signs of active ground movement.
Springs, seeps, or saturated ground appearing in areas that have not typically been wet before.
A sudden rise or fall in stream or creek water levels, especially during or right after a storm.
Tilting trees, retaining walls shifting, or doors and windows that suddenly stick or jam.
preparednessTips: Learn the landslide risk for your area by checking USGS landslide hazard maps.
Identify evacuation routes that lead away from hillsides and steep terrain.
During prolonged heavy rain, stay alert for the warning signs listed in this guide's red flags.
If you live on or near a slope, know where the downhill side of your building is relative to the hillside.

guides.title: Keeping Children, Elderly, and Frail Adults Warm Safely
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat

guideData.title: Keeping Children, Elderly, and Frail Adults Warm Safely
guideData.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guideData.summary: Keep vulnerable people in the warmest room, use safe layers and blankets, check them often, and avoid unsafe heating methods.
guideData.whenToUse: Cold homes, power outages, or outdoor delays involving infants, older adults, or people with illness or disability.
guideData.preferredOption: Move vulnerable people to the warmest sealed room, keep them off the floor, and monitor for early signs of hypothermia.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot keep them safely warm, relocate them to a friend’s home, healthcare facility, or official shelter if possible.
steps: Move vulnerable people to the warmest, best-sealed room and keep them off the floor.
Dress them in multiple layers and cover head, hands, and feet.
Offer warm drinks if they are awake and can swallow safely.
Check for unusual fatigue, confusion, slurred speech, or very cold skin.
Keep heaters and open flames away from bedding and clothing.
warnings: Children, older adults, and people with chronic illness can develop hypothermia faster and may not report symptoms clearly.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on hot water bottles, electric blankets, or heaters directly against skin for people who cannot move away or feel burns.
Do not use unsafe fuel-burning heaters indoors.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, extreme sleepiness, or bluish/pale very cold skin.
preparednessTips: Plan in advance where vulnerable household members could stay if your home becomes too cold.
Ensure smoke and CO alarms work and have backup power.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Notes higher-risk groups and protective steps.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety: Get Ready for Cooler Weather
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes safe heating and alarms.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Warns against indoor fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Layering Clothing and Bedding for Maximum Warmth
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat

guideData.title: Layering Clothing and Bedding for Maximum Warmth
guideData.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guideData.summary: Use several loose, dry layers and insulate underneath as well as on top to retain heat.
guideData.whenToUse: Any cold-weather situation indoors or outdoors, especially overnight.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a moisture-wicking inner layer, insulating middle layers, and an outer layer that blocks wind or moisture.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack ideal gear, improvise with multiple dry shirts, pants, blankets, and good coverage of head, neck, hands, and feet.
steps: Put on a thin moisture-wicking base layer if available.
Add one or more insulating layers such as fleece or wool.
Use an outer layer that blocks wind and sheds precipitation when outdoors.
For sleeping, put insulation under you and then cover with blankets or sleeping bags on top.
Adjust layers to avoid sweating and change out damp layers as soon as possible.
warnings: Cotton holds moisture and can make you colder when wet in outdoor settings.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore wet socks, gloves, or base layers.
redFlags: Shivering, clumsiness, or confusion that continue after adding layers and shelter.
preparednessTips: Keep at least one non-cotton base layer, warm hat, gloves, and extra socks per person in emergency kits.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear layering and fabric guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Recommends appropriate winter clothing and layering.

guides.title: Staying Warm in a Cold Home Without Power
guides.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat

guideData.title: Staying Warm in a Cold Home Without Power
guideData.parentTopic: Staying Warm Without Power or Heat
guideData.summary: Gather everyone in one small room, block drafts, insulate floors and windows, and use safe blankets and clothing—not fuel devices—to stay warm.
guideData.whenToUse: Any winter power outage or heating-system failure when indoor temperatures are dropping.
guideData.preferredOption: Shrink your living space, insulate yourself and the room, and use body heat, layers, and safe sleeping setups.
guideData.fallbackOption: If your home cannot be kept reasonably warm, move to a safer location such as a friend’s house, public shelter, or warming center if travel is safe.
steps: Choose a small room and close off unused areas.
Cover windows, close curtains, and block drafts under doors.
Insulate the floor where you sit and sleep with rugs, mats, cardboard, or cushions.
Wear multiple layers including hat, scarf, gloves, and warm socks; change out of damp clothes.
Eat regular meals and drink fluids; check vulnerable household members often.
warnings: Cold homes can lead to hypothermia, especially in infants, older adults, and people with chronic illness.
whatNotToDo: Do not use ovens, stovetops, camp stoves, charcoal grills, or generators indoors for heat.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, drowsiness, or very cold skin.
Headache, dizziness, or nausea while a fuel-burning device is nearby (possible CO exposure).
preparednessTips: Keep extra blankets, warm clothing, and a battery or crank radio.
Know where local warming centers are announced.

sourceReferences.title: Winter at Home – Staying Warm Without Power
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical actions for staying warm indoors during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Guidance on staying warm and avoiding cold-related illness.

guides.title: Shelter Building

guideData.title: Shelter Building
guideData.summary: Get off wet ground, reduce wind exposure, and create simple overhead cover with whatever materials you have.
guideData.whenToUse: When stranded outdoors for many hours or overnight without a tent, especially in cold, wet, or windy conditions.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a tarp or plastic sheet with cord to make a low, wind-sheltered lean-to or A-frame near a natural windbreak.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no sheet or tarp, use branches, leaves, and other natural materials to build a simple debris shelter that blocks wind and insulates you from the ground.
steps: Choose a spot above drainage channels and away from falling branches or rockfall.
Use trees, rocks, or trekking poles as supports for a tarp or sheet; pitch it low and tight with the open side away from wind.
Build a thick insulating layer under you using dry leaves, pine boughs, packs, or spare clothing.
Keep the shelter small so body heat can warm the space more effectively.
Keep your core and head insulated and change out of wet clothes if you have dry layers.
warnings: Improvised shelters offer limited protection; hypothermia is still possible in wet, windy, or very cold conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not build in dry riverbeds, on ridge tops in storms, or under dead branches (“widow-makers”).
redFlags: Numb hands or feet progressing to clumsiness or confusion.
Inability to stop shivering after shelter and dry layers.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one emergency tarp or space blanket, cord, and a foam pad in outdoor kits.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes shelter, insulation, and wind protection.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Describes tarps designed for temporary human shelter and emergency roof sheeting.

guides.title: Simple No-Tools Emergency Shelter Inside Buildings

guideData.title: Simple No-Tools Emergency Shelter Inside Buildings
guideData.summary: Close off one small interior room, block drafts, and build a warm “nest” with layers above and below you.
guideData.whenToUse: During power outages or heating failure when you can remain inside a building.
guideData.preferredOption: Use one small room, seal drafts, insulate the floor, and keep everyone in layered clothing and blankets.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot dedicate a room, create a smaller warm corner with furniture, mattresses, and blankets.
steps: Move everyone into one small room away from exterior doors if possible.
Close doors, blinds, and curtains; block drafts under doors and leaky windows.
Hang blankets, tarps, or plastic sheeting to reduce the space you need to warm.
Put insulation on the floor using mats, cardboard, cushions, or folded clothing.
Wear hats, socks, and layers; share blankets and stay fed and hydrated if safe water is available.
warnings: People can become hypothermic indoors if they are wet, underdressed, or immobile in very cold rooms.
whatNotToDo: Do not use ovens, grills, camp stoves, generators, or unvented fuel devices indoors for heat.
redFlags: Uncontrollable shivering, slurred speech, confusion, or drowsiness.
Indoor temperatures near freezing with no safe heat option.
preparednessTips: Keep extra blankets, foam pads, hats, gloves, and duct tape accessible to quickly warm one room.

sourceReferences.title: Winter at Home – Staying Warm Without Power
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concrete actions for staying warm indoors during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Home preparation and cold-health risk guidance.

guides.title: Choosing a Safe Spot for Emergency Shelter

guideData.title: Choosing a Safe Spot for Emergency Shelter
guideData.summary: Pick the safest dry, stable shelter you can reach, away from flood, fire, wind, and falling-debris hazards.
guideData.whenToUse: When you must stay put for hours or overnight because of storms, cold, or being stranded.
guideData.preferredOption: Choose a solid building first, using a small interior room or protected corner above ground level and away from obvious hazards.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no building is safe, use a vehicle or natural windbreak while avoiding flood channels, unstable slopes, and dead branches.
steps: Scan for the nearest intact shelter or protected spot.
Avoid low areas, riverbanks, rockfall zones, and avalanche or landslide paths.
Choose a small area out of wind and away from broken glass or loose debris.
Get off bare ground with pads, packs, cardboard, or spare clothing.
Keep exits clear and plan how to leave quickly if conditions worsen.
warnings: Damaged buildings after storms or earthquakes may collapse without warning.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter in basements or low spots if flood risk exists.
Do not shelter under trees in severe wind or lightning.
redFlags: Cracking sounds, sagging roof, or new wall cracks.
Rising water inside the shelter area.
Strong gas smell or smoke.
preparednessTips: Know nearby public shelters or warming centers in advance.
Keep a small grab kit with hat, gloves, light, snacks, and water.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health guidance on safe sheltering and cold-weather preparation.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical outdoor shelter and exposure-reduction advice.

guides.title: Apartment and Multi-Unit Housing Generator Safety

guideData.title: Apartment and Multi-Unit Housing Generator Safety
guideData.summary: Portable generators are usually unsafe and often prohibited for balconies, hallways, stairwells, or shared indoor spaces; use non-fuel alternatives or official building plans instead.
guideData.whenToUse: If you live in an apartment or multi-unit building and are considering generator use during an outage.
guideData.preferredOption: Follow building rules and local codes and avoid portable fuel generators in or near the building.
guideData.fallbackOption: If backup power is essential, rely on battery systems, community charging, or building-managed safe generator setups rather than personal balcony or hallway use.
steps: Check building rules and local fire code about generators.
Do not use generators on balconies, roof decks, hallways, garages, or near windows and doors.
Prioritize battery-powered lights, power banks, and approved charging areas managed by the building or community.
If outages regularly threaten your health, discuss long-term backup options or relocation planning with building management.
warnings: CO from a balcony or nearby generator can enter your unit and neighboring units.
Fire risk is higher in crowded multi-unit buildings.
whatNotToDo: Do not run generators in apartments, stairwells, parking garages, or attached carports.
Do not run cords through doors or windows in ways that keep them from closing safely in storms.
redFlags: CO alarm sounding anywhere in the building.
Exhaust smell inside apartments.
Unsafe generator use in a location that threatens multiple units.
preparednessTips: Store multiple power banks and USB-rechargeable lights.
Know community charging centers or shelters in your area.
Encourage building management to create a safe centralized backup-power plan if appropriate.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide and Generator Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear combined generator and CO safety guidance for household use.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide Safety in Power Outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: State emergency guidance on CO and generator placement.

guides.title: Powering Appliances Safely with a Generator

guideData.title: Powering Appliances Safely with a Generator
guideData.summary: Plug only essential appliances directly into the generator with proper cords; never connect a portable generator to home wiring unless a qualified electrician installed a transfer switch.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you plan to connect appliances or tools to a portable generator at home.
guideData.preferredOption: Power only essential loads and connect them directly to the generator using heavy-duty outdoor-rated cords.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are unsure about load or connections, limit use to a few low-power devices until you can confirm ratings or get professional help.
steps: List the essential appliances you need and check their wattage.
Keep total load below the generator’s rated output.
Use one heavy-duty outdoor cord per major appliance when possible.
Start the generator, then connect appliances one by one.
Unplug appliances first before shutting the generator down.
If your home has a professionally installed transfer switch, follow the electrician’s and manufacturer’s instructions.
warnings: Backfeeding power into home wiring can electrocute utility workers and neighbors and damage equipment.
Overloading a generator can overheat cords, outlets, and appliances.
whatNotToDo: Do not connect a generator to a home outlet with a homemade or backfeed cord.
Do not overload the generator.
Do not run sensitive electronics without surge protection if possible.
redFlags: Tingling, shock, or burning near appliances or cords.
Repeated breaker trips, visible sparks, or smoke from cords or appliances.
Any fire involving the generator, cords, or connected devices.
preparednessTips: Have a licensed electrician install and label any transfer switch.
Keep a written list of the appliances or circuits safe to run from the generator.
Store appropriate cords and surge protection with the generator.

sourceReferences.title: During a Power Outage
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Federal guidance on safe behavior during outages, surge protection, and communication.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide and Generator Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear combined generator and CO safety guidance for household use.

guides.title: Safe Placement and Operation of Portable Generators

guideData.title: Safe Placement and Operation of Portable Generators
guideData.summary: Always use generators outdoors at least 6 m / 20 ft from doors, windows, and vents, with exhaust pointing away; never run them indoors or in garages.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you plan to start, place, or move a portable generator during or after an outage.
guideData.preferredOption: Place the generator outside in a dry, ventilated location well away from openings, and follow the owner’s manual exactly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot place the generator safely outdoors far from openings, do not use it.
steps: Read and follow the owner’s manual and safety labels.
Place the generator outdoors on a firm, dry surface more than 20 ft from openings with exhaust directed away from people and buildings.
Protect it from rain or snow only with a cover designed for safe ventilation.
Plug appliances directly into generator outlets or use properly rated outdoor cords.
Turn it off and let it cool before refueling.
warnings: Generator exhaust contains carbon monoxide that can kill in minutes.
CO can build up in enclosed or partially enclosed spaces even with doors or windows open.
whatNotToDo: Do not use a generator inside homes, garages, basements, crawlspaces, sheds, or near windows and vents.
Do not run a generator on balconies, in stairwells, or near apartment openings.
Do not refuel while it is running or hot.
redFlags: Anyone nearby develops headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, or loss of consciousness.
CO alarm sounds.
Sparks, smoke, or unusual generator noises.
preparednessTips: Decide in advance where you will place a generator safely.
Install and test CO detectors.
Store fuel safely in approved containers away from living spaces and heat sources.

guides.title: Preventing CO Poisoning During Outages

guideData.title: Preventing CO Poisoning During Outages
guideData.summary: Never use fuel-burning devices indoors or in attached structures; keep generators, grills, and vehicles outside and far from doors, windows, and vents.
guideData.whenToUse: During power outages when people may turn to alternative heating, cooking, or power sources.
guideData.preferredOption: Use only properly vented fuel appliances and keep outdoor devices well away from buildings.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot use a device safely outdoors and vented, do not use it; rely on batteries, blankets, clothing, and shelters instead.
steps: Place generators outdoors at least 6 m / 20 ft from any opening.
Never use charcoal or gas grills, camp stoves, or unvented heaters indoors or in garages.
Do not run vehicles in garages, even with doors open.
Clear snow, ice, or debris from vents on approved fuel appliances.
Install and test CO detectors before storm season.
warnings: Improper use of generators and grills is a major cause of CO deaths after storms and outages.
whatNotToDo: Do not heat your home with a gas oven, stovetop, grill, or camp stove.
Do not sleep in a room where a fuel-burning space heater is running unless it is properly vented and approved.
redFlags: Any CO alarm activation.
Any CO symptoms while fuel-burning devices are in use.
preparednessTips: Plan non-fuel backup heating such as extra blankets and warm clothing.
Teach all household members what devices are never safe indoors.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear combined generator and CO safety guidance for household use.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: State emergency guidance on CO and generator placement.

guides.title: Carbon Monoxide Basics and Symptoms

guideData.title: Carbon Monoxide Basics and Symptoms
guideData.summary: Carbon monoxide is an odorless, colorless gas from fuel-burning devices that can kill quickly; early symptoms include headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, and confusion.
guideData.whenToUse: To understand CO risk whenever generators, heaters, stoves, grills, or vehicles may be used near buildings.
guideData.preferredOption: Prevent exposure by keeping fuel-burning devices outside or properly vented and by installing working CO detectors.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you suspect CO exposure, get everyone into fresh air immediately and call emergency services.
steps: Learn the common CO sources in homes and outage situations.
Recognize early symptoms such as headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, and confusion.
Treat a cluster of these symptoms around fuel appliances as a possible CO emergency.
Remember that sleeping or intoxicated people may not notice symptoms before they collapse.
warnings: CO cannot be seen, smelled, or tasted.
CO poisoning can be fatal within minutes at high levels.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore mild symptoms shared by multiple people in a home with fuel devices running.
Do not assume opening a window is adequate protection when a device is being used unsafely.
redFlags: CO alarm sounds.
Multiple people have sudden flu-like symptoms without fever.
Unconsciousness, severe confusion, or chest pain near fuel appliances.
preparednessTips: Install battery-powered or battery-backup CO detectors on every level and near sleeping areas.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries at least yearly.
Have fuel-burning appliances inspected and serviced regularly.

guides.title: Dust, Ash, and Airborne Irritants After Storms, Eruptions, or Collapses
guides.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash

guideData.title: Dust, Ash, and Airborne Irritants After Storms, Eruptions, or Collapses
guideData.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash
guideData.summary: Avoid breathing dust and ash, use appropriate masks if available, stay upwind, and clean up gently with moisture to avoid re-suspending particles.
guideData.whenToUse: After dust storms, volcanic ashfall, building collapses, or wildfires when particles cover surfaces or air quality is poor.
guideData.preferredOption: Limit outdoor time, especially for sensitive people, use fitted respiratory protection when cleaning, and keep children away from heavy dust.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no respirator is available, cover nose and mouth with a damp cloth or scarf and avoid vigorous cleanup that kicks up dust.
steps: Stay indoors as much as possible while dust or ash levels are high and close windows and doors.
If you must go outside or clean up, use a well-fitting respirator rated for fine particles if available.
Keep children, older adults, and people with lung or heart disease away from heavy dust and ash.
Lightly mist dusty surfaces and clean gently into sealed bags rather than dry sweeping.
Wash exposed skin and change clothes after cleanup.
warnings: Some dusts and ashes can contain toxic substances and fine particles that harm lungs and other organs.
whatNotToDo: Do not use leaf blowers or aggressive dry sweeping.
Do not allow children to play in ash or dust piles.
redFlags: Sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, severe coughing, or eye injury after dust or ash exposure.
preparednessTips: Store appropriate masks and eye protection if you live in dust-prone or volcanic regions.
Add local hazard-specific modules where relevant.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Smoke: A Guide for Public Health Officials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Cleanup and exposure reduction concepts for airborne particles.

sourceReferences.title: Environmental and Health Impacts of Air Pollution: A Review
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health effects of particulate matter and dust.

guides.title: Using AQI and Basic Smoke/Air Quality Precautions
guides.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash

guideData.title: Using AQI and Basic Smoke/Air Quality Precautions
guideData.parentTopic: Air Quality, Smoke, Dust, and Ash
guideData.summary: Check AQI and reduce outdoor exertion, close windows, and protect sensitive groups first as air quality worsens.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever the air looks hazy, smells smoky or dirty, or authorities issue air-quality or wildfire-smoke alerts.
guideData.preferredOption: Use AQI or local equivalent to guide behavior and reduce time and effort outdoors as conditions worsen.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot check AQI, treat visible smoke or strong odors as unhealthy and stay indoors with windows closed.
steps: Check AQI through official sources like AirNow or local agencies when possible.
If AQI is unhealthy for sensitive groups or worse, reduce prolonged or heavy outdoor exertion, especially for children, older adults, and people with heart or lung disease.
If AQI is unhealthy or higher, stay indoors as much as possible and use a portable air cleaner if available.
Avoid adding indoor pollution such as smoking, candles, frying, or vacuuming without HEPA filtration.
Follow local guidance on respirators such as N95-type masks if you must be outdoors in heavy smoke.
warnings: Poor air quality can worsen asthma, COPD, heart disease, and can trigger serious cardiorespiratory events.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on simple paper or cloth masks for fine smoke particles.
Do not exercise heavily outdoors during high smoke or pollution events.
redFlags: Difficulty breathing, chest pain, severe coughing, wheezing not relieved by usual medications, or confusion after smoke exposure.
preparednessTips: Users with asthma or heart disease should keep rescue medications accessible and follow clinician action plans.
Include AQI color explanations and offline behavior guidance in the app.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed AQI-linked public advice.

sourceReferences.title: Environmental and Health Impacts of Air Pollution: A Review

guides.title: Returning After Wildfire

guideData.title: Returning After Wildfire
guideData.summary: Return only when authorities say it is safe, and watch for ash, unstable structures, utility damage, and lingering smoke.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after evacuation or smoke sheltering once officials begin allowing re-entry.
guideData.preferredOption: Wait for official clearance and return with protective gear, water, phone power, and basic supplies.
steps: Confirm re-entry is allowed.
Wear sturdy shoes, gloves, and a respirator if ash or smoke remain heavy.
Check for hot spots, unstable trees, utility damage, and structural hazards.
Use caution around ash, debris, and contaminated water or food.
warnings: Ash and damaged utilities can still harm you after the flames have passed.
whatNotToDo: Do not turn on damaged gas or electrical systems casually.
Do not let children play in ash or debris.
redFlags: Gas smell, active hot spots, unstable structures, or heavy persistent smoke need professional help.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Evacuation Outreach Materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical evacuation planning and public action checklists.

sourceReferences.title: How Wildfire Smoke Affects Your Body
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Health risks and high-risk group guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Public Health Advice During Wildfires
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-health smoke and safety guidance.

guides.title: Wildfire Smoke: Protect Your Air

guideData.title: Wildfire Smoke: Protect Your Air
guideData.summary: Stay inside with windows and doors closed, create one cleaner-air room if possible, and avoid adding indoor pollution.
guideData.whenToUse: During wildfire smoke events when outdoor air is visibly smoky or AQI is unhealthy or worse and evacuation is not currently ordered.
guideData.preferredOption: Create a cleaner-air room at home, school, or work and limit outdoor time and exertion.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no filter or cleaner, close windows and doors, seal obvious gaps, and avoid indoor particle-generating activities.
steps: Choose a room that can be closed off and is large enough for people to stay in.
Close windows and doors and seal obvious drafts if safe and temporary.
Use a portable HEPA air cleaner or approved box-fan filter setup if available and safe.
Set HVAC systems to recirculate indoor air if possible.
Avoid indoor activities that add particles such as smoking, frying, or vacuuming without HEPA filtration.
Leave for cleaner air elsewhere if the home remains very smoky or high-risk people become symptomatic.
warnings: Indoor air can quickly become unhealthy if windows remain open during smoke events or indoor particle sources are added.
whatNotToDo: Do not run systems that pull unfiltered outdoor air in during heavy smoke unless you have specific local guidance.
Do not use unvented gas, propane, or charcoal heaters indoors.
redFlags: Severe breathing difficulty, chest pain, or signs of heart attack or stroke during smoke events.
preparednessTips: Identify a cleaner-air room and obtain filters before wildfire season.
Build a checklist for assembling a basic cleaner-air space.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire Smoke: A Guide for Public Health Officials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Indoor protection strategies and cleaner-air rooms.

guides.title: Wildfire Evacuation: Leave Early

guideData.title: Wildfire Evacuation: Leave Early
guideData.summary: Leave early when told to evacuate or when conditions are becoming unsafe. Do not wait until roads are blocked or smoke is overwhelming.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when evacuation orders are issued, fire is approaching, or authorities say conditions are unsafe.
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate promptly using planned routes.
guideData.fallbackOption: If evacuation is temporarily impossible, move to the safest available cleaner-air or official shelter location and monitor alerts continuously.
steps: Follow local alerts.
Load go-bags, medications, and pet supplies.
Back the car in if you have time and keep the tank sufficiently fueled.
Drive with windows up and ventilation recirculating if smoky.
warnings: Fires can spread rapidly and block roads.
Smoke can reduce visibility and impair breathing even ahead of flames.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until the last minute.
Do not return until officials say it is safe.
redFlags: If fire is approaching, roads are cut off, or someone has severe breathing trouble or burns, seek emergency help immediately.
preparednessTips: Maintain mapped evacuation routes.
Prepare pet plans and a ready go-bag before wildfire season.

guides.title: Distress Signaling

guideData.title: Distress Signaling
guideData.summary: Methods to signal for rescue and help.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: Overuse of signaling devices can exhaust batteries needed for longer emergencies.
False signals or uncontrolled fires can divert rescuers from genuine emergencies.
redFlags: Any worsening of injury, illness, or exposure while awaiting rescue.
Signals go unanswered for an extended period with no evidence of a search underway.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NPS guidance on what to do when lost outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical guidance on staying found and staying put once lost.

guides.title: Ground-to-Air Symbols for Aircraft

guideData.title: Ground-to-Air Symbols for Aircraft
guideData.summary: Use large, high-contrast symbols such as V for assistance and X for medical help so aircraft can understand your situation from above.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when aircraft search is likely or you can hear or see aircraft overhead and have access to an open area visible from above.
guideData.preferredOption: Build one or more standardized symbols using high-contrast materials in as open and flat an area as possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot build standard symbols, create a large obvious X, SOS, or directional arrow that clearly marks your position.
steps: Pick the flattest, most open safe area available.
Use bright or strongly contrasting materials like tarps, clothing, branches, or rocks.
Make symbols large enough to be visible from above.
Place yourself near the symbol but do not obstruct it.
Keep the symbol stable unless your situation changes.
warnings: Small or low-contrast symbols may be invisible from search altitude.
These symbols may guide searchers to you but do not guarantee a landing zone.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep changing symbols without reason.
Do not make messy or ambiguous shapes that could look like debris.
redFlags: You have an injured person needing medical help and aircraft are not responding; increase other signals if safe.
Weather has closed in so aircraft are unlikely to fly; shift focus to shelter and ground-based signaling.
preparednessTips: Learn the basic ICAO/NATO meanings for V, X, Y, N, and directional arrows.
Carry a high-visibility tarp or blanket that can help form symbols.

sourceReferences.title: Ground-air visual signal codes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary standard for V, X, Y, N, and directional symbols.

sourceReferences.title: Air-surface communication training materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for practical visibility and response context.

guides.title: Maximizing Rescue Visibility (Land)

guideData.title: Maximizing Rescue Visibility (Land)
guideData.summary: Combine contrast, motion, light, and sound in one place so you stand out from the landscape to both air and ground searchers.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you are waiting for rescue on land after calling for help or activating a beacon, or when you suspect a search is underway.
guideData.preferredOption: Create overlapping signals in one location: bright panels, ground symbols, whistle, and mirror or light.
guideData.fallbackOption: If gear is limited, maximize contrast with clothing or natural materials and focus on one clear visible position.
steps: Choose the safest visible location you can reach.
Lay out bright or contrasting items in large blocks or stripes.
Build simple ground symbols and, if useful, an arrow to your exact position.
Use mirror or light for active signaling when searchers may be nearby.
Use whistle patterns regularly and conserve your voice.
If fire signaling is safe and legal, consider it only with strong control and fire safety.
warnings: Signal fires can start wildfires and may be illegal or unsafe depending on conditions.
Hand-waving alone is weak compared with mirrors, lights, and contrast panels.
whatNotToDo: Do not spread signals across multiple locations.
Do not camouflage yourself when you want to be found.
Do not rely on a single method if you have alternatives.
redFlags: Aircraft or vehicles are repeatedly nearby but do not notice you.
Visibility is worsening rapidly from weather, smoke, or darkness.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one high-visibility item and a reflective emergency blanket.
Choose pack and shell colors that are visible if you often travel remotely.

sourceReferences.title: Visual distress signal effectiveness testing
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Controlled visibility testing for mirrors, flares, and hand-waving.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor emergency signaling guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports combined sound and light signaling when lost.

sourceReferences.title: Rescue signaling best practice education
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for combining multiple methods.

guides.title: Night Signaling and Battery Conservation

guideData.title: Night Signaling and Battery Conservation
guideData.summary: At night, light signals can help rescuers find you, but battery and warmth conservation still matter.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when stranded after dark or when aerial or distant visual spotting is possible.
guideData.preferredOption: Use short repeated light signals while preserving battery for longer-term survival needs.
steps: Use repeated short flashes rather than leaving a light on continuously if battery is limited.
Balance signaling with warmth, shelter, and hydration needs.
Keep a reserve of battery for later if rescue may be delayed.
warnings: Signal fires can spread if weather conditions change.
Exhausted batteries from nighttime signaling may leave you without a light or communication device.
whatNotToDo: Do not drain your only light source immediately if rescue may take time.
redFlags: No battery remaining and no rescue contact made.
Signs of hypothermia or serious deterioration while signaling at night.

sourceReferences.title: General Guidance on Emergency Communication Strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful communication concepts, but not ideal as a primary wilderness signaling source.

sourceReferences.title: Recommended Emergency Preparedness Guidelines for Rail Transit Systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contains signaling and coordination patterns, but source fit for lost-person rescue needs review.

guides.title: Mirror and Light Signaling (Land, Day and Night)

guideData.title: Mirror and Light Signaling (Land, Day and Night)
guideData.summary: Aim sunlight or a bright light at aircraft, boats, or distant people using a mirror or reflective object to create intense flashes.
guideData.whenToUse: Daytime in open or partly open terrain when you can see aircraft, boats, or distant rescuers, or at night with a flashlight or headlamp.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a dedicated signal mirror and aiming method to sweep flashes across potential rescuers.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack a signal mirror, use any reflective surface or strobe mode on a light at night.
steps: In sunlight, hold the mirror near your eye and use the V-finger method or built-in sight to walk the reflected spot onto the target.
Sweep flashes slowly across aircraft, ridgelines, trail corridors, or boats and repeat regularly.
At night, use a flashlight or headlamp to send groups of three flashes toward known search directions or moving lights.
If you cannot move much, prop a mirror or reflective blanket where it can catch light passively.
Pair mirror and light signaling with whistle or other methods.
warnings: Mirrors are almost useless without direct or strong indirect light.
Do not shine high-intensity lights into nearby drivers’ or pilots’ eyes at close range.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on a mirror as your only signal.
Do not assume rescuers saw one flash; keep signaling when search activity is nearby.
redFlags: If you see search aircraft, drones, or boats, prioritize signaling over non-essential tasks.
If injured and unable to move, set reflective items where they can work without constant effort.
preparednessTips: Carry a small signal mirror or reflective card and practice aiming it before trips.
Keep your phone screen reasonably clean as a backup reflector.
Choose emergency blankets with a reflective side that can double as a visual signal.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed mirror-signaling methods and realistic field limitations.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative Red Cross field guide listing mirrors and beacons as key signaling tools.

guides.title: Whistle Distress Signals (Land)

guideData.title: Whistle Distress Signals (Land)
guideData.summary: Use three short whistle blasts repeated regularly as your basic distress signal.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you are lost, injured, or need help and think searchers or other people may be within hearing range.
guideData.preferredOption: Blow three short blasts on a whistle, wait 30 to 60 seconds, and repeat.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no whistle, shout in patterns, bang metal objects, or use a radio or phone if available.
steps: Carry the whistle on your person so it is always reachable.
Give three strong blasts, each about one second long.
Pause for 30 to 60 seconds to listen and conserve energy, then repeat.
If you hear two blasts or a vocal response, continue giving short blasts every 10 to 15 seconds to guide rescuers in.
If using a radio or phone, keep whistle signaling intermittent and consistent.
warnings: Continuous blowing or yelling can exhaust you and make direction-finding harder for rescuers.
A whistle is less effective in strong wind, near loud rivers, or with helicopter noise.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on your voice.
Do not give random patterns; consistent three-blast signals are easier to recognize as distress.
redFlags: Use whistle signals immediately if anyone is injured, unable to move, or stuck in dangerous terrain.
If no response comes and conditions are worsening, shift more effort toward shelter and self-protection while continuing intermittent signals.
preparednessTips: Carry a pea-less plastic whistle on all trips, attached to your person not just your pack.
Practice the three-blast pattern in safe settings.
Include a whistle in every emergency kit and go-bag.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative Red Cross field guide listing three-whistle-blast emergency signaling.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical explanation of three-blast distress timing and field use.

guides.title: Stay Put vs Move

guideData.title: Stay Put vs Move
guideData.summary: In many lost-person situations, staying near the last known point can improve rescue chances, but there are exceptions.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when deciding whether to stay in place or move after becoming lost or stranded.
guideData.preferredOption: If staying put improves safety, shelter, and signaling, remain near your known location.
guideData.fallbackOption: Move only if staying is clearly more dangerous or if movement meaningfully improves shelter, water, or rescue chances.
steps: Assess injury, weather, terrain, water, daylight, and communication options.
Choose the safer option rather than the option that feels more active.
If staying, improve shelter and signaling.
If moving, do so deliberately and leave clear signs of your route if possible.
warnings: Panic movement can increase exposure and separation from rescuers.
whatNotToDo: Do not wander aimlessly.
Do not abandon a strong shelter or signal position without a reason.
redFlags: Imminent hazard at current location such as fire, flooding, or structural collapse.
Severely injured person who cannot be safely moved or stayed with.

sourceReferences.title: General Guidance on Emergency Communication Strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful communication concepts, but not ideal as a primary wilderness signaling source.

sourceReferences.title: Recommended Emergency Preparedness Guidelines for Rail Transit Systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Contains signaling and coordination patterns, but source fit for lost-person rescue needs review.

guides.title: Lost on Land: First 30 Minutes

guideData.title: Lost on Land: First 30 Minutes
guideData.summary: Stop, calm down, assess your situation, try only a short safe backtrack if the route is obvious, then stay put, shelter, and start signaling.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you realize you may be off-route or lost on land and are not facing an immediate threat like fire, flash flood, or rockfall.
guideData.preferredOption: Use STOP: Stop, Think, Observe, Plan.
guideData.fallbackOption: If communication and safe backtracking are not possible, choose a safer nearby base location, shelter, and begin signaling.
steps: Stop moving as soon as you suspect you are lost.
Control breathing and avoid panic.
Check time, weather, terrain, last known point, and available gear.
If a short, obvious retrace is safe, try it once slowly.
If that fails or is uncertain, stop again and stay put.
Call or text for help if possible and share landmarks or GPS.
Pick a safer base area away from immediate hazards.
Start signaling with whistle, bright items, and light if needed.
Conserve warmth, water, and battery.
warnings: Wandering often moves you farther from the expected search area.
Poor visibility and worsening weather sharply increase travel risk.
Night movement without a clear route is high-risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep wandering without a deliberate plan.
Do not split the group.
Do not waste phone battery on non-essential use.
redFlags: You are injured, hypothermic, dehydrated, or trapped by terrain.
Weather is worsening and you lack good shelter.
You are in avalanche terrain, flood drainages, cliff areas, or fire zones and need to move only far enough to reach safer ground.
preparednessTips: Leave a route plan and return time with a trusted contact before trips.
Carry whistle, light, extra warm layer, basic shelter, map, and compass.
Learn local emergency numbers before travel.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical lost-person guidance for outdoor users.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative movement decisions in poor visibility.

guides.title: Cold-Wet Exposure: Rain, Wind, and Chill

guideData.title: Cold-Wet Exposure: Rain, Wind, and Chill
guideData.summary: In cold, wet, windy conditions, staying dry and sheltered is as important as temperature; seek windbreaks, change wet clothes, and insulate from the ground.
guideData.whenToUse: For hikers, outdoor workers, or stranded travelers in cool to cold weather with rain, wet snow, or strong wind.
guideData.preferredOption: Reduce exposure by finding or creating shelter, changing into dry layers, and staying off cold ground or metal.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot fully shelter, improvise with a vehicle, tarp, overhang, or dense trees where safe, and maximize insulation with dry materials.
steps: Seek or create shelter from wind and precipitation.
Remove and replace wet clothing with dry items, or wring out clothes and add insulation if nothing else is available.
Sit or lie on insulating material instead of bare ground or metal.
Keep extremities covered and avoid sweating heavily into your clothes.
Monitor closely for early hypothermia and warm up early.
warnings: Hypothermia risk is high in cold-wet-windy conditions even when air temperatures are above freezing.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore wet clothing.
Do not rely only on exertion to stay warm; exhaustion increases risk.
redFlags: Confusion, extreme fatigue, stumbling, or other possible hypothermia signs.
preparednessTips: Promote waterproof outer layers and emergency rain gear in gear checklists.
Encourage bail-out routes or shelter options when traveling in cold-wet environments.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes wet clothing and warming.

sourceReferences.title: Winter Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical clothing and storm advice.

guides.title: Staying Warm and Dry in Cold Weather (Prevention)

guideData.title: Staying Warm and Dry in Cold Weather (Prevention)
guideData.summary: Dress in layers, stay dry, cover extremities, limit wind and wet exposure, and avoid overexertion that makes you sweat heavily.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you must be outside in cold, windy, or freezing conditions and are not yet showing signs of hypothermia or frostbite.
guideData.preferredOption: Use layered clothing, waterproof outer layers, hats, gloves, and insulated footwear, and seek warm shelter regularly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If gear is limited, improvise insulation with dry spare clothing, blankets, plastic sheeting as a windbreak, and shared shelter.
steps: Dress in loose layers: moisture-wicking base, insulating middle, and windproof or waterproof outer layer.
Cover head, ears, hands, and feet with hat, gloves or mittens, and appropriate footwear.
Stay as dry as possible and change out of wet clothes quickly.
Limit time in strong wind and take frequent warm-up breaks indoors or in sheltered areas.
Eat regular meals and drink warm non-alcoholic fluids when available.
warnings: Being wet and exposed to wind speeds heat loss dramatically and can cause hypothermia even above freezing.
Tight clothing can reduce circulation and worsen cold injury risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not use alcohol to warm up.
Do not keep wearing wet socks, gloves, or base layers if dry replacements exist.
redFlags: Very cold, confused, or drowsy behavior.
Numb, pale, hard, or waxy skin on fingers, toes, ears, or nose.
preparednessTips: Pack extra dry socks, gloves, and a hat whenever you go out in cold weather.
Keep a cold-weather vehicle kit with blankets, spare clothing, snacks, and chemical hand warmers.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes CDC prevention advice on staying warm and dry.

sourceReferences.title: Winter Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical winter safety measures.

guides.title: Hypothermia

guideData.title: Hypothermia
guideData.summary: Recognizing and treating dangerous cold exposure.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: Hypothermia can progress rapidly and is often not recognized by the person affected.
Do not rub hypothermic limbs vigorously; this can cause dangerous cardiac arrhythmias.
redFlags: Confusion, slurred speech, loss of coordination, or uncontrollable shivering.
Cardiac arrhythmia, loss of consciousness, or cessation of shivering in very cold exposure.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Cold
sourceReferences.whyUseful: CDC guidance on cold exposure, hypothermia, and frostbite prevention.

guides.title: Frostbite: Protect and Rewarm Safely

guideData.title: Frostbite: Protect and Rewarm Safely
guideData.summary: Protect the area from further cold, avoid walking on frostbitten feet, and rewarm in warm water only if you can prevent refreezing.
guideData.whenToUse: When skin exposed to cold becomes numb, hard, pale, or waxy, especially on fingers, toes, nose, ears, or cheeks.
guideData.preferredOption: Get to a warm place, remove wet or tight items, gently rewarm the area, and obtain medical care promptly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the tissue might refreeze, protect from further cold, pad and immobilize it, and delay active rewarming until refreezing risk is lower.
steps: Move the person to warm shelter as soon as possible.
Remove wet gloves, socks, or shoes, avoiding force on stuck items.
Do not walk on frostbitten feet or toes unless absolutely necessary.
Rewarm the area in warm water that feels comfortable to unaffected skin if refreezing risk is low.
Protect the area with loose clean covering and seek medical care as soon as practical.
warnings: Refreezing after thawing can cause severe tissue damage.
Rewarming can be painful but direct high heat can burn numb skin.
whatNotToDo: Do not rub frostbitten skin with snow or massage it.
Do not use direct dry heat from fires, stoves, or heating pads.
redFlags: Blistering, black or very dark skin, loss of sensation after rewarming, or large affected areas.
Frostbite plus hypothermia symptoms.
preparednessTips: Teach users that early tingling or numbness is a warning to stop and rewarm before frostbite develops.
Carry extra socks, gloves, and a dry insulating layer on all cold outings.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes basic field management of frostbite.

guides.title: Working or Exercising Safely in High Heat

guideData.title: Working or Exercising Safely in High Heat
guideData.summary: Increase water intake, schedule harder work for cooler times, take frequent shade breaks, and build up activity gradually in hot conditions.
guideData.whenToUse: For outdoor workers, athletes, hikers, and anyone doing sustained physical activity in hot or humid weather.
guideData.preferredOption: Adjust workload, rest breaks, clothing, and hydration while monitoring yourself and teammates for early symptoms.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the schedule cannot change, shorten work, increase rest breaks, use every available shade or cooling option, and reduce intensity.
steps: Check heat alerts and move the hardest tasks to early morning or evening if possible.
Build up activity gradually over 1 to 2 weeks on the first hot days to support acclimatization.
Drink small frequent amounts of water throughout the activity rather than waiting for thirst.
Take regular breaks in shade or cooling areas.
Wear light breathable clothing, a hat, and sunscreen.
Stop early if cramps, dizziness, nausea, headache, or unusual fatigue appear.
warnings: Some medications, alcohol, and high humidity increase heat risk.
Heat illness can happen even when the temperature does not seem extreme if humidity is high and exertion is heavy.
whatNotToDo: Do not perform maximal-effort workouts or the heaviest labor during the hottest parts of the day if it can be avoided.
Do not ignore cramps, dizziness, or nausea.
redFlags: Confusion, chest pain, fainting, persistent vomiting, or symptoms that do not improve with rest and cooling.
preparednessTips: Use work-rest and hydration reminders tied to local heat alerts.
Encourage buddy systems in hot environments.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Risk factors and early symptoms for exertional heat illness.

sourceReferences.title: Protect Yourself From the Dangers of Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: General outdoor planning and heat behavior advice.

guides.title: Everyday Extreme Heat Precautions (Non-Emergency)

guideData.title: Everyday Extreme Heat Precautions (Non-Emergency)
guideData.summary: Stay cool, hydrated, out of direct sun, and check on high-risk people during extreme heat.
guideData.whenToUse: During heat advisories, heat waves, or any period when temperatures or heat index are much higher than normal and you are not yet acutely ill.
guideData.preferredOption: Limit heat exposure, use cooler indoor spaces or shade, drink water regularly, and shift activity to cooler parts of the day.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no air-conditioning, use fans when outdoor air is cooler, cross-ventilation, cool showers, damp cloths, and frequent shaded rest.
steps: Check local forecasts and heat alerts and plan outdoor tasks for early morning or evening.
Wear loose, light-colored clothing, a hat, and sunscreen on exposed skin.
Drink water in small, regular amounts rather than waiting until you feel very thirsty.
Limit alcohol, very sugary drinks, and heavy hot meals that can worsen dehydration or heat stress.
Use cool showers, baths, or damp cloths on neck, armpits, and groin if you feel overheated.
Check on older adults, infants, people with chronic disease, outdoor workers, and unhoused neighbors during heat waves.
Never leave children, disabled people, or pets in parked cars.
warnings: Heat can worsen heart, lung, kidney, and other chronic conditions before obvious heat-illness symptoms appear.
Dehydration can be harder to notice in older adults and children.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on thirst to decide when to drink.
Do not do strenuous activity during peak afternoon heat if it can be avoided.
Do not leave anyone in a closed parked vehicle.
redFlags: Confusion, fainting, chest pain, trouble breathing, or vomiting during heat exposure.
Symptoms suggesting severe dehydration or heat stroke.
preparednessTips: Identify the coolest room in your home and how to ventilate it safely.
Know locations of public cooling centers or air-conditioned public spaces.
Store extra drinking water and oral rehydration supplies if outages are common.

guides.title: Heat Safety and Prevention

guideData.title: Heat Safety and Prevention
guideData.summary: Hydration, shade, lighter clothing, pacing, and checking vulnerable people reduce the risk of heat illness.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before outdoor work, travel, sports, or heat waves.
guideData.preferredOption: Plan activities for cooler times, drink regularly, rest in shade, and monitor people at higher risk.
steps: Drink regularly and do not wait until you are very thirsty.
Wear lightweight, breathable clothing.
Schedule heavy activity for cooler hours when possible.
Take shade or cooling breaks frequently.
Check children, older adults, and people with chronic illness regularly.
warnings: Heat illness can develop even in people who seem healthy, especially during heat waves.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave people or pets in parked vehicles.
redFlags: Confusion, fainting, chest pain, or vomiting in the heat.
Hot, dry skin with no sweating in a person exposed to heat.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Concise emergency cooling and EMS actions.

guides.title: Dehydration

guideData.title: Dehydration
guideData.summary: Prevention and treatment of fluid loss.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: Severe dehydration can cause kidney failure, seizures, and death.
Rehydrating too rapidly with plain water can cause hyponatremia (low sodium); use oral rehydration salts if available.
redFlags: Confusion, irritability, or rapid breathing combined with thirst.
No urination for 8 or more hours.
Loss of consciousness.

sourceReferences.title: Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: CDC guidance on preventing and responding to heat illness.

guides.title: Dehydration: Signs and Immediate Care

guideData.title: Dehydration: Signs and Immediate Care
guideData.summary: Move to a cooler place and give small frequent sips of safe fluids if the person is awake and not vomiting.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after heat exposure, vomiting, diarrhea, or prolonged exertion when someone is thirsty, weak, dizzy, or making very little dark urine.
guideData.preferredOption: Rest in a cool or shaded place and sip water or oral rehydration solution slowly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If only limited fluids are available, use small frequent sips of the safest available drink and avoid alcohol.
steps: Check for dry mouth, dark urine, dizziness, fatigue, or reduced urination.
Move to shade or a cool area.
Loosen tight clothing.
Give small sips of water or oral rehydration solution if fully awake.
Monitor over the next 1–2 hours.
warnings: Children, older adults, and chronically ill people may worsen quickly.
Severe dehydration can cause shock and organ failure.
whatNotToDo: Do not force fluids into someone who is vomiting repeatedly, very drowsy, or confused.
Do not rely on thirst alone.
redFlags: No urine, confusion, fainting, repeated vomiting, seizures, or rapid worsening need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Carry oral rehydration salts or electrolyte packets.
Teach urine-color awareness as a simple hydration check.

sourceReferences.title: Signs of Dehydration
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative public guidance on dehydration signs and when to seek help.

sourceReferences.title: Dehydration Symptoms
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear symptom guidance for public use.

guides.title: Heat Exhaustion: Cool Down and Rehydrate

guideData.title: Heat Exhaustion: Cool Down and Rehydrate
guideData.summary: Move the person to a cooler place, cool them down, and give small sips of fluid; seek care if symptoms worsen or last about an hour.
guideData.whenToUse: When someone in the heat has heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, or dizziness but is still awake and able to drink.
guideData.preferredOption: Cool the person promptly with shade or AC, wet cloths, and fluids, then monitor closely for worsening symptoms.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no AC, use shade, loosen clothing, fan the person, and apply cool wet cloths while offering small sips of cool water if they can drink.
steps: Move the person to a cooler area, ideally indoors with air-conditioning or otherwise into shade.
Lay them down and loosen or remove excess clothing.
Apply cool wet cloths, mist the skin, or use a cool bath if available and safe.
Give cool water or oral rehydration solution in small frequent sips if the person is fully awake and not vomiting.
Stay with them and monitor for at least an hour; seek medical care if symptoms do not improve or worsen.
warnings: Heat exhaustion can progress to life-threatening heat stroke.
People with heart disease, kidney disease, or certain medications may need medical advice earlier.
whatNotToDo: Do not give alcohol or very sugary drinks.
Do not leave the person alone or send them back to strenuous activity the same day.
redFlags: Confusion, loss of consciousness, seizures, chest pain, or trouble breathing.
Vomiting that prevents the person from keeping fluids down.
Very hot or dry skin suggesting heat stroke.
preparednessTips: Keep oral rehydration salts or electrolyte packets in first aid kits.
Teach early signs of heat illness to workers, athletes, and families.

guideData.title: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
guideData.summary: Food handling priorities when refrigeration and cooking are limited.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during outages, evacuations, and prolonged sheltering.
warnings: Food that smells normal can still be unsafe to eat after temperature abuse.
Children, pregnant people, older adults, and immunocompromised people are at higher risk from foodborne illness.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, high fever, or severe abdominal pain after eating food stored without refrigeration.
Dehydration or confusion in vulnerable people after suspected foodborne illness.
