guideData.title: Food Preservation
guideData.summary: Methods to store food long-term.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: Improperly preserved food can harbor dangerous bacteria including botulinum toxin.
Do not consume preserved food that smells abnormal, shows mold, or has unusual texture.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, or neurological symptoms after consuming preserved food.
Bloated or leaking containers, unusual smell, or unusual color of preserved food.

guides.title: Safe Food Handling When Water Is Limited

guideData.title: Safe Food Handling When Water Is Limited
guideData.summary: Use safe water for any food-contact task, and if safe water is scarce, prioritize drinking, medication, hand hygiene before food prep, and rinsing food-contact items.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during emergencies when safe water is limited but you still need to prepare food or clean utensils and surfaces.
guideData.preferredOption: Use safe water for handwashing before food prep, washing produce that will be eaten raw, rinsing utensils, and cleaning food-contact surfaces.
guideData.fallbackOption: If safe water is scarce, use alcohol-based hand sanitizer on visibly clean hands and prioritize disposable utensils and minimal-contact food prep.
steps: Prioritize safe water for drinking, medications, hand hygiene, food rinsing, and dish rinsing.
Wash hands with safe water and soap when possible.
Use hand sanitizer on visibly clean hands when water is limited.
Use safe water for produce eaten raw.
If dishwashing water is limited, scrape first, wash in small basins, and sanitize with safe-water-based solution.
warnings: Using contaminated water on produce, utensils, or surfaces can re-contaminate food.
Gray water is not safe for food handling or handwashing.
whatNotToDo: Do not rinse ready-to-eat foods with unsafe water.
Do not reuse dirty dishwater for final rinsing or hand hygiene.
redFlags: Cluster of gastrointestinal illness after changes in water use or dishwashing.
Vomiting or diarrhea in vulnerable household members.
preparednessTips: Store extra safe water labeled for cooking and kitchen hygiene.
Keep disposable utensils and sanitizer in emergency kits.
Plan low-water food preparation methods in advance.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH and food safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports safe kitchen use of limited water.

guides.title: Deciding When to Throw Away Food After an Outage

guideData.title: Deciding When to Throw Away Food After an Outage
guideData.summary: When power returns, keep perishable foods only if they stayed at or below 4 C / 40 F; when in doubt, throw it out.
guideData.whenToUse: When power has returned or you must decide what to discard after a prolonged outage.
guideData.preferredOption: Check temperatures and discard high-risk foods that were above 4 C / 40 F for 2 hours or more.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no thermometer is available, use conservative time rules and discard perishable refrigerated items after 4 hours or more without safe cooling.
steps: Check fridge and freezer temperatures when power returns.
Check high-risk foods such as meat, poultry, fish, eggs, dairy, and leftovers first.
Discard perishable foods that were above 4 C / 40 F for 2 hours or more.
Frozen foods with ice crystals and safe temperatures can usually be refrozen or cooked.
When uncertain, discard rather than risk illness.
warnings: Unsafe foods may still look and smell normal.
Children, pregnant people, older adults, and immunocompromised people are at higher risk from foodborne illness.
whatNotToDo: Do not refreeze thawed foods that were warm for too long.
Do not rely on touch alone to judge safety.
Do not feed questionable foods to pets or livestock.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, or high fever after eating outage-stored food.
Confusion or dehydration in vulnerable people after suspected foodborne illness.
preparednessTips: Keep printed charts of safe food timelines and discard rules with your emergency supplies.
Maintain thermometers in your fridge and freezer so decisions are easier.

sourceReferences.title: Are You and Your Food Prepared for a Power Outage?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: USDA food safety timelines for refrigerators and freezers during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Avoid Foodborne Illness During Temporary Power Outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specific refrigerator and freezer timelines plus food-safety advice during outages.

sourceReferences.title: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms outage timelines and safety rules for food and water.

guides.title: Freezer Safety During Outages

guideData.title: Freezer Safety During Outages
guideData.summary: A full freezer can stay cold about 48 hours, or 24 hours if half-full, if the door stays closed; food with ice crystals may still be safe to refreeze or cook.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during and after a power outage when frozen food may be thawing.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep the freezer closed and monitor with a thermometer if possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the outage will be long, group foods together and move critical items to coolers with ice or dry ice if available.
steps: Before outages, keep the freezer cold and reasonably full.
During outage, keep the door closed.
After outage, check for ice crystals and safe temperature.
Discard foods that have been above safe temperatures too long.
Refreeze only foods that stayed cold enough, understanding quality may decline.
warnings: Food can become unsafe even if it still looks normal.
Do not rely only on smell or color.
whatNotToDo: Do not open the freezer repeatedly.
Do not refreeze food that stayed warm too long.
Do not leave thawed food at room temperature for hours.
redFlags: Household members become sick after eating outage-affected previously frozen food.
preparednessTips: Keep a freezer thermometer installed.
Store extra ice or gel packs for coolers.
Label and group foods for quicker decision-making.

sourceReferences.title: Food safety during power outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for freezer hold times and safety decisions.

guides.title: Refrigerator Safety During Outages

guideData.title: Refrigerator Safety During Outages
guideData.summary: Keep the fridge closed; perishable foods are generally safe for about 4 hours without power if the door stays shut and temperatures stay cold enough.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during and immediately after a power outage when you still have refrigerated food that may be at risk.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep refrigerator doors closed as much as possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the outage will exceed about 4 hours, move the most perishable items to a cooler with ice or gel packs if you can keep them cold enough.
steps: Before outages, keep a thermometer in the refrigerator.
During outage, keep the door closed.
After outage, check temperature.
Discard high-risk foods that were above 4°C / 40°F for too long.
Keep low-risk items only if still cold and otherwise safe.
warnings: Danger-zone temperatures allow rapid bacterial growth.
Smell and appearance are not reliable safety tests.
whatNotToDo: Do not repeatedly open the refrigerator just to check.
Do not taste food to test safety.
Do not keep leftovers that sat warm too long.
redFlags: Several people get vomiting, diarrhea, or fever after eating outage-affected refrigerated food.
Vulnerable household members have symptoms after eating questionable food.
preparednessTips: Keep a thermometer in the fridge.
Store shelf-stable foods that do not require refrigeration.
Plan coolers and ice packs for longer outages.

sourceReferences.title: Food safety during power outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary rules for refrigerator safety and discard decisions.

guides.title: Solar Still

guideData.title: Solar Still
guideData.summary: Collecting water using solar energy.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: A solar still produces very little water and may not cover minimum daily hydration needs.
Do not rely solely on a solar still if other safer water sources are available.
redFlags: Signs of severe dehydration: no urination, extreme thirst, confusion, or dizziness.
Any sign of heat stroke or heat exhaustion while relying on a solar still.

guides.title: Water Purification

guideData.title: Water Purification
guideData.summary: Methods to make water safer for drinking.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: Untreated water can carry bacteria, viruses, and parasites causing severe illness.
Chemical treatments have correct dosing requirements; overdosing or underdosing can be harmful.
redFlags: Signs of waterborne illness: severe diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, or fever after consuming treated water.
Confusion or inability to treat water due to cognitive impairment from dehydration.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Water Supply
sourceReferences.whyUseful: CDC guidance on safe emergency water sources and treatment.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based guidance on water purification in the field.

guides.title: Emergency Water Storage at Home

guideData.title: Emergency Water Storage at Home
guideData.summary: Store at least 1 gallon of safe water per person per day for at least 3 days, and ideally up to 2 weeks, in clean food-grade containers.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before emergencies, or when you still have safe water and time to prepare for outages, storms, or disaster disruption.
guideData.preferredOption: Store safe tap or bottled water in clean tightly sealed food-grade containers in cool, dark locations away from chemicals.
guideData.fallbackOption: If ideal containers are unavailable, carefully reuse suitable beverage bottles such as PET soda bottles after proper cleaning and sanitizing.
steps: Calculate at least 1 gallon per person per day, with more for heat, pets, illness, or special needs.
Choose food-grade containers that close tightly and have not stored chemicals.
Wash, rinse, sanitize, and dry self-filled containers before use.
Fill with safe water and label with date and drinking-water designation.
Store away from sunlight, heat, fuels, pesticides, and chemical vapors.
Rotate self-filled stored water every 6 months.
warnings: Vapors from gasoline, pesticides, and chemicals can penetrate some plastics.
Milk jugs and some food containers are poor long-term choices because residues can promote bacterial growth.
whatNotToDo: Do not partially close containers.
Do not keep topping off old water without emptying, cleaning, and refilling.
Do not assume unknown plastics are food-grade.
redFlags: Stored water smells foul, appears slimy, or has visible growth.
An emergency occurs and you have no safe stored water and unsafe public supply.
preparednessTips: Aim for a 2-week supply if space and budget allow.
Store smaller grab-and-go bottles as well as larger home containers.
Use reminders to rotate stored water every 6 months.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water supply guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary household storage quantity and handling guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Household water storage best practices
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports container cleaning, storage, and rotation.

guides.title: When Treated Water Is Still Unsafe to Drink

guideData.title: When Treated Water Is Still Unsafe to Drink
guideData.summary: Water can remain unsafe after boiling or chlorination if it contains fuel, toxic chemicals, or heavy metals, or if authorities issue a do-not-drink notice.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you have already boiled or disinfected water but suspect chemical contamination or are under a stronger official advisory than a normal boil-water notice.
guideData.preferredOption: Obey do-not-drink or do-not-use advisories and use only confirmed safe water for drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, and infant formula.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no safe drinking water is available, use suspect water only for limited non-drinking tasks like flushing if allowed and seek emergency water distribution quickly.
steps: Check whether the advisory is boil-water, do-not-drink, or do-not-use.
Inspect the water for oily sheen, strong chemical or fuel odor, or unusual contamination indicators.
Use safe water only for high-risk activities like drinking, cooking, infant feeding, brushing teeth, dish rinsing, and wound cleaning.
Reserve suspect water only for low-risk non-contact uses if officials allow it.
warnings: Floodwater is often contaminated with sewage and chemicals.
Some dangerous contaminants have no obvious taste or smell.
whatNotToDo: Do not drink water from vehicles, heating systems, or radiators.
Do not wash open wounds or medical devices with chemically suspect water.
Do not assume boiling fixes chemical hazards.
redFlags: Sudden dizziness, chest pain, breathing problems, or confusion after exposure to contaminated water.
Known industrial or fuel contamination in the water system.
preparednessTips: Store extra safe water so you are less likely to drink questionable sources.
Know where local emergency water distribution points may be located.
Keep a battery or solar radio for advisories.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety and treatment limits
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear distinction between boil-water and do-not-drink situations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Important limits of boiling and chlorination methods.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports practical messaging on contamination and use restrictions.

guides.title: Using Non-Drinking Water Safely

guideData.title: Using Non-Drinking Water Safely
guideData.summary: Reserve suspect or lower-quality water for low-risk tasks like flushing or cleaning non-food-contact surfaces, and keep it away from drinking, cooking, wounds, and infant feeding.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you have lower-quality or questionable water that is not safe to drink but may still be useful for limited non-drinking household needs.
guideData.preferredOption: Clearly separate safe drinking water from non-drinking water and use only the lowest-risk source for each task.
guideData.fallbackOption: If quality is uncertain and official guidance is unavailable, use questionable water only for flushing or other minimal-contact tasks.
steps: Label containers clearly as drinking or non-drinking water.
Use safe water for food, teeth, dishes that contact food, infant feeding, and wounds.
Use lower-quality water only for flushing, some cleaning tasks, or other non-contact uses if allowed.
Avoid splashing contaminated water into living or food-prep areas.
Discard non-drinking water if it shows obvious chemical contamination that makes even handling unsafe.
warnings: Using unsafe water for produce, utensils, wounds, or infant feeding can cause illness.
Gray water is not safe for handwashing or food contact.
whatNotToDo: Do not rinse ready-to-eat foods with unsafe water.
Do not wash open wounds with suspect water.
Do not use contaminated water for final dish rinses or handwashing before food prep.
redFlags: Chemical odor, oily sheen, or irritation from skin contact with suspect water.
Household illness cluster after using questionable water in food or cleaning processes.
preparednessTips: Store extra water specifically designated for hygiene and cleaning.
Keep written instructions on which tasks require fully safe water.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports safe vs unsafe uses of compromised water.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for separating drinking and non-drinking water tasks.

guides.title: Choosing Safe Hydration Sources

guideData.title: Choosing Safe Hydration Sources
guideData.summary: Use sealed bottled water first; use tap water only if officials say it is safe; otherwise boil or disinfect the clearest available source before drinking.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when an emergency disrupts normal water service or you are unsure which available water is safest to drink, cook with, or use for brushing teeth.
guideData.preferredOption: Drink sealed bottled water from trusted sources first.
guideData.fallbackOption: If neither is available, use the clearest water from the safest protected source you can find and then boil or disinfect it before use.
steps: Check local emergency, health, or water authority alerts.
Rank your sources from safest to riskiest.
Reserve the safest water for drinking, infant formula, food preparation, brushing teeth, and wound cleaning.
Use lower-quality water only for non-contact uses like flushing or cleaning.
warnings: Boiling or bleach does not remove fuel, toxic chemicals, or heavy metals.
Floodwater is usually contaminated with sewage, chemicals, and debris and should be treated as unsafe for drinking.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume clear-looking water is safe.
Do not drink from radiators, boilers, chemically treated pools, or hot tubs.
Do not ignore boil-water or do-not-drink notices.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, or fever after drinking questionable water.
Known sewage or chemical contamination exposure.
Infants, older adults, pregnant people, or immunocompromised people showing dehydration signs.
preparednessTips: Store at least 1 gallon per person per day for at least 3 days, with 2 weeks as a better target if possible.
Keep printed boiling and bleach-treatment instructions with your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong public guidance on choosing safe emergency water sources.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for treatment methods and limitations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports practical emergency communication and contamination limits.

guides.title: Balancing Activity and Rest to Avoid Exhaustion
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling

guideData.title: Balancing Activity and Rest to Avoid Exhaustion
guideData.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guideData.summary: Pace yourself; overexertion increases risk of injury, poor decisions, and hypothermia or heat illness.
guideData.whenToUse: Throughout any extended emergency or survival situation.
guideData.preferredOption: Alternate periods of necessary work with rest, and stop tasks before you become exhausted or confused.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are already exhausted, simplify your plan and focus only on shelter, warmth, and minimal signaling until you recover.
steps: After initial planning, schedule short work blocks followed by rest, especially in difficult conditions.
Keep tasks simple and high-value; avoid complex projects when tired.
Monitor yourself and others for fatigue, irritability, and poor coordination.
Adjust plans as daylight changes and avoid demanding work in the dark unless absolutely necessary.
warnings: Exhaustion and low blood sugar impair judgment.
In cold or wet conditions, activity can hide early hypothermia until you stop moving.
whatNotToDo: Do not insist on finishing non-critical tasks when you are clearly beyond your limits.
Do not work in hazardous terrain when extremely tired or in the dark unless it is the lesser danger.
redFlags: Severe confusion, inability to follow simple instructions, or repeated falls.
Chest pain or shortness of breath not explained by mild exertion.
preparednessTips: Maintain basic fitness and health.
Bring enough food and water to reduce exhaustion and dehydration risk.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative, energy-aware emergency planning once stranded.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Reinforces that having supplies reduces the need for risky overexertion in emergencies.

guides.title: When to Drop Tasks and Focus on Signaling
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling

guideData.title: When to Drop Tasks and Focus on Signaling
guideData.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guideData.summary: When rescuers are likely searching for you or you see or hear them, stop extra tasks and focus on being seen and heard.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you suspect a search has started or see aircraft, drones, boats, or search teams.
guideData.preferredOption: Shift energy to whistle use, mirror or light flashes, and making your location obvious with ground or visual signals.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot actively signal continuously, set up passive signals like bright clothing or reflective blankets where they will be most visible.
steps: When you hear or see potential rescuers, stop non-essential tasks.
Move to a visible area if safe, bringing whistle, mirror, and bright items.
Use three-blast whistle signals and mirror or light flashes toward rescuers.
Stand or place bright gear where you contrast with the surroundings.
Once contact seems likely, stay in place unless directed to move.
warnings: Missing a narrow window of visibility can delay rescue significantly.
Over-focusing on camp tasks can make you miss distant sounds or signs of search teams.
whatNotToDo: Do not continue long, noisy tasks that mask searcher calls or aircraft noise.
Do not hide under dense canopy when you could safely stand in a clearing.
redFlags: If your condition is deteriorating, communicate that clearly as soon as contact is made.
preparednessTips: Keep signaling tools accessible, not buried in your pack.
Establish a group plan so everyone pauses and signals when search activity appears.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative field guide stressing signaling early and using whistles, mirrors, and beacons.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical field timing and responsiveness for signaling once a search may be active.

guides.title: Signs You Must Switch to Shelter and Warmth
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling

guideData.title: Signs You Must Switch to Shelter and Warmth
guideData.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guideData.summary: If you are getting cold, wet, or exhausted, stop non-essential tasks and put your energy into staying warm, dry, and out of the wind.
guideData.whenToUse: When weather worsens, daylight fades, or you notice early signs of hypothermia or heat stress.
guideData.preferredOption: Pause other activities and work on simple, effective shelter and clothing adjustments.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot build shelter, move to more protected terrain, huddle with others, and use any available insulation and emergency blankets.
steps: Watch for uncontrollable shivering, clumsiness, confusion, or slurred speech in cold conditions.
If present, stop tasks like exploring or building elaborate shelters.
Add dry layers, get out of wind or rain, insulate from the ground, and share body warmth if appropriate.
In hot conditions, move to shade, loosen clothing, sip safe water, and rest.
Once basic comfort is restored, re-evaluate priorities and continue only essential tasks.
warnings: Hypothermia and heat stroke can progress quickly and quietly.
Continuing physical work in these conditions can push you into collapse.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to stay warm if you are wet, exhausted, and unsure of direction.
Do not ignore shivering or confusion in yourself or others.
redFlags: Confusion, loss of coordination, or collapse in cold or hot environments.
Shivering that stops suddenly while the person is still cold.
preparednessTips: Pack adequate clothing and an emergency blanket even on day trips.
Learn signs of hypothermia and heat illness through formal first-aid training.
Check forecasts and plan for worse-than-expected conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative emergency planning and shelter-first behavior when stranded.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical exposure-recognition and shelter priorities for public outdoor users.

guides.title: Daypack-Size Emergency Kit for Hikes
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit

guideData.title: Daypack-Size Emergency Kit for Hikes
guideData.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guideData.summary: Add core essentials to your hiking pack: navigation, light, extra clothing, shelter, water, food, first aid, fire, tools, and signaling.
guideData.whenToUse: For day hikes, short trips, or local outings where an unplanned night out is possible.
guideData.preferredOption: Pack items that let you survive an unplanned night outdoors: extra warmth, rain protection, emergency shelter, water and treatment, first aid, light, and signaling.
guideData.fallbackOption: If space or budget is limited, prioritize shelter or warmth, light, signaling, and water over duplicate tools.
steps: Carry map, compass, and or offline GPS or phone maps.
Pack an extra warm layer, hat, light rain shell, and emergency blanket or bivy.
Bring a headlamp and spare batteries.
Carry enough water for the day plus a treatment method.
Add extra high-energy snacks.
Pack a basic first aid kit, blister care, whistle, reflective item, and a small knife or multi-tool used conservatively.
warnings: Overpacking can lead to leaving the kit behind; find a balance you will actually carry.
Some locations restrict open flame and certain tools.
whatNotToDo: Do not pack only cool gear while neglecting basics like warm clothing or water treatment.
Do not assume group members will share kits; everyone should have basics.
redFlags: If your kit cannot cover an unplanned night in current weather, consider shortening or turning back from the trip.
preparednessTips: Use official emergency kit checklists as a baseline and adapt for hiking.
Review your kit after each trip and adjust based on what you used or missed.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official checklist that adapts well to an outdoor daypack kit.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong official baseline for water, food, light, tools, and first aid priorities.

guides.title: Pocket Always-With-You Kit
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit

guideData.title: Pocket Always-With-You Kit
guideData.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guideData.summary: Carry a tiny set of essentials—whistle, light, basic bandages, fire starter, and emergency blanket—on your person, not just in your pack.
guideData.whenToUse: Everyday and on any outdoor trip where you might be separated from your main pack.
guideData.preferredOption: Assemble a small kit that fits in a pocket or worn pouch and covers signaling, light, basic first aid, and minimal shelter.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot carry a full micro-kit, prioritize a whistle, small flashlight, and a way to contact help.
steps: Include a whistle, small flashlight or headlamp, adhesive bandages, alcohol wipes, gauze, and tape.
Add a compact emergency blanket, lighter or matches if allowed, and a few water treatment tablets where appropriate.
Keep the kit on your body, not only in your backpack.
Check items regularly and replace dead batteries and expired supplies.
warnings: Relying only on a larger kit in your pack can leave you vulnerable if you are separated from it.
Some items such as lighters may be restricted in specific environments.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload the pocket kit so much that you stop carrying it.
Do not assume your phone alone is enough; batteries and signal can fail.
redFlags: If you use critical items such as all your bandages or water tablets, treat that as a cue to shorten the trip or seek safer conditions.
preparednessTips: Adapt official emergency kit guidance to pocket scale.
Customize for local conditions such as extra warmth in cold climates or sun protection in hot climates.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official checklist that supports small, practical grab-and-go essentials such as light, whistle, and first aid.

guides.title: When Not to Use a Knife
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs

guideData.title: When Not to Use a Knife
guideData.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guideData.summary: Skip the knife if you are cold, exhausted, unstable, or tempted to use it for risky tasks like prying, throwing, or self-defense.
guideData.whenToUse: When deciding whether a task truly justifies pulling out your knife.
guideData.preferredOption: Ask whether you can solve the problem another way without adding blade risk.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must use a knife while not at your best, slow down, reduce the task scope, and consider whether a steadier person can do it instead.
steps: Check whether your hands are cold or numb, whether you are very tired, or whether you are emotionally stressed.
Consider alternatives such as moving the shelter, using a different anchor, or breaking branches by hand.
If you proceed, choose the smallest and simplest cut that will work.
Stop after short intervals to reassess your control and need.
warnings: Many serious outdoor knife injuries happen at the end of the day when people are cold and tired.
Panic-driven or tactical uses of knives increase risk without improving safety.
whatNotToDo: Do not use knives for wildlife self-defense plans; follow official wildlife safety guidance instead.
Do not attempt knife skills you have only seen online without prior safe practice.
redFlags: Any deep or heavily bleeding knife wound.
Wounds with loss of movement or feeling in fingers or toes.
preparednessTips: Reduce knife dependence with pre-cut cord lengths or prepared stakes.
Train on safe tool use before extended trips.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative, safety-first decisions about whether to use a knife at all.

guides.title: Safe Cutting Tasks for Shelter and Repairs
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs

guideData.title: Safe Cutting Tasks for Shelter and Repairs
guideData.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guideData.summary: Use knives for small, controlled tasks—cutting cord, trimming branches, making stakes—not for heavy chopping or prying.
guideData.whenToUse: When you need to create or repair basic shelter elements, prepare simple food, or cut cordage.
guideData.preferredOption: Plan cuts, stabilize materials, and work from a safe position, taking breaks to avoid fatigue.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot safely cut, look for alternatives such as breaking branches by hand, using existing debris, or changing the repair approach.
steps: Use the knife to sharpen stakes, cut cord, and trim small branches rather than chop large wood.
For repairs, make controlled cuts to remove frayed material and shape patches instead of using the knife to pry.
For food, cut on a stable surface and keep fingers curled and away from the blade path.
Brace workpieces on the ground or against a solid object instead of holding them loosely.
warnings: Using a knife as a pry bar can break the blade and cause injury.
Heavy chopping with an unsuitable knife adds risk without much benefit in short-term survival settings.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt to cut large logs or branches when smaller deadfall will work.
Do not carve while walking or distracted.
redFlags: Injury from a broken blade or large splinters.
Lacerations that do not stop bleeding with direct pressure.
preparednessTips: Include a small saw if you anticipate regular shelter-building tasks.
Practice low-risk cutting tasks before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical, public-facing knife-safety limits and task framing.

guides.title: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs

guideData.title: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
guideData.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guideData.summary: Keep your body and others out of your knife’s path, work slowly, and stop using knives when you are cold, exhausted, or impaired.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you use a knife or multi-tool outdoors or in an emergency.
guideData.preferredOption: Use controlled cuts away from your body, keep others outside your arm’s-length safety bubble, and sheath the knife immediately when not in use.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are too cold, tired, or shaky to control the blade, delay the task or use safer alternatives such as tearing, breaking, or using scissors on a multi-tool.
steps: Define your cutting safety bubble at full arm’s reach and keep others out of it.
Avoid cutting in the area between your thighs where a slip can hit major blood vessels.
Cut away from your body and hands using stable support where possible.
Keep blades sharp; dull blades require more force and slip more easily.
Close folding blades and sheath fixed blades immediately after use.
Stop and reassess if you feel rushed, overloaded, or unstable.
warnings: Knife injuries can be life-threatening in remote settings because of bleeding and infection.
Cold, fatigue, and intoxication significantly increase accident risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not use knives while intoxicated or under the influence of drugs.
Do not cut toward your own body or hold material in your lap while cutting.
Do not hand an open knife to another person; close it or pass it handle-first safely.
redFlags: Deep cuts, heavy bleeding that does not stop with direct pressure, or suspected tendon or nerve injury.
Wounds in the groin, inner thigh, or neck.
preparednessTips: Learn basic first aid for bleeding and carry proper dressings.
Choose a sturdy, simple knife or multi-tool with a secure locking mechanism.
Practice safe handling at home before relying on a knife in emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Accessible outdoor knife safety guidance including safe zones and risk factors.

guides.title: Everyday Cordage Uses in Emergencies
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses

guideData.title: Everyday Cordage Uses in Emergencies
guideData.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guideData.summary: Cordage is most useful for shelter setup, securing gear, simple splints, and improvised repairs—not for climbing or rescue.
guideData.whenToUse: In almost any outdoor or disaster scenario where you need to secure, bundle, or support non-life-critical loads.
guideData.preferredOption: Use cord to pitch tarps, secure gear, lash broken items, or support simple improvised splints.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack dedicated cordage, twist strips of strong fabric or webbing for temporary use.
steps: Identify tasks where cord lowers risk, such as keeping shelter taut, bundling gear, or supporting a simple splint.
Use simple knots like half hitches, taut-line hitches, and bowlines for these tasks.
For splints, pad the injured area, place rigid supports, and tie gently above and below the injury rather than over it.
Check knots and cord tension regularly, especially before bad weather.
warnings: Improvised splints require judgment and poor splinting can worsen injuries.
Thin cord can cause pressure injuries if tied too tightly around limbs.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt technical rescues, rappels, or body-weight systems with ordinary cordage.
Do not tie anything so tight that skin turns pale, blue, or numb unless you are applying a trained tourniquet for life-threatening bleeding.
redFlags: Signs of reduced circulation below a tied area such as cold, pale, or numb extremities.
Serious fractures, deformity, or inability to move a limb.
preparednessTips: Pack several meters of medium-strength cord or webbing in your kit.
Practice low-risk tasks like hanging tarps or securing bundles.
Include simple illustrated guides for splinting from recognized first-aid organizations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Supply Kit Checklist
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Illustrates why rope or cord is commonly recommended in emergency kits.

guides.title: Simple Loops and Attachment Knots (Figure-Eight on a Bight, Bowline)
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses

guideData.title: Simple Loops and Attachment Knots (Figure-Eight on a Bight, Bowline)
guideData.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guideData.summary: Use simple, non-slipping loops to attach lines to objects or create fixed loops for gear; do not use them for life-safety loads unless trained.
guideData.whenToUse: For attaching gear to packs, hanging food, or creating fixed loops in a line for non-life-critical tasks.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a bowline or figure-eight on a bight to create a fixed loop at the end or in the middle of a rope.
guideData.fallbackOption: Use multiple overhand knots and backups if you do not know these knots, recognizing they may be harder to untie.
steps: Tie a bowline by making a small loop, passing the working end up through the loop, around the standing part, and back down through the loop.
Tie a figure-eight on a bight by doubling the rope, forming a loop, wrapping around the standing part to make an 8, then passing the loop through and tightening.
Use these loops for gear attachment or fixed points for non-life-safety tasks.
Dress and tighten the knot carefully before loading it.
warnings: Knots can loosen under repeated loading if not dressed and tightened.
For untrained users, any knot used for life safety is outside safe public guidance.
whatNotToDo: Do not use these knots for rappelling, hauling people, or technical rescue.
Do not assume any knot is safe for life support unless you are trained and using proper systems.
redFlags: If you think you need to move someone on steep terrain with ropes, call professional rescue rather than improvising rope systems.
preparednessTips: Practice these knots under calm conditions.
Include printed or in-app diagrams for quick reference.

sourceReferences.title: Knots for Survival and Rescue
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear visual instruction for simple loops, though the app must filter out advanced rescue and climbing content.

guides.title: Managing Small Wounds and Skin Problems

guideData.title: Managing Small Wounds and Skin Problems
guideData.summary: Clean small cuts and blisters early, keep them dry and covered, and watch closely for signs of infection.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you get a small cut, scrape, blister, or insect bite in the field.
guideData.preferredOption: Rinse with clean or treated water, apply antiseptic if available, and cover with a clean bandage.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack antiseptic, rinse with the best available safe water, gently remove dirt, and keep the wound as clean and dry as you can.
steps: Clean your hands or use sanitizer before touching the wound.
Rinse gently with safe water to remove dirt and debris.
Apply antiseptic ointment if available.
Cover with a clean bandage or dressing without cutting off circulation.
Check daily for redness, swelling, pus, warmth, or increasing pain.
For blisters, protect from friction and avoid opening unless necessary for function.
warnings: Even minor wounds can become serious infections in outdoor or disaster settings.
Some ointments and antiseptics can trigger allergic reactions.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply dirt, ash, or unverified natural substances to open wounds.
Do not ignore increasing redness, swelling, or pain.
redFlags: Spreading redness, warmth, significant swelling, fever, or red streaks.
Puncture wounds, animal bites, or deep cuts, especially near joints.
preparednessTips: Carry a basic first aid kit with gloves, dressings, bandages, and antiseptic.
Learn basic first aid from Red Cross or similar organizations.
Keep tetanus immunizations up to date.

sourceReferences.title: When and How to Wash Your Hands
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports the importance of clean hands before wound care.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Supply Kit Checklist
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms basic wound-care items recommended in emergency kits.

guides.title: Human Waste and Washing Basics

guideData.title: Human Waste and Washing Basics
guideData.summary: Keep waste and washing well away from water sources and camp, and bury human waste only where local rules allow.
guideData.whenToUse: On overnight or multi-day trips without toilets, or when infrastructure fails in disasters.
guideData.preferredOption: Use existing toilets or latrines where provided; if not, follow local guidance for catholes and washing at a distance from water.
guideData.fallbackOption: In fragile or heavily regulated areas where burying waste is not allowed, pack out human waste using approved bags per local guidance.
steps: Use toilets or latrines if they exist.
If none exist, choose a site at least about 60 to 70 meters from water, camp, and trails, following local recommendations.
Dig a small cathole 15 to 20 cm deep if allowed and bury waste; pack out toilet paper where required.
Carry wash water away from streams or lakes and scatter greywater broadly instead of dumping it in one spot.
Keep washing and waste areas separate from food storage and sleeping areas.
warnings: Poor waste disposal can contaminate water, attract animals, and spread disease.
Local rules may differ and can override general advice, especially in sensitive or high-use areas.
whatNotToDo: Do not defecate directly in or next to streams, lakes, or springs.
Do not wash dishes or yourself with soap directly in natural water sources.
redFlags: Outbreaks of diarrhea or gastrointestinal illness in your group.
Community or park notices about water contamination.
preparednessTips: Pack a small trowel, toilet paper, hand sanitizer, and waste bags where appropriate.
Learn local regulations before going out; some parks require packing out all waste.
For disasters at home, follow local public health guidance on sanitation and waste disposal.

sourceReferences.title: Camping Hygiene: Tips for Staying Clean Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical outdoor hygiene and waste-management advice.

sourceReferences.title: Handwashing in Emergencies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emergency hygiene principles that support conservative sanitation practices.

guides.title: Hand Hygiene and Food Safety in the Field

guideData.title: Hand Hygiene and Food Safety in the Field
guideData.summary: Clean your hands before eating and after toilet use, and keep food prep surfaces and utensils as clean and dry as possible.
guideData.whenToUse: During any multi-hour or overnight stay outdoors, especially when preparing or eating food or after using the toilet.
guideData.preferredOption: Wash hands with safe water and soap before eating or handling food and after toilet use; use alcohol-based sanitizer when water is limited.
guideData.fallbackOption: If water is scarce, prioritize sanitizer before eating and after toilet use, and wipe away visible dirt first.
steps: If you have safe water, wash hands with soap for at least 20 seconds and rinse well.
If no water is available, apply alcohol-based hand sanitizer and rub until dry.
Prepare food on the cleanest surface available and keep raw and cooked foods separate.
Wash utensils and dishes with safe water and soap if possible, then air-dry.
Store food away from waste and animals.
warnings: Dirty hands are a major source of gastrointestinal illness in the field.
Some germs are harder to remove with sanitizer alone; soap and water are preferred when available.
whatNotToDo: Do not prepare or eat food immediately after toileting or handling waste without cleaning your hands.
Do not use clearly contaminated water on food-contact items if you can avoid it.
redFlags: Persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or inability to keep fluids down.
Signs of dehydration such as dizziness, confusion, or very low urine output.
preparednessTips: Pack travel-sized soap, alcohol-based sanitizer, and resealable waste bags.
Include lightweight biodegradable soap for dishes and basic washing.
Practice hygiene habits at home so they are automatic in the field.

sourceReferences.title: When and How to Wash Your Hands
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong general handwashing guidance applicable in emergency and field settings.

sourceReferences.title: Camping Hygiene: Tips for Staying Clean Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical limited-water outdoor hygiene guidance.

guides.title: When It’s Better to Risk Untreated Water

guideData.title: When It’s Better to Risk Untreated Water
guideData.summary: If you are at high risk of life-threatening dehydration and have no way to treat water, drinking the best available untreated water may be safer than not drinking at all.
guideData.whenToUse: In extreme situations where you cannot treat water and are showing signs of severe dehydration.
guideData.preferredOption: Choose the cleanest-appearing source available and drink enough to stabilize, understanding the infection risk.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you can delay briefly, rest, reduce exertion, and see whether you can improvise treatment before deciding.
steps: Assess severe dehydration signs such as dry mouth, minimal urine, dizziness, confusion, or inability to keep walking.
Confirm you truly cannot treat water with fire, fuel, filter, tablets, or safe containers.
Select the least contaminated source available.
Drink slowly at first, especially if nauseated.
After rescue or reaching care, tell medical personnel that you drank untreated water.
warnings: This is a high-risk last-resort choice and can lead to gastrointestinal illness or infection.
Children, older adults, pregnant people, and those with chronic illness may be more vulnerable to waterborne disease.
whatNotToDo: Do not drink clearly hazardous water with visible chemicals, strong fuel odor, or strong sewage smell unless the alternative is likely immediate death from dehydration.
Do not assume a small sip can test safety; pathogens are not detectable by taste.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, blood in stool, fever, abdominal pain, or worsening dehydration after drinking suspect water.
preparednessTips: Always carry a basic water treatment method on outdoor trips.
Store emergency water at home for several days per person.
Learn local emergency guidance on boil-water advisories and chemical spills.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Provides the nuanced risk-benefit context behind last-resort untreated water decisions.

guides.title: Collecting and Storing Water Safely Before Treatment

guideData.title: Collecting and Storing Water Safely Before Treatment
guideData.summary: Use the cleanest container you have, avoid dipping with dirty hands or gear, and protect stored water from re-contamination.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you gather water from natural or emergency sources before you can purify or disinfect it.
guideData.preferredOption: Fill dedicated water containers directly from the source or by pouring, minimize contact with dirty surfaces, and separate untreated from treated water.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack proper containers, improvise with food-grade bottles, cooking pots, or clean plastic bags and keep them away from waste and fuel.
steps: Rinse containers with source water if they held something else and empty them fully.
Fill containers directly or pour from a cleaner cup or pot rather than dipping with dirty hands.
Keep untreated water separate from treated water.
Cover containers tightly to keep out dirt and insects.
Store in shade if possible and away from fuel, chemicals, or latrine areas.
Treat the water as soon as practical by boiling, filtration, or chemicals.
warnings: Cross-contamination between treated and untreated water is a common failure point.
Some improvised containers may leach chemicals and should not be used.
whatNotToDo: Do not store untreated water in containers that recently held fuel, pesticides, or unknown chemicals.
Do not top off treated water with untreated water without re-treating it.
redFlags: Multiple people develop gastrointestinal illness after drinking from a shared source.
Fuel smell or chemical taste suggesting chemical contamination.
preparednessTips: Include sturdy bottles, a collapsible container, and a treatment method in your emergency kit.
Practice using your filter, tablets, or other methods before an emergency.
For home emergencies, follow public guidance on storing tap water in clean containers.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Technical and evidence-based field water handling and treatment principles.

guides.title: Choosing the Safest Available Water Source

guideData.title: Choosing the Safest Available Water Source
guideData.summary: Pick the clearest, most upstream, and least contaminated-looking source you can, then plan to treat it.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you must collect natural water for drinking or hygiene in the field.
guideData.preferredOption: Select flowing water from higher, cleaner-looking locations and avoid sources near livestock, sewage, or heavy human activity.
guideData.fallbackOption: If only poor-quality sources are available, choose the least bad option and treat it as thoroughly as your tools allow.
steps: Look for flowing water rather than stagnant pools and avoid water with strong odor, scum, or dead animals.
Collect from upstream of camps, trails, or livestock areas whenever possible.
Prefer water that is clear to the eye; if silty, allow sediment to settle before treatment.
Avoid collecting from beaver ponds, irrigation channels, or near latrines and wastewater discharge.
Treat the water before drinking whenever possible.
warnings: Even clear high-mountain water can contain microorganisms.
Contamination risk is higher downstream of agriculture, grazing, or human settlement.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume natural equals safe.
Do not drink from obviously contaminated sources unless you face life-threatening dehydration and have no alternative.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, or dehydration after drinking untreated water.
Known compromise of a public or shared supply after a disaster.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one reliable treatment method on extended trips.
Learn local guidance about water safety in your destination.
Bring enough stored water for the first day where feasible.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based medical guidance supporting conservative field water collection and treatment decisions.

guides.title: Ground, Fire, and Passive Visual Signals

guideData.title: Ground, Fire, and Passive Visual Signals
guideData.summary: Create big, simple, high-contrast shapes on the ground and use bright materials or smoke so rescuers can spot you from air or ground.
guideData.whenToUse: When you are staying put or moving slowly and want to attract aircraft, drones, or distant ground searchers.
guideData.preferredOption: Use large ground symbols and bright passive markers, and use fire or smoke only when safe and legal.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot make fire or large symbols, hang bright clothing or reflective materials in visible places and keep your area obvious.
steps: Choose an open area visible from above or at a distance if possible.
Use rocks, logs, or gear to make letters or shapes such as SOS, X, or a large arrow at least several feet wide.
If fire is safe and allowed, build small signal fires and be ready to add green material for smoke when searchers are near.
Drape bright clothing or a reflective blanket over rocks or trees to create strong contrast.
At night, group three lights or fires in a line or triangle to signal distress.
Maintain signals and check that they remain visible.
warnings: Fire signaling can be dangerous and may be illegal or reckless in dry or windy conditions.
Smoke can worsen breathing or hide your position if poorly placed.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in high wind, extreme dryness, or near flammable brush.
Do not create cluttered ground markings; simple, large shapes work best.
redFlags: If you see aircraft or hear searchers, focus on boosting visibility quickly.
If fire escapes your control or wind shifts dangerously, move away immediately and reassess.
preparednessTips: Carry a reflective emergency blanket and high-visibility clothing.
Pack at least one reliable fire-starting method if local regulations and wildfire risk allow signal fires.
Learn your area’s fire restrictions before depending on open-flame signaling.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative guide mentioning fires, smoke, mirrors, and whistles for signaling.

sourceReferences.title: How to Signal for Help in the Wild
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical advice on three fires, ground symbols, and layered signaling methods.

guides.title: Simple Survival Priorities: What Comes First
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan

guideData.title: Simple Survival Priorities: What Comes First
guideData.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guideData.summary: For most short-term emergencies, focus on safety, first aid, shelter or warmth, signaling, and then water; food and complex tasks can wait.
guideData.whenToUse: When making a quick plan for the next hours after becoming stranded or after an emergency.
guideData.preferredOption: Follow a simple order: safety and severe injuries, shelter and temperature, signaling and communication, water, then food and extras.
guideData.fallbackOption: If conditions are mild, adjust the order slightly but still avoid spending early energy on low-value tasks like foraging or elaborate construction.
steps: Check airway, breathing, severe bleeding, and immediate hazards first.
Get out of wind, rain, or direct sun and adjust clothing to avoid hypothermia or heat illness.
Use phone, whistle, mirror, bright clothing, or ground signals early to make yourself findable.
Identify the safest available water source and plan how to treat it.
Conserve energy rather than hunting, foraging, or building complex structures in most short-term events.
warnings: Hypothermia or heat illness can kill in hours, long before hunger.
Treating water is important, but usually less urgent than staying warm or cool.
whatNotToDo: Do not spend your first hour on complex shelters or food gathering if you lack basic shelter or signaling.
Do not ignore obvious hazards while focused on secondary tasks.
Do not assume you must find food immediately.
redFlags: Signs of hypothermia or severe heat illness.
Ongoing severe bleeding, chest pain, difficulty breathing, or altered consciousness.
preparednessTips: Learn basic first aid and CPR through Red Cross or similar training.
Pack an emergency kit that supports shelter, signaling, and water treatment first.
Practice short priority drills on day hikes.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Survival Priorities / Seven Priorities
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes common survival-priority frameworks around first aid, shelter, signaling, water, and food.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based guidance supporting conservative, realistic water priorities in the field.

guides.title: STOP-Think-Observe-Plan (First 30 Minutes)
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan

guideData.title: STOP-Think-Observe-Plan (First 30 Minutes)
guideData.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guideData.summary: Pause, calm down, check for injuries and hazards, then make a simple plan instead of walking blindly.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you realize you are lost, stranded, off-route, or unexpectedly stuck outdoors.
guideData.preferredOption: Use the STOP method, then act on the safest, simplest short plan.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot think clearly, focus only on immediate safety: move out of danger, treat obvious bleeding, get out of wind or rain, then rest and reassess.
steps: Stop in a safe spot away from immediate hazards such as rockfall, traffic, avalanche terrain, surf, or fire.
Breathe slowly for 30 to 60 seconds to reduce panic.
Check yourself and others quickly for serious bleeding, breathing problems, or inability to move.
Observe the time of day, weather, terrain, nearby landmarks, last known point, and any cell signal or GPS information.
Ask what the main threat is in the next few hours: cold, heat, darkness, dehydration, or injury.
Make a simple 15 to 30 minute plan such as staying put, improving shelter, and signaling for help.
If possible, text or call emergency services and a trusted contact with your situation and last known location.
Carry out the short plan, then pause and reassess.
warnings: Panic and aimless wandering are major causes of worsening lost-person outcomes.
Poor decisions made in the first 30 minutes can be hard to reverse in bad terrain or bad weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to do something if you are unsure where you are.
Do not ignore injuries or severe cold or heat to push on a bit more.
Do not rely only on your memory of the route if you are clearly disoriented.
redFlags: Severe bleeding, difficulty breathing, altered mental status, or inability to walk.
Rapidly worsening weather with poor shelter or warm or dry clothing.
Nightfall close at hand with no light, no clear route, or no shelter.
Immediate terrain hazards such as flooding, avalanche slopes, cliffs, or surf.
preparednessTips: Share a trip plan with a trusted person including route and return time.
Carry a whistle, map, compass, charged phone, power bank, and simple emergency kit.
Practice the STOP method on day hikes so it feels automatic under stress.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public guidance on what to do if you get lost, including stopping, assessing, and staying put.

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical guidance on staying found and staying put once lost.

sourceReferences.title: What should you do if you are lost during a hike
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Accessible STOP-based guidance for lost hikers.

guides.title: Weather-Related Red Flag Symptoms (Heat, Cold, Air, Lightning)
guides.parentTopic: When Weather Exposure Becomes a Medical Emergency

guideData.title: Weather-Related Red Flag Symptoms (Heat, Cold, Air, Lightning)
guideData.parentTopic: When Weather Exposure Becomes a Medical Emergency
guideData.summary: Confusion, chest pain, trouble breathing, fainting, seizures, or loss of consciousness during weather exposure are medical emergencies.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time someone becomes very unwell during extreme heat or cold, smoke or dust exposure, storms, or after a lightning strike.
guideData.preferredOption: Recognize these signs quickly, move to the relevant emergency action guide, and call for help immediately.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot call right away, begin first aid and get help from others nearby as fast as possible.
steps: Check for red-flag symptoms such as confusion, inability to walk or talk normally, chest pain, severe breathing difficulty, seizures, or unresponsiveness.
In heat, suspect heat stroke if the person is very hot, confused, or collapsed and call emergency services while cooling.
In cold, suspect severe hypothermia if the person is very cold, confused, or not shivering and call for help while warming gently.
In smoke or dust, treat severe shortness of breath, chest tightness, or blue lips as emergencies.
After lightning, treat all strikes as emergencies even if the person initially seems okay.
warnings: Trying to tough it out with these symptoms can lead to death or permanent disability.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay calling for help to see whether severe symptoms go away.
Do not rely only on over-the-counter medications when severe symptoms are present.
redFlags: Confusion, collapse, chest pain, severe trouble breathing, seizures, or unresponsiveness after weather exposure.
preparednessTips: Build a unified Red Flags screen in the app that links to emergency action cards and local emergency contacts.
Allow a quick tap from any weather guide into this red-flag reference.

sourceReferences.title: Heat-related Illnesses
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emergency heat-illness features and actions.

sourceReferences.title: Heat Cramps, Exhaustion, Stroke
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear emergency criteria for severe heat illness.

sourceReferences.title: What Is Hypothermia and Frostbite?
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Cold emergency signs summarized from CDC content.

sourceReferences.title: Environmental and Health Impacts of Air Pollution: A Review
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Serious cardiorespiratory impacts of pollution and particulate exposure.

guides.title: Basic Altitude Illness Prevention for Trekkers

guideData.title: Basic Altitude Illness Prevention for Trekkers
guideData.summary: Ascend gradually, include rest days, stay hydrated, avoid alcohol and overexertion, and stop or descend if symptoms appear.
guideData.whenToUse: When planning or beginning treks, climbs, or travel above roughly 2,500 to 3,000 meters, especially if you are not acclimatized.
guideData.preferredOption: Limit sleeping-altitude gain, schedule acclimatization days, and adjust plans based on symptoms.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must ascend faster, reduce exertion, monitor symptoms closely, and be ready to stop or descend early.
steps: Above roughly 3,000 meters, plan no more than about 300 to 500 meters increase in sleeping altitude per day and include rest days every few days.
Maintain steady hydration and eat regular nutritious meals.
Avoid alcohol and sedatives, especially at night.
Watch closely for headache, nausea, unusual fatigue, sleep disruption, or worsening symptoms and stop ascending if they intensify.
warnings: Rapid ascent increases the risk of altitude illness, which can become life-threatening if ignored.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore persistent or worsening symptoms while ascending.
Do not continue to higher sleeping altitude if you already have clear signs of altitude sickness.
redFlags: Severe headache, repeated vomiting, trouble walking straight, shortness of breath at rest, or altered behavior at altitude.
preparednessTips: Use region-specific altitude profiles and conservative ascent plans for routes such as the Himalaya.
Encourage pre-trip consultation with experienced providers about medications and higher-risk conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Health and Safety Tips for High Altitude Sickness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical trekking-oriented prevention advice.

guides.title: Everyday Sun Protection and UV Safety
guides.parentTopic: Sun Exposure and UV-Related Heat Stress

guideData.title: Everyday Sun Protection and UV Safety
guideData.parentTopic: Sun Exposure and UV-Related Heat Stress
guideData.summary: Limit midday sun, cover skin, use high-SPF sunscreen, and protect your eyes to reduce UV damage and heat stress.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you will be outdoors for more than brief periods, especially around midday, at high altitude, or near reflective surfaces.
guideData.preferredOption: Use shade, clothing, hats, sunglasses, and sunscreen together and move activities to lower-UV times where possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If sunscreen or full clothing is not available, prioritize shade, hats, and limiting time in direct sun.
steps: Plan outdoor activities for early morning or late afternoon when UV and heat are lower.
Wear long-sleeved tightly woven clothing, a broad-brimmed hat, and UV-blocking sunglasses.
Apply broad-spectrum high-SPF sunscreen to all exposed skin and reapply as directed.
Use shade whenever possible, especially during peak sun hours.
Drink water regularly to compensate for heat and sun exposure.
warnings: Excessive solar UV causes skin cancer, cataracts, and other eye damage and can contribute to heat stress.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on sunscreen alone without clothing and shade.
Do not use tanning as a way to protect your skin.
redFlags: Severe sunburn with large blisters, fever, chills, confusion, or signs of infection.
preparednessTips: Carry sunscreen, a hat, and sunglasses in outdoor kits.
Remind users that cloudy days can still have high UV.

sourceReferences.title: Health consequences of excessive solar UV radiation
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Risks and prevention recommendations for excessive UV exposure.

sourceReferences.title: Protect Yourself From the Dangers of Extreme Heat
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Includes sunscreen, clothing, and shade advice.

guides.title: Immediate Actions if Thunderstorm or Lightning Approaches Outdoors

guideData.title: Immediate Actions if Thunderstorm or Lightning Approaches Outdoors
guideData.summary: Get inside a substantial building or hard-topped metal vehicle immediately; if you cannot, move away from high points, isolated trees, and water.
guideData.whenToUse: At the first sound of thunder or sight of lightning when you are outdoors, especially in open areas or on high ground.
guideData.preferredOption: Stop outdoor activity and go to a fully enclosed building or a hard-topped vehicle with the windows closed.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no shelter or vehicle is available, move quickly to lower terrain away from tall objects and water, spread out if in a group, and minimize ground contact.
steps: At first thunder, stop swimming, boating, hiking on ridges, or field activity.
Head for the nearest substantial building with wiring and plumbing, or a hard-topped vehicle.
If caught on a ridge or open high ground, move downhill to lower terrain away from solitary trees and tall objects.
Get away from water, shorelines, metal fences, and railings.
warnings: Lightning can strike miles from the center of a storm; if you hear thunder, you are close enough to be struck.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter under isolated trees, in small open structures, or near tall metal objects.
Do not stay in or near bodies of water.
redFlags: Any lightning strike victim: call emergency services immediately and begin CPR if trained and needed.
preparednessTips: Follow the principle: when thunder roars, go indoors.
Plan escape routes from fields, beaches, lakes, and peaks.

sourceReferences.title: 7 lightning safety tips if you are caught outside during a thunderstorm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes NOAA-style recommendations on seeking shelter quickly.

guides.title: Child and Pet Safety Around Emergency Lighting

guideData.title: Child and Pet Safety Around Emergency Lighting
guideData.summary: Keep all flames and hot lights out of reach of children and pets, and teach kids to use flashlights safely instead of matches or candles.
guideData.whenToUse: When planning emergency lighting for homes with children or pets.
guideData.preferredOption: Use cool, stable LED lighting and assign a responsible adult to manage any higher-risk devices.
guideData.fallbackOption: If candles or fuel devices are unavoidable, keep them to one closely supervised area and keep children and pets elsewhere.
steps: Store matches, lighters, and fuel where children cannot access them.
Give children flashlights and teach them not to shine them in eyes or play with them as toys near hazards.
Keep pets out of rooms with open flames or hot lanterns.
Explain family rules about not touching lighting or heating devices.
warnings: Children and pets can easily knock over candles or lanterns and cause fires or burns.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave children alone with any open flame or fuel-burning device.
Do not let pets chew cords or trip over lighting setups.
redFlags: Burns, smoke inhalation, or a fire started by lighting devices.
preparednessTips: Practice lights-out drills that include safe behavior around emergency lighting.
Keep a dedicated flashlight in each child’s room for comfort during outages.

guides.title: Fuel-Burning Lanterns and Heaters: Last-Resort Guidance

guideData.title: Fuel-Burning Lanterns and Heaters: Last-Resort Guidance
guideData.summary: Avoid fuel-burning lanterns and heaters indoors; if you must use one, follow manufacturer instructions strictly, keep ventilation adequate, and never sleep with it running.
guideData.whenToUse: Only when no safer lighting or heating options exist and conditions are dangerous.
guideData.preferredOption: Replace fuel-burning devices with battery-powered alternatives whenever possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If a fuel device must be used, use it briefly in a ventilated area, keep children and pets away, and ensure CO detectors are working.
steps: Read and follow manufacturer warnings before use.
Use devices outdoors or in the safest ventilated space possible, away from combustibles.
Keep children and pets away from the device.
Turn the device off when leaving the room or going to sleep.
warnings: Fuel-burning devices can produce carbon monoxide and cause fires and burns.
Many agencies simply advise against indoor use of these devices.
whatNotToDo: Do not use unvented heaters or lanterns in small enclosed rooms.
Do not modify devices or use fuels not specified by the manufacturer.
redFlags: Any CO symptoms or alarm activation.
Flames outside the intended burner area or fuel leaks.
preparednessTips: Prioritize getting battery-powered alternatives so you rarely need this guide.
If you own a fuel-burning device, get local fire-authority guidance on safe use.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear combined generator and CO safety guidance for household use.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide Safety in Power Outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: State emergency guidance on CO and generator placement.

guides.title: Using Flashlights and Battery Lanterns Safely

guideData.title: Using Flashlights and Battery Lanterns Safely
guideData.summary: Use battery-powered lights on stable surfaces, away from water and heat, and keep spare batteries nearby; they are the safest lighting option in outages.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you need light during outages or in damaged buildings.
guideData.preferredOption: Use LED flashlights and lanterns and keep them dry, cool, and away from flammable materials.
guideData.fallbackOption: If batteries are scarce, share a central lantern and rotate flashlight use among household members.
steps: Place lanterns on flat, stable surfaces where they will not be knocked over.
Use headlamps when hands-free work is needed.
Keep lights away from open fuel containers, gas appliances, and water sources.
Store spare batteries in a cool, dry place and check them for leaks.
warnings: Improper charging of rechargeable lights can lead to overheating and fire.
Water contact with battery devices can cause malfunction and sometimes shock risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt to repair damaged battery packs or cracked housings yourself.
Do not leave lights charging on bedding, sofas, or other flammable surfaces.
redFlags: Smoke, unusual smell, or extreme heat from chargers or lighting devices.
preparednessTips: Keep at least one flashlight per person and one lantern per main room.
Include extra batteries and a safe charger in your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.whyUseful: Federal guidance on safe behavior during outages, surge protection, and communication.

guides.title: When to Leave and Not Return Until Professionally Inspected
guides.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes

guideData.title: When to Leave and Not Return Until Professionally Inspected
guideData.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes
guideData.summary: If a building has major structural, electrical, or gas damage, leave and do not return until authorities or qualified inspectors say it is safe.
guideData.whenToUse: After severe storms, earthquakes, explosions, or fires.
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate, stay elsewhere, and wait for official clearance.
guideData.fallbackOption: If staying nearby is unavoidable, stay outside or in an undamaged separate structure away from the affected building.
steps: Identify major damage such as partial collapse, large structural cracks, displaced foundation, or major roof failure.
Leave the building and move to a safe location.
Notify authorities, landlord, or insurance and request inspection.
Follow official orders regarding unsafe buildings or restricted areas.
warnings: Re-entering badly damaged buildings has caused injuries and deaths after disasters.
whatNotToDo: Do not camp or sleep inside or immediately next to a severely damaged building.
Do not try to shore up structural components yourself.
redFlags: Any new collapse, shifting, or severe cracking.
Fire or gas leaks from the building.
preparednessTips: Review your insurance and local inspection processes before disasters occur.

guides.title: Safe Behavior Inside a Damaged Building
guides.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes

guideData.title: Safe Behavior Inside a Damaged Building
guideData.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes
guideData.summary: If you must go inside a lightly damaged building, move slowly, avoid hazards, and be ready to leave immediately if anything seems unsafe.
guideData.whenToUse: When entering a building that appears mostly intact but may have minor damage.
guideData.preferredOption: Limit time inside to essential tasks, keep exits clear, and watch constantly for hazards.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you are unsure about safety, stay out and wait for inspection.
steps: Use a flashlight to check floors, stairs, and ceilings before stepping forward.
Avoid walking under visibly cracked beams, loose ceiling material, or leaning walls.
Do not step into standing water if electrical equipment, cords, or outlets are nearby.
Keep at least one clear exit path at all times.
Leave immediately if you smell gas, see sparks, or feel the building shift.
warnings: Hidden damage and aftershocks can turn minor damage into collapse risk.
Electrical faults and gas leaks can ignite fires inside damaged structures.
whatNotToDo: Do not move heavy debris or structural components yourself.
Do not use elevators after earthquakes or when power is unstable.
redFlags: New cracking, loud creaking, or falling debris.
Trapped or injured people inside beyond what you can safely reach.
preparednessTips: Store sturdy shoes and gloves in your emergency kit to protect feet and hands from debris.

sourceReferences.title: What You Need to Know When the Power Goes Out Unexpectedly
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical CDC guidance on immediate outage actions, safety, food, and preparedness.

guides.title: Checking if a Building Is Safe to Enter After a Disaster
guides.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes

guideData.title: Checking if a Building Is Safe to Enter After a Disaster
guideData.parentTopic: Damaged Buildings After Storms or Earthquakes
guideData.summary: Do an external safety check first: if you see major structural damage, leaning walls, sagging roof, gas smell, or downed lines, do not enter.
guideData.whenToUse: After storms, earthquakes, explosions, or similar damage, before going back inside a building.
guideData.preferredOption: Stay out of clearly damaged buildings and wait for official or professional inspection.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must enter briefly for a life-saving reason and it appears reasonably safe, minimize time inside and leave at the first sign of danger.
steps: Walk around the building from a safe distance and look for cracks, leaning walls, sagging roofs, broken windows, or partial collapse.
Look and listen for gas leaks, fires, electrical arcing, or rushing water.
Stay well away if downed power lines are on or near the building.
If nothing obvious is wrong, enter carefully while watching for falling debris, broken glass, and standing water.
Leave immediately if the building shifts, creaks loudly, sheds dust, or develops new cracks.
warnings: Hidden damage can cause sudden collapse even when a building looks mostly intact.
Aftershocks can worsen earthquake damage.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter buildings with visible major structural damage unless directed by trained rescuers.
Do not use candles or open flames where there may be gas leaks.
redFlags: Strong gas odor, hissing pipes, or visible fire.
Major cracks, leaning walls, or partial collapse.
Downed power lines on or near the structure.
preparednessTips: Know and follow local storm and earthquake re-entry guidance.
Document damage for insurance only after safety is confirmed.
