guides.title: When Not to Touch Utility Controls and Call the Utility Instead
guides.parentTopic: Gas Leaks and Utility Shutoff Basics

guideData.title: When Not to Touch Utility Controls and Call the Utility Instead
guideData.parentTopic: Gas Leaks and Utility Shutoff Basics
guideData.summary: If there is flooding, visible damage, fire, or gas smell near utility controls, do not touch them; evacuate and call the utility or emergency services instead.
guideData.whenToUse: After storms, earthquakes, fires, or flooding when meters, panels, or pipes may be damaged.
guideData.preferredOption: Stay away from damaged or wet utilities and leave shutoff and repair work to professionals.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot reach authorities and an immediate threat exists, prioritize evacuation and warning others nearby.
steps: Inspect from a safe distance for fallen lines, damaged meters, flooded panels, or broken pipes.
If any damage or water is present, do not approach or touch the equipment.
Keep people away and call utilities or emergency services.
Follow responder instructions and do not attempt temporary fixes.
warnings: Water and electricity together are deadly.
Gas and sparks can cause explosions.
whatNotToDo: Do not step in water near electrical equipment.
Do not use tools to poke, move, or manipulate damaged utility components.
redFlags: Any downed or low-hanging power line.
Arcing, buzzing, or flames around utility equipment.
Gas smell or hissing near meters or pipes.
preparednessTips: Save utility emergency numbers in your phone and post them at home.
Teach household members to treat all downed lines as live and extremely dangerous.

sourceReferences.title: What You Need to Know When the Power Goes Out Unexpectedly
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical CDC guidance on immediate outage actions, safety, food, and preparedness.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to a Gas Leak
guides.parentTopic: Gas Leaks and Utility Shutoff Basics

guideData.title: Recognizing and Responding to a Gas Leak
guideData.parentTopic: Gas Leaks and Utility Shutoff Basics
guideData.summary: If you smell gas or suspect a leak, leave the building immediately without using switches or phones inside, then call the gas company or emergency services from a safe location.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you smell rotten-egg odor, hear hissing near gas lines, or see damaged gas equipment after storms or earthquakes.
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate, avoid sparks, and report the leak; let trained professionals handle repairs and any needed shutoff.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have been taught how and local utility guidance allows it, you may shut off gas from outside only if it is clearly safe; otherwise leave and call.
steps: Do not turn lights or appliances on or off and do not use flames.
Leave the building immediately and take others with you.
From outside and away from the suspected leak, call your gas utility emergency line or emergency services.
Stay away until professionals say it is safe.
warnings: Gas leaks can cause explosions and fires.
Even small sparks can ignite gas-air mixtures.
whatNotToDo: Do not use phones inside the building.
Do not adjust thermostats or electrical switches.
Do not try to locate the exact leak source yourself.
Do not re-enter until cleared by professionals.
redFlags: Strong gas smell indoors or near meters or appliances.
Hissing or blowing sounds near gas lines.
Dead vegetation near buried gas lines with no clear cause.
preparednessTips: Store your gas utility emergency number in your phone and post it at home.
Have gas appliances inspected regularly by qualified technicians.

sourceReferences.title: What You Need to Know When the Power Goes Out Unexpectedly
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical CDC guidance on immediate outage actions, safety, food, and preparedness.

guides.title: Preparing Your Kitchen for Future Outages
guides.parentTopic: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages

guideData.title: Preparing Your Kitchen for Future Outages
guideData.parentTopic: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages
guideData.summary: Equip your kitchen with thermometers, ice, coolers, and shelf-stable foods so you can keep food safe during outages.
guideData.whenToUse: During preparedness planning, before storms, or before any period when outages are likely.
guideData.preferredOption: Stock thermometers, frozen water, coolers, and shelf-stable foods and keep your fridge and freezer at safe temperatures year-round.
guideData.fallbackOption: If resources are limited, prioritize one reliable thermometer and a basic stock of shelf-stable foods.
steps: Install appliance thermometers in the refrigerator and freezer and keep them at or below safe target temperatures.
Store ice packs and frozen water containers in the freezer to help hold temperatures during outages.
Keep at least one cooler large enough for key perishables.
Build a 3-day supply of shelf-stable foods that do not require refrigeration.
Rotate supplies periodically before expiration.
warnings: If you do not track normal appliance temperatures, it is much harder to judge safety during outages.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until a storm is imminent to buy all supplies.
Do not overload your freezer so tightly that air cannot circulate.
redFlags: Persistent refrigerator or freezer temperature problems even when power is on.
preparednessTips: Combine kitchen planning with overall emergency kit planning for water, medicines, and lighting.
Teach all household members the basic outage food-safety rules.

guides.title: Keeping Food Safe During a Power Outage
guides.parentTopic: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages

guideData.title: Keeping Food Safe During a Power Outage
guideData.parentTopic: Refrigerator, Freezer, and Food Safety During Outages
guideData.summary: Keep fridge and freezer doors closed; a refrigerator keeps food safe about 4 hours, and a full freezer 24–48 hours, if doors stay shut.
guideData.whenToUse: As soon as power goes out and through the first day of an outage.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep refrigerator doors shut, minimize freezer opening, and move perishables to coolers with ice only if the outage will exceed safe timelines.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack ice or coolers, keep doors closed and plan to discard perishable refrigerated food after 4 hours without power.
steps: Check that fridge and freezer temperatures are at safe ranges using appliance thermometers if you have them.
Immediately stop opening the refrigerator; use a cooler for any needed items if available.
Remember the main timelines: about 4 hours for an unopened fridge, about 48 hours for a full unopened freezer, and about 24 hours for a half-full freezer.
If the outage will likely last longer than 4 hours, move critical perishables to a cooler with ice if you can keep them at or below 4 C / 40 F.
warnings: Food in the danger zone above 4 C / 40 F for more than 2 hours can cause foodborne illness.
Taste and smell are not reliable indicators of safety.
whatNotToDo: Do not sample questionable food to see if it is safe.
Do not refreeze food that has been above 4 C / 40 F for more than 2 hours.
Do not rely on cool outside air unless you can keep food at safe, monitored temperatures.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, or severe abdominal pain after eating food stored during an outage.
Dehydration symptoms in vulnerable household members.
preparednessTips: Keep appliance thermometers in fridge and freezer.
Store ice packs and frozen water bottles to buy extra time during outages.
Maintain a supply of shelf-stable foods that do not require refrigeration.

guides.title: Safe Use of Extension Cords and Avoiding Overloads

guideData.title: Safe Use of Extension Cords and Avoiding Overloads
guideData.summary: Use heavy-duty, undamaged cords rated for the load, plug them directly into outlets or generator sockets, and avoid daisy-chaining or overloading power strips.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you are powering devices from generators, distant outlets, or limited working circuits during or after outages.
guideData.preferredOption: Use one heavy-duty cord per major appliance or tool and connect only the devices you actually need.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the cord capacity is unclear, limit use to low-power devices until you can confirm ratings or get a safer setup.
steps: Check cord labels for amperage or wattage ratings and confirm they match the device.
Inspect for cuts, exposed wires, damaged plugs, or heat damage.
Plug extension cords directly into the outlet or generator; avoid chaining cords or strips together.
Keep cords uncoiled and away from standing water, doorways, and crush points.
Unplug by the plug, not by pulling the cable.
warnings: Overloaded or damaged cords can overheat and cause fire or electric shock.
Cords in water or on damp ground greatly increase shock risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not backfeed a generator into a home outlet.
Do not run cords under rugs, carpets, or bedding.
Do not use indoor-only cords outdoors.
redFlags: Smoke, melting-plastic smell, or scorch marks on cords, outlets, or power strips.
Tingling, shock, or burns when touching appliances, cords, or nearby metal.
Fire in cords or outlets.
preparednessTips: Buy at least one outdoor-rated heavy-duty extension cord sized for planned generator or high-load use.
Label and store cords with their ratings visible.
Teach family members not to overload strips or piggyback power bars.

guides.title: Charging Plans and Backup Power Options

guideData.title: Charging Plans and Backup Power Options
guideData.summary: Plan several safe ways to charge essential devices: mains power when available, power banks, vehicle charging, and if needed, generators or solar chargers used safely.
guideData.whenToUse: When preparing your household for extended outages or during multi-day blackouts.
guideData.preferredOption: Use certified power banks and safe wall chargers while power is available; during outages, rotate charging from vehicle power or properly placed generators.
guideData.fallbackOption: If capacity is limited, designate one emergency phone and share charging in a planned rotation.
steps: Identify essential devices such as one phone, a radio, and any battery-backed medical equipment.
Fully charge devices and power banks before storms or likely outages.
If using a generator, keep it outdoors and connect devices directly with appropriate cords.
If using a vehicle, run it only outdoors away from enclosed spaces and use built-in charging outlets safely.
Schedule charging windows so everyone gets some battery and one phone remains available for emergency contact.
warnings: Improper generator or vehicle use can cause deadly carbon-monoxide poisoning.
Overloading outlets or daisy-chaining power strips increases fire risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not connect a generator directly to home wiring unless a qualified electrician installed a transfer switch.
Do not run vehicles in garages or attached carports, even with doors open.
Do not rely on cheap or uncertified power banks that overheat.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, or collapse while a generator or vehicle is running nearby.
Electrical smells, smoke, or sparking from chargers, outlets, cords, or power banks.
preparednessTips: Buy quality power banks, vehicle chargers, and if appropriate a properly sized generator before emergencies.
Practice your charging rotation plan during a short test outage.
Label which outlets and cables are safe to use with generator or vehicle power.

guides.title: Phone Battery Conservation in Emergencies

guideData.title: Phone Battery Conservation in Emergencies
guideData.summary: Turn your phone into a low-power emergency tool: dim the screen, enable battery saver, close power-hungry apps, and use it mainly for key calls and messages.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever power may be out for more than a few hours or charging is uncertain.
guideData.preferredOption: Enable battery-saver mode, reduce screen use, and prioritize SMS or critical calls over entertainment or background activity.
guideData.fallbackOption: If battery is very low, turn the phone off completely between necessary uses such as scheduled check-ins or emergency calls.
steps: Turn on battery saver or low-power mode.
Lower screen brightness and shorten screen timeout.
Turn off Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and GPS unless needed for a specific task.
Close apps that stream video, play games, or constantly refresh.
Prefer SMS or short calls over long voice or video sessions.
If reception is poor, use airplane mode between planned check-ins so the phone does not keep searching for signal.
warnings: Entertainment use can drain battery needed for emergency calls and alerts.
Damaged chargers, cables, or overheating batteries can create fire and shock risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave phones or power banks charging on beds, sofas, or other flammable surfaces.
Do not use damaged cables or chargers with exposed wires.
redFlags: Phone or power bank becomes very hot, swells, or smokes.
You cannot contact emergency services because the battery is dead during a safety or medical emergency.
preparednessTips: Keep phones and power banks charged when storms or outages are possible.
Store at least one high-capacity power bank per household and test it regularly.
Keep a paper list of key phone numbers in case your device fails.

guides.title: Safe Home Lighting During Blackouts

guideData.title: Safe Home Lighting During Blackouts
guideData.summary: Use flashlights and battery-powered lanterns for light; avoid candles whenever possible.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you need lighting during a blackout at home, work, or a shelter.
guideData.preferredOption: Rely on LED flashlights and lanterns, using the lowest brightness that still lets you move safely.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must use candles, treat them as a last resort: keep them in sturdy holders on clear, nonflammable surfaces and never leave them unattended.
steps: Gather flashlights and battery lanterns for each occupied room.
Use headlamps or small flashlights for hands-free tasks like cooking or first aid.
Place one lantern in a stable raised location to light shared space.
Keep spare batteries where you can find them in the dark.
If using a candle as a last resort, keep it well away from anything that can burn and extinguish it before sleep or leaving the room.
warnings: Candles are a leading cause of home fires during outages.
Improvised fuel-burning lights can cause burns, fires, and carbon-monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave candles or fuel-burning lanterns unattended.
Do not use open flames near oxygen equipment, curtains, bedding, or paper.
Do not hang lanterns by cords where they can fall.
redFlags: Any flame that gets out of control or produces heavy smoke.
Burns that are deep, large, or involve the face, hands, genital area, or joints.
preparednessTips: Stock several LED flashlights or headlamps and long-life batteries for each household member.
Store at least one safe battery lantern per main living area.
Teach children never to play with matches, lighters, or candles.

guides.title: Deciding Whether to Stay or Go During a Blackout

guideData.title: Deciding Whether to Stay or Go During a Blackout
guideData.summary: Stay if the building is sound and you can manage temperature and medical needs safely; leave if heat, cold, damage, or health risks make staying unsafe.
guideData.whenToUse: After the first 30–60 minutes of an outage when it may last for hours or longer.
guideData.preferredOption: Shelter safely at home if there are no structural, fire, or health hazards and you can manage temperature, water, and food safely.
guideData.fallbackOption: If home is unsafe or you cannot support medical or temperature needs without power, relocate to an official shelter, cooling center, or a safe friend or family home if travel is safe.
steps: Check building safety first for structural damage, flooding, fire, and gas smells.
Consider weather and whether you can safely stay warm or cool using approved methods.
Review household medical needs such as refrigerated medications, oxygen, or powered equipment.
Listen for outage estimates and shelter announcements from authorities.
If staying, set up a safe central room with light, radio, water, and medications.
If leaving, take medications, IDs, water, food, and go-bags, then travel only if routes are safe.
warnings: Extreme indoor heat or cold can become life-threatening, especially for children, older adults, and people with chronic illness.
Unsafe heating or power improvisations indoors greatly increase fire and carbon-monoxide risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay in a visibly damaged or partially collapsed building.
Do not travel through floodwater, downed lines, or active severe weather just to reach more comfortable conditions.
Do not rely on candles, charcoal grills, or gas stoves for long-term lighting or heating.
redFlags: Indoor temperatures causing confusion, fainting, uncontrolled shivering, or breathing difficulty.
Medical devices cannot operate and no safe backup is available.
Authorities issue evacuation or shelter-elsewhere instructions.
preparednessTips: Know the locations of official shelters or cooling centers in advance.
Store extra blankets, warm clothing, and battery fans or hand fans based on your climate.
Keep copies of medical information and prescriptions in your go-bag.

guides.title: First 30 Minutes of a Power Outage

guideData.title: First 30 Minutes of a Power Outage
guideData.summary: Stay calm, check for hazards, protect appliances, keep cold food closed up, and switch to safe battery lighting while you confirm whether the outage is local or widespread.
guideData.whenToUse: As soon as power goes out unexpectedly at home or work.
guideData.preferredOption: Stabilize the situation first: check for fire, gas, wiring, or flood hazards; protect appliances; preserve cold food; and conserve batteries.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot move safely because of debris, water, or structural damage, stay in the safest room, use one flashlight, and contact authorities or neighbors if possible.
steps: Check immediately for fire, burning smell, sparks, downed lines outside, or gas smell; evacuate and call emergency services if any are present.
Confirm whether the outage is just your home or a wider area by checking outside, asking neighbors, or contacting building staff.
Turn off or unplug major appliances and electronics to reduce surge damage when power returns.
Leave one small light switched on so you notice when power comes back.
Use flashlights or battery lanterns for light and keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed.
Conserve phone battery and monitor official updates by radio, SMS, or trusted apps.
warnings: Power can return suddenly with damaging surges.
Opening cold appliances unnecessarily can lead to food spoilage and illness.
Moving through debris, broken glass, or water in the dark raises injury and shock risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not use candles if you can avoid them, especially around children, pets, or oxygen.
Do not touch downed power lines or anything touching them.
Do not start a generator inside any enclosed or partially enclosed space.
Do not repeatedly open fridges or freezers just to check them.
redFlags: Arcing, sparks, or fire in wiring, outlets, or appliances.
Gas smell, hissing near gas lines, or suspected gas leak.
A downed or low-hanging power line nearby.
Electric shock, loss of consciousness, or breathing difficulty.
preparednessTips: Keep at least one flashlight and spare batteries in an easy-to-reach place in each main room.
Store an emergency radio, power bank, and printed emergency contacts including utility emergency numbers.
Use appliance thermometers so you can judge fridge and freezer safety quickly.
