guides.title: When to Seek Public Shelters or Warming Centers
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities

guideData.title: When to Seek Public Shelters or Warming Centers
guideData.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guideData.summary: If you cannot keep your home above a safe temperature or must consider unsafe heating methods, go to a public shelter or warming center if travel is safe.
guideData.whenToUse: Extended power outages, fuel shortages, or severe cold that overwhelm safe heating options at home.
guideData.preferredOption: Monitor local alerts for shelter or warming center locations and go early before conditions worsen.
guideData.fallbackOption: If travel is unsafe, use cold-home strategies while staying in contact with neighbors or authorities.
steps: Follow local emergency alerts for shelter or warming center information.
Decide early whether your home can remain safely habitable.
Gather essential supplies such as medications, IDs, and warm clothing and move to the shelter if travel is safe.
Check on neighbors and vulnerable people who may also need help.
At the shelter, follow staff instructions and keep your personal emergency kit with you.
warnings: Waiting too long can make travel more dangerous and reduce space at shelters.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay in an unheated home and resort to unsafe devices instead of going to a shelter.
Do not ignore official evacuation or shelter guidance.
redFlags: Household members showing hypothermia signs or indoor temperatures approaching freezing with no safe heat source.
preparednessTips: Know in advance how local authorities announce shelters and where common locations are.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Encourages planning for shelters and checking on others.

sourceReferences.title: How to Heat Your Home Safely as Cold Weather Sets In
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses safer alternatives and community support.

guides.title: Prioritizing Tasks in the First 30 Minutes of Exposure
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities

guideData.title: Prioritizing Tasks in the First 30 Minutes of Exposure
guideData.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guideData.summary: Use your first 30 minutes to get out of wind and rain, adjust clothing, protect hands and feet, and secure shelter; leave advanced fire tasks for later.
guideData.whenToUse: Onset of any cold exposure emergency such as getting lost, being stranded, or the start of a power outage.
guideData.preferredOption: Follow a simple order: location safety, shelter, clothing/layers, insulation, then warmth/food and communication.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must move because of immediate danger, prioritize reaching safer terrain and minimum shelter before anything else.
steps: Move away from immediate hazards such as rising water, unstable structures, or avalanche paths.
Get under any available shelter.
Adjust or change clothing to a drier layered setup; protect head, hands, and feet.
Insulate from the ground using whatever you have.
Only then think about fire, additional insulation, or signaling for help.
warnings: Poor early decisions such as staying in hazard areas or ignoring wet clothing quickly make later survival tasks harder.
whatNotToDo: Do not wander aimlessly in storms.
Do not spend your first half hour on low-priority tasks while still exposed.
redFlags: Immediate life threats such as rising water, structural collapse, or serious injury take precedence over warmth decisions.
preparednessTips: Mentally rehearse a simple first-30-minutes checklist for your climate and activities and share it with family or group members.

sourceReferences.title: Cold-weather and severe-weather safety principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: App-friendly checklist synthesized from multiple cold-exposure and severe-weather guidance sources.

guides.title: Deciding When Shelter Matters More Than Fire
guides.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities

guideData.title: Deciding When Shelter Matters More Than Fire
guideData.parentTopic: Shelter vs Fire vs Clothing: Choosing Priorities
guideData.summary: In most cold emergencies, getting under shelter and into dry, insulated clothing is more reliable and safer than trying to build a fire.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever you are exposed to cold and considering spending time and energy making a fire.
guideData.preferredOption: Prioritize wind and rain protection, dry clothing, and insulation from the ground first; treat fire as a supplement.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no shelter or adequate clothing is possible, then consider a carefully chosen, safe fire as an additional heat source where allowed.
steps: Assess wind, wetness, and available shelter options.
Secure shelter and dry layers first, even if simple.
Once reasonably protected, decide whether a fire adds enough benefit to justify the risk and effort.
If you choose to build a fire, follow fire-safety guidance closely.
warnings: Overemphasizing fire starting can waste critical time and energy while you continue losing heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay getting under shelter or changing out of wet clothes because you are focused on getting a fire going.
redFlags: Ongoing shivering, confusion, or clumsiness despite shelter and clothing improvements.
preparednessTips: Practice quick shelter setups and carry proper clothing so you do not depend on fire as a primary survival tool.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes staying dry and sheltered.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Highlights clothing, shelter, and moisture management over fire.

guides.title: Recognizing and Responding to Carbon Monoxide Exposure

guideData.title: Recognizing and Responding to Carbon Monoxide Exposure
guideData.summary: Treat any CO alarm as an emergency: get everyone into fresh air immediately, then call emergency services from a safe location.
guideData.whenToUse: Whenever a CO alarm activates or you suspect CO even without an alarm.
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate everyone outdoors, including pets, and call emergency services from outside.
guideData.fallbackOption: If mobility issues or blocked exits make full evacuation difficult, move toward the nearest fresh air while waiting for help.
steps: Do not ignore the alarm; stop what you are doing.
Get everyone out immediately using the quickest safe route.
Call emergency services and, if advised, your gas or utility company from outside.
Do not re-enter until authorities say it is safe.
Arrange inspection and repair of fuel-burning appliances and vents before using them again.
warnings: Opening windows instead of leaving may delay escape while CO levels remain dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not silence the alarm and continue normal activities.
Do not search for the source yourself while staying inside.
redFlags: Anyone collapses, has severe confusion, breathing trouble, or chest pain.
preparednessTips: Ensure everyone knows the CO alarm sound and escape routes.
Practice CO alarm drills the same way you practice fire drills.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide and Generator Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear combined generator and CO safety guidance for household use.

sourceReferences.title: Carbon Monoxide Safety in Power Outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: State emergency guidance on CO and generator placement.

sourceReferences.title: Generator Safety Fact Sheet
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative placement, fueling, and carbon-monoxide guidance for generators.

guides.title: Unsafe Indoor Heating Methods to Avoid

guideData.title: Unsafe Indoor Heating Methods to Avoid
guideData.summary: Never use generators, charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens to heat your home; they can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning and fires.
guideData.whenToUse: Any cold-weather emergency where people might feel tempted to improvise heat indoors.
guideData.preferredOption: Reject unsafe methods and instead rely on clothing, bedding, room sealing, and safe heaters or relocation to a warming center.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot stay warm without unsafe methods, move to a safer location if possible.
steps: Identify clearly unsafe indoor heat sources such as generators, camping stoves, charcoal or gas grills, and gas ovens left open.
Commit to not using them inside homes, garages, or near windows and vents.
Use staying-warm-without-power methods instead.
If others suggest unsafe heating, show them clear CO and fire warnings.
warnings: CO is invisible and odorless; many people have died using generators and fuel-burning devices indoors for heat.
whatNotToDo: Do not bring generators inside or run them near windows.
Do not use stoves or ovens for house heating.
Do not sleep in rooms with burning charcoal or unvented fuel devices.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, or confusion when a fuel device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Install CO detectors with battery backup and learn which devices are safe only outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Warns against using generators and grills indoors.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: CO dangers from fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Safe Options for Heating Indoors During Power Outages

guideData.title: Safe Options for Heating Indoors During Power Outages
guideData.summary: Use built-in heating systems, properly vented fireplaces or wood stoves, and listed portable electric heaters; avoid unvented fuel devices.
guideData.whenToUse: Cold-weather power outages or heating failures when you must stay in place.
guideData.preferredOption: Rely on safe, code-compliant heating systems plus room sealing and clothing layers.
guideData.fallbackOption: Use portable electric heaters on generator power only if the generator is outside and all heater safety steps are followed.
steps: Use central heating if it still works safely; otherwise move to a small room and focus on insulation and clothing.
If using a fireplace or wood stove, ensure the chimney or flue is clear and keep combustibles three feet away.
Use only modern listed electric space heaters indoors and follow heater safety rules.
If powering heaters by generator, keep the generator outdoors away from windows and vents and use proper cords.
Monitor carbon monoxide and smoke alarms and never block vents or flues.
warnings: Improper heating is a major cause of home fires and carbon monoxide poisoning during outages and cold spells.
whatNotToDo: Do not use charcoal grills, camp stoves, or gas ovens for space heating.
Do not bring generators, patio heaters, or gas heaters indoors.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion during heater use.
Any signs of fire or smoke from heating equipment.
preparednessTips: Have heating systems and chimneys inspected before winter and maintain working smoke and CO detectors.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Indoor heating and CO safety tips.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Safe heater and fireplace use.

guides.title: Fuel Storage and Handling During Emergencies
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)

guideData.title: Fuel Storage and Handling During Emergencies
guideData.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guideData.summary: Store fuels outside living areas in approved containers, away from heat sources, and never use them indoors for open burning.
guideData.whenToUse: Keeping gasoline, propane, or similar fuels on hand for generators, stoves, or vehicles during emergencies.
guideData.preferredOption: Follow local rules for approved containers and store fuel outside the home, upright, sealed, and away from ignition sources.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you must temporarily keep small quantities closer, ensure containers are closed, upright, and away from heat and sparks.
steps: Use only approved, labeled fuel containers.
Store fuels outside living spaces in a shed or detached garage if possible.
Keep containers upright, sealed, and out of direct sunlight or high heat.
When refueling generators or stoves, turn them off and let them cool first.
Clean spills immediately and ventilate the area.
warnings: Improper storage raises risks of fire, explosion, and toxic fumes including carbon monoxide when fuels are burned.
whatNotToDo: Do not store fuel next to heaters, fireplaces, or cooking appliances.
Do not use gasoline or similar fuels to start indoor fires or stoves.
redFlags: Strong fuel odors inside the home, visible leaks, or any ignition of spilled fuel.
preparednessTips: Plan fuel needs realistically, avoid hoarding, and learn local storage and transport rules.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Notes CO risks from fuel-burning devices.

guides.title: Safe Use of Portable Heaters Indoors
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)

guideData.title: Safe Use of Portable Heaters Indoors
guideData.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guideData.summary: Keep space heaters three feet from anything that can burn, plug them directly into wall outlets, and turn them off when you sleep or leave the room.
guideData.whenToUse: Using electric or fuel-fired portable heaters during cold weather or power shortages.
guideData.preferredOption: Use modern, certified heaters with tip-over and overheat protection exactly as directed.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack safe heaters, rely on clothing, blankets, and room-sealing techniques rather than unsafe heat sources.
steps: Place the heater on a flat, stable, nonflammable surface.
Keep a three-foot kid- and pet-free zone around it.
Plug electric heaters directly into a wall outlet, never a power strip or extension cord.
Turn heaters off and unplug them when leaving the room or going to sleep.
Install and test smoke alarms and carbon monoxide detectors where appropriate.
warnings: Portable heaters are a leading cause of home heating fires and related deaths.
whatNotToDo: Do not use damaged heaters, run cords under rugs, use heaters near water, or use outdoor fuel heaters indoors.
redFlags: Burning smells, sparks, discolored outlets, or any small fire in the heater or nearby materials.
preparednessTips: Inspect heaters before cold seasons and keep alarms working with backup power.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Key portable-heater safety rules.

sourceReferences.title: Safety Tips: Portable Space Heaters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical heater safety advice.

guides.title: Fully Extinguishing Outdoor Fires
guides.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)

guideData.title: Fully Extinguishing Outdoor Fires
guideData.parentTopic: Fire Safety and Fuel Management (Outdoor and Indoor)
guideData.summary: Drown the fire with water, stir the ashes, and repeat until everything is cold to the touch; never leave warm or smoking material behind.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you are done with an outdoor fire or must leave it unattended.
guideData.preferredOption: Use plenty of water and stirring to cool all coals, not just visible flames.
guideData.fallbackOption: If water is scarce, use dirt or sand while mixing thoroughly, then check for remaining heat; avoid relying on soil alone in peat or root systems.
steps: Let the fire burn down to ash and small coals if time allows.
Pour water over the entire fire area, including logs and surrounding ground.
Stir with a stick or tool, exposing hidden coals; add more water until hissing stops and all material is cool.
If using dirt or sand, mix thoroughly and check again for heat.
Feel carefully near the surface; if it is warm, repeat the cooling steps.
warnings: Poorly extinguished fires can smolder and later ignite wildfires, especially in dry or windy conditions.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave coals or large unburned logs.
Do not bury a hot fire under thick soil without confirming it is cold.
redFlags: Smoke or flames reappearing after you thought the fire was out and beginning to spread beyond your control.
preparednessTips: Carry enough water for extinguishing or avoid fires altogether when water is limited and fire risk is high.

sourceReferences.title: Wildfire-prevention fire-out guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Conservative extinguishing practices consistent with park and wildfire-prevention messaging.

guides.title: When Not to Attempt a Fire in Wet Conditions

guideData.title: When Not to Attempt a Fire in Wet Conditions
guideData.summary: Skip building a fire if it is unsafe, banned, or would distract from higher-priority tasks like shelter and getting dry.
guideData.whenToUse: Any wet, cold situation where building a fire is difficult or risky.
guideData.preferredOption: Focus first on shelter, dry clothing, insulation, and food; treat fire as a backup tool only when conditions and safety allow.
guideData.fallbackOption: Use chemical heat packs, movement if safe, or shared insulation if available.
steps: Check local fire restrictions and evaluate wildfire risk.
Assess your skills, tools, and available dry tinder and fuel honestly.
If risk or difficulty is high, spend your time improving shelter, clothing, and bedding instead.
Maintain morale and body temperature with movement, warm drinks if possible, and social support.
warnings: Spending hours trying to start a fire in harsh conditions can leave you colder, wetter, and more exhausted.
whatNotToDo: Do not try risky improvisations with accelerants or unknown fuels out of frustration.
redFlags: You are wet, cold, unable to stop shivering, and lack safe fire or better shelter options.
preparednessTips: Plan so you rarely depend on fire alone: carry proper clothing, shelter, and emergency warming supplies.

sourceReferences.title: Hypothermia prevention principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing shelter, dryness, and insulation over marginal fire attempts.

guides.title: Building a Fire Lay That Survives Rain and Snow

guideData.title: Building a Fire Lay That Survives Rain and Snow
guideData.summary: Raise the fire off soaked ground, protect it from above, and stack fuel so it can dry and catch as the fire grows.
guideData.whenToUse: When the ground is wet or snow-covered but you still need and are allowed to build a fire.
guideData.preferredOption: Create a small platform of sticks or rocks, build your tinder and kindling on top, and use overhead cover only if it can be kept well away from flames.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot provide overhead cover, build a compact fire with a windward log wall and keep it small and controlled.
steps: Lay a base of small sticks or rocks to raise the fire off wet ground.
Place tinder on the platform and arrange kindling in a teepee or log-cabin style.
Stack larger damp fuel nearby so it can pre-dry from the heat once the fire is established.
If using a tarp or poncho for rain protection, keep it high and far enough from heat to avoid melting or ignition.
Keep the fire small and compact for better heat and fuel efficiency.
warnings: Using overhead plastic or fabric covers near open flame is risky and must be constantly supervised.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in wet or windy conditions just because they are harder to start.
redFlags: Spreading flames into nearby vegetation or inability to control the fire due to wind or fuel conditions.
preparednessTips: Practice building small, efficient fires in safe settings before relying on them in emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Practical field fire-lay techniques
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common wet-ground and wet-weather fire-lay techniques taught in field courses.

guides.title: Finding and Using Dry Tinder in Wet Weather

guideData.title: Finding and Using Dry Tinder in Wet Weather
guideData.summary: Look in sheltered places for the driest fine material, and use more tinder than usual to overcome dampness.
guideData.whenToUse: Rain, snow, or soaked ground make normal fire starting difficult, but a fire is still safe and allowed.
guideData.preferredOption: Use carried tinder plus the driest inner wood or bark you can process into fine shavings.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no carried tinder, collect dry inner wood, dead standing twigs, and fibrous bark and process them into thin curls or fibers.
steps: Check your kit first for dry tinder stored in waterproof packaging.
Look under logs, under overhangs, or inside dead standing wood for dry material.
Shave or scrape dry inner wood or bark into thin curls or fibers.
Build a large tinder bundle, then surround it with very fine kindling.
Shield your tinder from rain with your body, pack, or tarp while lighting.
warnings: Fire starting in wet conditions is skill-intensive and may fail; do not rely on it as your only survival plan.
whatNotToDo: Do not strip bark from live trees in protected areas.
Do not waste your only ignition source on poorly prepared tinder.
redFlags: Repeated failed attempts while you are becoming colder and wetter.
preparednessTips: Always carry some dry tinder and a waterproof ignition source; do not assume you can improvise in all conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Field-oriented hypothermia prevention guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing dryness and shelter and acknowledges the difficulty of wet-weather fire starting.

guides.title: Choosing and Preparing Dry Fuel

guideData.title: Choosing and Preparing Dry Fuel
guideData.summary: Collect small, dead, and dry wood from off the ground and sheltered areas, and split larger pieces to expose drier inner wood if you have the skill and tools.
guideData.whenToUse: Any outdoor fire building, especially after rain or snow.
guideData.preferredOption: Gather a generous supply of small, dry fuel before lighting; prioritize dead branches from standing trees and sheltered spots.
guideData.fallbackOption: If most wood is damp, split thicker pieces to access drier cores and use extra tinder and kindling.
steps: Look for dead branches still on trees or under logs and rocks where they stayed drier.
Snap small twigs; if they break crisply, they are more likely to be dry enough.
Collect plenty of kindling and medium sticks before lighting.
If experienced and properly equipped, split thicker pieces to expose dry inner surfaces.
Keep your driest fuel under cover once collected.
warnings: Handling cutting tools when cold and tired increases injury risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not cut live trees or branches in protected areas.
Do not rely on a tiny fuel pile and assume you can gather more after dark.
redFlags: You cannot find or prepare enough dry fuel to stay warm and are already cold and wet.
preparednessTips: Carry a small amount of known-dry fuel or prepared tinder where wood may be scarce or wet.

sourceReferences.title: Fuel-selection strategies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely taught fuel-selection and preparation techniques for outdoor fires.

guides.title: Safe Fire Site Selection and Wind Management

guideData.title: Safe Fire Site Selection and Wind Management
guideData.summary: Choose an open, clear area away from vegetation, roots, and overhead branches, and account for wind so sparks and smoke blow away from people and shelter.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you plan to make an outdoor fire.
guideData.preferredOption: Use existing fire rings or designated sites whenever possible; otherwise clear down to mineral soil and keep a bare radius around the fire.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the ground cannot be cleared safely, skip the fire and rely on shelter, clothing, and hot drinks instead.
steps: Look up and around; avoid branches, tents, tarps, and dry grass.
Select bare soil, sand, or gravel if possible and clear a 1–2 meter radius of flammable material.
Note wind direction and keep sparks blowing into nonflammable areas.
Keep water, dirt, or sand nearby to extinguish the fire quickly.
Keep the fire small and manageable.
warnings: Wildfire risk can be extreme in dry or windy conditions; authorities may restrict all open flame.
whatNotToDo: Do not build fires in high winds, during fire bans, inside or under shelters, or directly on peat or organic soils.
redFlags: Rapidly moving grass fire, uncontrolled flames, or smoke threatening people or structures.
preparednessTips: Check local fire danger ratings and learn at least one warming method that does not rely on open flame.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-safety principles
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard fire-agency messaging on site selection, spark control, and fire bans.

guides.title: Overnight Survival in a Vehicle in Cold Weather

guideData.title: Overnight Survival in a Vehicle in Cold Weather
guideData.summary: Stay in the vehicle, insulate yourself, run the engine only periodically with a clear exhaust pipe, and avoid sleeping with continuous engine or heater use.
guideData.whenToUse: When stranded in a vehicle during winter storms or extreme cold.
guideData.preferredOption: Remain in the vehicle, keep visible to rescuers, and manage warmth with clothing, blankets, and carefully limited engine use.
guideData.fallbackOption: If staying is unsafe, such as due to buried exhaust, fuel leak, or another immediate hazard, move to a safer nearby shelter only if you can do so without getting lost or badly exposed.
steps: Stay with your vehicle; it is easier to find than a person on foot.
Check that the exhaust pipe is not blocked by snow or debris before running the engine.
Run the engine and heater only long enough to warm the interior, then shut it off to conserve fuel and reduce CO risk.
Open a window slightly on the side away from the wind for ventilation.
Wear all available layers and use blankets, sleeping bags, or extra clothing for insulation.
warnings: Blocked exhaust pipes or running engines in enclosed spaces can cause deadly carbon monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not sleep with the engine running continuously.
Do not use charcoal grills or camp stoves inside the vehicle.
redFlags: Headache, dizziness, nausea, or confusion while the engine is running.
Snow building up around the vehicle.
preparednessTips: Keep a winter car kit with blankets, hats, gloves, shovel, traction aid, food, water, and flashlight.
Maintain at least a half tank of fuel in winter.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specific advice on staying in vehicles and checking exhaust pipes.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Guidance for Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Explains CO dangers from engines and fuel devices.

guides.title: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet

guideData.title: Emergency Overnight Setup When You Are Already Wet
guideData.summary: Get under cover, remove wet layers if possible, insulate yourself from the ground, and warm your core with dry clothes or shared insulation.
guideData.whenToUse: Unexpected overnight in cold, wet conditions where you or others are already soaked.
guideData.preferredOption: Use any available shelter, change into dry layers, and use dry blankets or sleeping bags to protect the core first.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no dry clothes exist, wring out wet layers, put them back on, and maximize insulation with natural materials and shared coverings.
steps: Move immediately to the best available shelter.
Remove wet upper-body clothing and replace it with anything dry; protect the head and torso well.
Lay down thick insulation under you using packs, branches, leaves, cardboard, or extra clothing.
Wrap in all available blankets, sleeping bags, or dry fabrics.
Eat and drink if safe to fuel heat production and avoid more exposure outside the shelter.
warnings: Remaining in wet clothing in cold conditions can lead to hypothermia even above freezing temperatures.
whatNotToDo: Do not sit or sleep directly on bare ground or metal.
Do not rely only on a fire if building one would be slow, unsafe, or impossible.
redFlags: Any signs of moderate hypothermia such as confusion, slurred speech, or stumbling despite warming efforts.
preparednessTips: Pack at least one fully dry emergency set of base layers and socks in a waterproof bag for cold/wet trips.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Advises staying dry and warm.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Highlights changing out of wet clothing and insulation.

guides.title: Managing Moisture Inside Tents and Improvised Shelters

guideData.title: Managing Moisture Inside Tents and Improvised Shelters
guideData.summary: Ventilate enough to reduce condensation while keeping wind and rain out, and keep sleeping areas off wet ground.
guideData.whenToUse: When sleeping in tents or tarp shelters in damp, cold conditions.
guideData.preferredOption: Use ground insulation, open small vents or gaps, and keep wet gear separate from sleeping areas.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot ventilate well, wipe condensation periodically and rotate damp items out of sleeping areas.
steps: Pitch on slightly higher ground to avoid pooling water.
Use a groundsheet or tarp under the sleeping area, slightly smaller than the tent floor.
Open small vents or gaps at the top or downwind side to let moisture escape.
Keep wet clothing and boots away from sleeping insulation.
Wipe interior condensation with a cloth if it builds up.
warnings: Humidity and condensation can chill you overnight and dampen insulation, increasing hypothermia risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not fully seal all vents while cooking or using fuel appliances in or near the shelter.
redFlags: Drowsiness, headaches, or nausea while any fuel-burning device is in use nearby.
preparednessTips: Carry a small groundsheet and cloth for wiping condensation; learn your tent vents before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Weather Camping and Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Discusses keeping tents dry and managing moisture.

guides.title: Drying Wet Clothing and Bedding Safely Indoors

guideData.title: Drying Wet Clothing and Bedding Safely Indoors
guideData.summary: Hang wet items spread out in the warmest, best-ventilated space you have, away from heaters or flames that could ignite them.
guideData.whenToUse: After getting wet during cold weather once you are in an indoor or sheltered area.
guideData.preferredOption: Use clotheslines, hangers, or chairs to air-dry items while keeping them at least three feet from heaters.
guideData.fallbackOption: If heat is limited, prioritize drying socks, gloves, and base layers; wring items thoroughly and rotate them closer to safe heat sources while supervising constantly.
steps: Wring out wet clothes and bedding away from sleeping areas.
Hang items so air can circulate; avoid piling wet items in heaps.
Keep all fabrics at least three feet away from heaters, stoves, or open flames.
Ventilate briefly if possible to reduce dampness and mold risk.
Put on dry layers while items dry rather than waiting in wet clothing.
warnings: Clothing or bedding too close to heaters is a common cause of home fires.
whatNotToDo: Do not drape clothes directly on heaters or over open-flame devices.
Do not leave heaters unattended while drying items.
redFlags: Smoke, scorch marks, burning smell, or any small fire that is not immediately controllable.
preparednessTips: Keep clips or rope for indoor drying lines and maintain working smoke alarms near heating areas.

sourceReferences.title: Home Heating Safety Tips
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses keeping combustibles three feet from heat sources.

sourceReferences.title: Safety Tips: Portable Space Heaters
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical heater safety distances and guidance.

guides.title: Keeping Dry in Rain and Snow Outdoors

guideData.title: Keeping Dry in Rain and Snow Outdoors
guideData.summary: Use water-resistant outer layers, protect hands and feet, and minimize time in heavy rain or slush to prevent dangerous chilling.
guideData.whenToUse: Outdoor travel or work in wet, cold weather, especially when returning to shelter may be delayed.
guideData.preferredOption: Wear waterproof or water-resistant outer layers, keep spare dry socks and gloves, and avoid soaking your clothing when possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack waterproof gear, improvise with ponchos or plastic over critical areas and reduce exposure time.
steps: Put on or improvise a water-resistant shell over insulating layers.
Protect shoes and socks and change into dry socks when you can.
Keep gloves or mittens as dry as possible and carry a spare pair if available.
Limit time standing in slush, puddles, or wet snow.
Once under shelter, remove wet outer layers and hang them to dry.
warnings: Wet clothing greatly increases heat loss and hypothermia risk in cold or windy weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking in wet cotton layers if you can change.
Do not ignore wet feet and hands.
redFlags: Shivering that does not stop after shelter and dry clothes, or progressing clumsiness or confusion.
preparednessTips: Carry simple rain protection and at least one spare pair of socks during cold seasons.

sourceReferences.title: Preparing for a Winter Storm
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Urges staying dry to prevent heat loss.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes proper clothing and staying dry.

guides.title: Using Buildings and Tarps for Temporary Roof and Wall Protection

guideData.title: Using Buildings and Tarps for Temporary Roof and Wall Protection
guideData.summary: Use heavy-duty tarps to cover damaged roofs or broken windows, but stay off unsafe structures.
guideData.whenToUse: After storms or earthquakes when a building is mostly sound but has leaks or broken openings.
guideData.preferredOption: Cover small to moderate roof or wall damage with a properly anchored tarp to keep the interior dry until repairs are possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot safely access the damage, confine living space to dry rooms and use containers to catch leaks.
steps: Inspect roof and walls from the ground first; avoid climbing if anything looks unstable.
For reachable roof leaks, spread a tarp over the damage and extend it well past the leak.
Secure the tarp tightly with ropes, sandbags, or boards so it does not flap loose in the wind.
For broken windows, tape plastic sheeting or a tarp over the opening from the inside and seal the edges.
Keep one clear exit path and do not block vents needed by fuel-burning appliances.
warnings: Working on damaged roofs causes many post-disaster injuries; falls and structural collapse are serious risks.
whatNotToDo: Do not walk on obviously damaged roofs.
Do not cover chimneys or flue outlets that appliances need for safe venting.
redFlags: Sagging roof, large cracks, or leaning walls.
Strong gas smell or visible sparking wires near damage.
preparednessTips: Store at least one heavy-duty tarp, rope, and duct tape if you live in storm-prone areas.
Know whether local “blue roof” or similar programs operate in your area.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Details tarp design for emergency shelter and roof sheeting.

guides.title: Low A-Frame Tarp Shelter for Cold Nights

guideData.title: Low A-Frame Tarp Shelter for Cold Nights
guideData.summary: Pitch the tarp low in an A-frame with both sides close to the ground to conserve heat and block wind.
guideData.whenToUse: Overnight in cold, breezy, or light-snow conditions when you need more enclosure than a lean-to.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a strong ridgeline and pull both tarp sides down close to the ground, leaving only small gaps for airflow.
guideData.fallbackOption: If stakes are unavailable, use rocks, logs, or filled bags to hold the tarp edges down.
steps: Choose slightly elevated ground with a natural windbreak.
Tie a ridgeline between two anchors at knee-to-thigh height.
Center the tarp over the ridgeline and pull both sides down toward the ground.
Stake or weigh down the edges to create a low triangular tunnel, leaving small openings at each end for condensation control.
Lay thick insulation underneath and sleep away from the open ends.
warnings: Low shelters can collect condensation; wet gear and clothing reduce insulation and increase hypothermia risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not light a fire inside or directly under the tarp; tarps are flammable and smoke will build up quickly.
redFlags: Progressive cold, drowsiness, or confusion despite shelter and dry layers.
preparednessTips: Carry a ground pad and emergency bivy or space blanket to boost warmth inside tarp shelters.

sourceReferences.title: Cold Weather Camping and Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical guidance on keeping shelters dry and insulated.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emphasizes insulation and wind-blocking.

guides.title: Fast Tarp Lean-To for Wind and Rain

guideData.title: Fast Tarp Lean-To for Wind and Rain
guideData.summary: Pitch a tarp as a low lean-to facing away from the wind, with thick insulation underneath you.
guideData.whenToUse: For short-term protection from wind and rain when you need quick setup and access to the outside.
guideData.preferredOption: Anchor the top edge between two fixed points and keep the windward side low to block weather.
guideData.fallbackOption: If anchors are poor, use poles, paddles, or sturdy branches and weigh them down with rocks or packs.
steps: Face your back to the wind and find two anchors 2–3 meters apart.
Tie a ridgeline between them at about chest height.
Drape the tarp over the ridgeline so one side reaches the ground on the windward side.
Stake or weigh down the ground edge and angle the open side to shed rain while allowing ventilation.
Build a thick insulating layer underneath where you will sit or lie.
warnings: A lean-to blocks wind on one side only; in very cold or windy conditions a lower A-frame may be warmer.
whatNotToDo: Do not fully seal all edges in cold, damp conditions; trapped moisture can make you colder.
redFlags: Dropping temperature, stronger wind, or soaking wetness that you cannot control with shelter and layers.
preparednessTips: Practice one or two standard tarp pitches in good weather before you need them.

sourceReferences.title: Blue FEMA-Spec’d Tarps
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms tarps’ emergency-shelter role.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Stresses wind, rain, and ground insulation.

guides.title: Alert Systems: Smoke Alarms, Weather Radio, and Phone Alerts
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks

guideData.title: Alert Systems: Smoke Alarms, Weather Radio, and Phone Alerts
guideData.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guideData.summary: Use layered alerts: working smoke alarms, a weather radio, and emergency phone alerts so one failure does not leave you without warning.
guideData.whenToUse: When setting up home readiness, before severe-weather seasons, or when building a multi-channel warning system for the household.
guideData.preferredOption: Maintain smoke alarms, enable emergency phone alerts, and add a NOAA Weather Radio or equivalent official alert device where relevant.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot get a weather radio right away, make sure smoke alarms work and phone alerts are enabled while following local radio or TV channels.
steps: Install smoke alarms on every level and in or near bedrooms.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries or units as needed.
Set up a weather radio with battery backup if available in your area.
Enable Wireless Emergency Alerts or the local equivalent on phones.
Teach the household what each alarm or alert means and how to respond.
Keep backup charging options for phones and radios.
warnings: One alert method can fail or be missed.
Turning off alerts for convenience can leave you without life-saving warning.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore emergency alerts assuming they are overblown.
Do not place radios where no one can hear them overnight.
redFlags: Any severe emergency alert such as tornado warning, evacuation order, or active smoke alarm with no clear cause requires immediate action or cautious investigation.
If alarms sound and you cannot identify the cause safely, call authorities.
preparednessTips: Test weather radios and alert settings regularly.
Keep a backup battery pack for phones.

sourceReferences.title: Smoke alarm guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core home fire warning system guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Weather Radio and Wireless Emergency Alerts
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official layered warning-system information.

guides.title: Basic Home Hazard and Injury Prevention Check
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks

guideData.title: Basic Home Hazard and Injury Prevention Check
guideData.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guideData.summary: Walk through your home to reduce the everyday fire, trip, electrical, and chemical hazards that become worse during disasters.
guideData.whenToUse: When moving into a new home, before severe-weather seasons, or as an annual household safety check.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a room-by-room checklist to find and fix small hazards before they become emergency problems.
guideData.fallbackOption: If a full walkthrough is too much, start with bedrooms, exits, stairs, and the kitchen.
steps: Check for overloaded outlets and damaged cords.
Clear walkways and stairs and secure rugs.
Store flammables away from heat sources.
Lock chemicals away from children and separate them from food.
Secure heavy furniture and shelves in earthquake-prone areas.
Check exterior paths and make sure the house number is visible from the street.
warnings: Falls and fires are common home injuries that worsen under stress and poor lighting.
Small electrical issues can become major fire hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not ignore recurring flickering lights, hot outlets, or trip hazards.
Do not store flammables near stoves, heaters, or candles.
redFlags: Frayed wiring, scorch marks, chemical exposure with breathing trouble, or dangerous structural instability need urgent attention and sometimes evacuation.
preparednessTips: Repeat the same checklist yearly so improvements are trackable.
Involve older children in basic hazard awareness.

sourceReferences.title: Home hazard checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common room-by-room injury and fire prevention practices.

guides.title: Utility Shutoff and Gas Leak Safety
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks

guideData.title: Utility Shutoff and Gas Leak Safety
guideData.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guideData.summary: Know where your main shutoffs are and when to use them, but only shut utilities off if authorities recommend it or if there is an immediate hazard and you have been shown how.
guideData.whenToUse: During preparedness planning and during events like major leaks, flooding, or fires when safe shutoff is necessary.
guideData.preferredOption: Ask your utility, landlord, or a qualified professional to show you how and when to shut off gas, water, and electricity safely, and label the controls clearly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot safely identify or reach shutoffs, focus on evacuation and contacting utilities or emergency services.
steps: Locate main gas, water, and electrical shutoffs with help from a qualified person if needed.
Label each shutoff and post utility emergency numbers nearby.
Know that electrical panels near water, scorch marks, or buzzing should be left to professionals.
Follow local utility guidance for gas shutoff and remember that many utilities do not want residents turning gas back on themselves.
Know how to shut water off if instructed during contamination or major leaks.
warnings: Improper gas or electrical shutoff can cause fire, explosion, or electrocution.
whatNotToDo: Do not stand in water while touching electrical panels or appliances.
Do not attempt to restore gas service yourself.
Do not force stuck valves or switches.
redFlags: Water rising near electrical outlets or panels.
Sparking, smoke, or fire from panels or meters.
Damage to gas meters, lines, or any gas smell.
preparednessTips: Ask utility-recommended professionals to review your home shutoffs and provide written instructions.
Practice locating shutoffs in daylight so you can find them quickly if needed.

sourceReferences.title: What You Need to Know When the Power Goes Out Unexpectedly
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical CDC guidance on immediate outage actions, safety, food, and preparedness.

guides.title: Home Fire Safety and Escape Readiness
guides.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks

guideData.title: Home Fire Safety and Escape Readiness
guideData.parentTopic: Home Readiness Basics and Safety Checks
guideData.summary: Install and test smoke alarms, keep extinguishers accessible, and practice two escape routes from every room with a family meeting point.
guideData.whenToUse: For all households, especially multi-story homes or homes with children, older adults, or higher cooking/heating fire risk.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a written home fire safety checklist and practice an escape plan regularly.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot do everything immediately, at least test alarms, clear exits, and choose a meeting place.
steps: Install smoke alarms on every level and near sleeping areas.
Test alarms monthly and replace batteries as needed.
Replace alarms older than about 10 years.
Keep an ABC fire extinguisher on each floor if possible.
Identify two exits from each room and make sure they open easily.
Choose and practice an outside meeting place.
Keep exits clear of clutter.
warnings: Home fires spread fast and can leave only minutes to escape.
Once outside, re-entry is extremely dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not disable smoke alarms instead of fixing nuisance alarm causes.
Do not try to fight large or spreading fires instead of evacuating.
redFlags: If an alarm sounds and you see or smell smoke, evacuate and call emergency services.
If exits are blocked or mobility issues prevent escape, seek professional planning help before an emergency.
preparednessTips: Teach children to crawl low under smoke and never hide during a fire.
Use time-change dates or other regular reminders to test alarms.

sourceReferences.title: Smoke alarm and home escape planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: National fire-safety guidance for alarms and escape drills.

sourceReferences.title: Home hazard checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical room-by-room safety planning support.

guides.title: Pet Evacuation and Shelter Planning

guideData.title: Pet Evacuation and Shelter Planning
guideData.summary: Know where your pets can go before you need to evacuate. Confirm shelters, hotels, friends, or boarding options ahead of time.
guideData.whenToUse: When building family evacuation plans or before hazards likely to trigger evacuation orders such as flood, wildfire, storm, or hazardous-material incidents.
guideData.preferredOption: Identify pet-friendly options and write them into your family emergency plan before a disaster happens.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you have no guaranteed option, still take your pet and kit to the nearest emergency shelter and ask for pet support or referral.
steps: Research local pet-friendly shelters and emergency animal services.
List relatives, friends, boarding options, and pet-friendly hotels.
Write down addresses, contacts, and any vaccination requirements.
Keep carriers and leashes accessible and practice loading pets calmly.
Bring the pet kit and records on every evacuation.
warnings: Some general shelters only allow service animals inside main areas.
Pets without vaccination records may be refused at some facilities.
whatNotToDo: Do not release pets outdoors and assume they will survive alone.
Do not show up with uncontained aggressive animals if you can prevent that safely.
redFlags: If you must leave pets temporarily, notify authorities or animal services as soon as possible.
Injured pets need veterinary emergency care.
preparednessTips: Include pet plans in the family communication and reunion plan.
Practice short travel sessions so pets tolerate carriers and vehicles better.

sourceReferences.title: Pet evacuation planning
sourceReferences.organization: CDC / local emergency pet safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful planning structure for shelters and transport.

guides.title: Pet Emergency Go-Kit

guideData.title: Pet Emergency Go-Kit
guideData.summary: Prepare a pet kit with food, water, medications, records, and containment so your animal can evacuate or shelter with you.
guideData.whenToUse: When any household has pets and may need to evacuate, shelter in place, or stay away from home for days or weeks during a disaster.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep a labeled pet emergency kit stored near your household go-bags.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot prepare a full kit yet, at least keep ID, proof of vaccination, food, and a way to contain the pet ready.
steps: Store 2 weeks of pet food and safe water.
Pack bowls, a manual can opener if needed, medications, and written care instructions.
Include veterinary and vaccination records plus proof of ownership.
Keep collars, ID tags, microchip details, recent photos, and a carrier or crate ready.
Add sanitation and comfort items like litter, poop bags, toys, and bedding.
warnings: Shelters may not accept pets in all areas.
Stress can make animals escape or behave unpredictably during emergencies.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave pets behind if at all possible.
Do not assume pets can safely drink or eat from any available source.
redFlags: Difficulty breathing, seizures, persistent vomiting, or serious injury needs veterinary emergency help.
If pets must be left temporarily, notify animal services or authorities as soon as possible.
preparednessTips: Write clear feeding and medication instructions.
Keep carriers easy to reach, not buried in storage.

sourceReferences.title: Pet disaster preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong baseline kit and evacuation guidance for pets.

sourceReferences.title: Veterinary emergency preparedness
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Helpful detail on medicines, records, and transport.

guides.title: Power-Dependent Medical Devices Planning
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup

guideData.title: Power-Dependent Medical Devices Planning
guideData.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guideData.summary: If someone depends on powered medical equipment, plan now for outages, backup batteries, alternate locations, and emergency contacts.
guideData.whenToUse: When households rely on oxygen concentrators, CPAP/BiPAP, dialysis support, powered mobility devices, or similar equipment and outages are possible.
guideData.preferredOption: Create a written outage plan with your doctor, equipment provider, and utility company where available.
guideData.fallbackOption: If formal medical-priority utility registration is not available, prepare charged batteries and alternate powered locations ahead of time.
steps: List each power-dependent device and its battery runtime.
Ask clinicians and suppliers about outage procedures and transport needs.
Check whether your utility has a medical-priority registration process.
Arrange backup power or alternate safe locations.
Store device instructions and emergency numbers with your medical documents.
Practice moving critical devices safely for evacuation.
warnings: Indoor generator use causes fatal carbon monoxide risk.
Improvised unsafe wiring can create fire and shock hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume responders automatically know you rely on power-dependent equipment.
Do not leave all batteries uncharged or stored in flood-prone areas.
redFlags: If a critical device fails and no backup exists, call emergency services immediately.
If a person dependent on oxygen, ventilation, or dialysis is in distress, seek urgent care.
preparednessTips: Create a one-page device summary for responders.
Keep spare essential device parts when possible.

sourceReferences.title: Home medical equipment outage planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Consistent preparedness principles for device-dependent households.

guides.title: Household Critical Documents Kit
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup

guideData.title: Household Critical Documents Kit
guideData.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guideData.summary: Gather copies of IDs, insurance, legal, financial, property, and emergency documents into a protected grab-and-go kit before disaster damages the originals.
guideData.whenToUse: When building home emergency kits and go-bags or before floods, wildfires, storms, or evacuations that could destroy or isolate original documents.
guideData.preferredOption: Create one protected paper-and-digital document kit that can be grabbed quickly during evacuation.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot copy everything at once, start by listing key account numbers, contact numbers, IDs, and insurance details.
steps: Collect IDs, passports, birth certificates, and similar documents.
Add insurance policies, account details, wills, powers of attorney, and property records.
Include home and vehicle photos or inventories.
Store copies in a waterproof, fire-resistant folder and secure digital backup.
Keep the kit in an easy-to-reach but protected location.
Review after major life changes.
warnings: Leaving all originals in one vulnerable spot can lead to complete loss.
Sensitive documents can be misused if poorly protected.
whatNotToDo: Do not store the only copy in a damp basement without protection.
Do not share full document copies casually or through insecure channels.
redFlags: If identity documents are destroyed or stolen, start replacement and fraud-protection steps quickly.
If legal or medical decision documents are unavailable before urgent care, inform providers immediately.
preparednessTips: Keep a checklist on the folder cover so you know what should be inside.
Review annually.

sourceReferences.title: Household document kit guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used public guidance for critical document readiness.

guides.title: Medical Information and Health Document Backup
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup

guideData.title: Medical Information and Health Document Backup
guideData.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guideData.summary: Keep copies of medical summaries, allergies, insurance information, and provider contacts in both paper and digital form so care can continue when systems are disrupted.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for chronic illness, complex medical history, allergies, travel, evacuation, or any situation where records may be hard to access quickly.
guideData.preferredOption: Create a waterproof health document packet plus secure digital copies that can travel with you or your go-bag.
guideData.fallbackOption: If a full packet is hard to build, at least create a one-page medical summary with medicines, allergies, diagnoses, and emergency contacts.
steps: Include medication lists, allergies, diagnoses, surgeries, implants, and provider contacts.
Add insurance cards or numbers and emergency contact details.
Store copies in a waterproof folder with other critical documents.
Keep digital copies on a secure device or cloud account with safe password access.
Update the packet annually or after major medical changes.
warnings: Outdated medical info can cause mistakes.
Sensitive health information should be stored securely.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on one clinic's electronic record system.
Do not assume emergency providers will know your history without your help.
redFlags: If records are unavailable during a serious medical event, seek care immediately and communicate what you know.
If your health data is exposed or stolen, notify providers and insurers promptly.
preparednessTips: Keep a short medical summary for each family member in go-bags.
List preferred hospitals or clinics when useful.

sourceReferences.title: Health document backup guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Good basis for medical summary and record continuity.

guides.title: Essential Medication Backup Planning
guides.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup

guideData.title: Essential Medication Backup Planning
guideData.parentTopic: Medical Needs, Medicines, and Document Backup
guideData.summary: Work with clinicians and pharmacists to maintain a backup supply of essential medicines and a clear medication list before disasters disrupt access.
guideData.whenToUse: When anyone in the household depends on daily or life-sustaining prescription medicines, especially before storm, wildfire, flood, or outage seasons.
guideData.preferredOption: Create an emergency medication plan that includes backup supply, safe storage, rotation, and a current medication list.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot get extra medicine, keep an up-to-date list of names, doses, prescribers, and pharmacies so care can be restored faster.
steps: Make a list of every essential medication, dose, schedule, doctor, and pharmacy.
Ask your doctor or pharmacist about keeping a 7–30 day buffer supply if possible.
Understand refill and insurance rules before disasters happen.
Store medicine in original labeled packaging in a cool, dry place.
Plan specifically for temperature-sensitive medicines.
Rotate older medicine first and monitor expiry dates.
Keep copies of the medication list in your wallet, kit, and with a trusted contact.
warnings: Stopping or changing critical medicines suddenly can be dangerous.
Improper storage can make some medicines unsafe or ineffective.
whatNotToDo: Do not mix pills into unlabeled containers.
Do not store medicines in overheated cars or direct sun.
redFlags: Running out of critical medication with symptoms such as chest pain, seizure activity, severe shortness of breath, or dangerous blood sugar problems needs urgent care.
Flood- or fire-contaminated medicine should not be used without professional guidance.
preparednessTips: Keep photos of prescription labels with your document kit.
Record medication allergies clearly.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency medication planning
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong principles for refill planning, documentation, and storage.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness for medicines
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports backup supply planning and medical readiness.

guides.title: Shelter-in-Place Kit (Home)

guideData.title: Shelter-in-Place Kit (Home)
guideData.summary: Prepare a dedicated kit so your household can remain indoors safely for at least three days during storms, air-quality events, chemical releases, or security incidents.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when authorities advise staying indoors rather than evacuating, or when travel is too dangerous and the household must remain inside.
guideData.preferredOption: Build a dedicated shelter-in-place kit that can be moved quickly into your chosen safe room.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no dedicated kit exists, gather your 72-hour home supplies into the room you will use for sheltering.
steps: Pack three days of water and food per person.
Add flashlights, batteries, and a radio.
Include sanitation items and simple waste-management supplies.
Keep plastic sheeting, duct tape, scissors, and towels for room sealing if official instructions call for it.
Store medications, first aid, pet supplies, and comfort items with the kit.
Identify the room where the kit would be used.
warnings: Chemical or smoke incidents may require sealing the room and turning off ventilation only if authorities tell you to do so.
Indoor generator use can cause fatal carbon monoxide poisoning.
whatNotToDo: Do not leave doors or windows open during a shelter-in-place order for smoke or chemical danger.
Do not rely only on candles for lighting.
redFlags: Breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, or loss of consciousness need emergency help.
If you smell strong gas or chemical odors inside, follow emergency instructions and evacuate if safe and advised.
preparednessTips: Choose an interior room with few windows in advance.
Keep a printed shelter-in-place steps sheet with the kit.

sourceReferences.title: Shelter-in-Place Checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common household room-sealing and supply guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness kit guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Good overlap with dedicated indoor shelter kits.

guides.title: Practice Drills and Plan Refresh

guideData.title: Practice Drills and Plan Refresh
guideData.summary: Plans work better when you rehearse them. Run simple drills for evacuation, meeting places, and communication, then update your plan based on what you learn.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after creating or changing a family emergency plan and at least once a year thereafter.
guideData.preferredOption: Schedule simple age-appropriate drills for evacuation, check-ins, and shelter-in-place scenarios.
guideData.fallbackOption: If full drills are hard to run, do table-talk walk-throughs and scenario discussions instead.
steps: Practice a home evacuation and meeting-point drill.
Walk to neighborhood reunion points with children.
Test communication methods with your out-of-area contact.
Run a basic shelter-in-place practice in the chosen room.
After each drill, note what worked and what failed.
Update your written plan and kit contents accordingly.
warnings: Avoid causing panic or trauma during drills, especially for children or anxious household members.
Do not simulate dangerous conditions in ways that create real hazards.
whatNotToDo: Do not skip drills because everyone 'already knows' the plan.
Do not practice only one scenario forever.
redFlags: If mobility or anxiety issues prevent safe evacuation practice, seek professional or local emergency-management guidance.
Severe distress during drills may justify health-professional support.
preparednessTips: Tie drills to time changes, school start, or storm seasons as reminders.
Let children occasionally help lead simple drills.

sourceReferences.title: USFA fire safety materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Drill and escape-practice principles.

sourceReferences.title: Family plan templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful annual-refresh structure for household plans.

guides.title: School, Work, and Caregiver Coordination

guideData.title: School, Work, and Caregiver Coordination
guideData.summary: Your family plan should fit the emergency procedures used by schools, workplaces, and caregivers—not conflict with them.
guideData.whenToUse: When children attend school or childcare, adults work away from home, or anyone depends on outside caregivers or home-health support.
guideData.preferredOption: Collect written emergency procedures and contact details from every school, workplace, and caregiver involved in your household plan.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no formal plan is available, confirm by phone how the location handles lockdowns, evacuations, and reunification.
steps: For each school or childcare site, record contact numbers and pick-up rules.
For each workplace, note evacuation or shelter-in-place procedures.
List agencies, backup caregivers, and contact numbers for people with extra support needs.
Align your meeting places and pickup assumptions with those outside plans.
Store copies of these procedures with your family plan and go-bags.
Review them at the start of each school or work year.
warnings: Schools and workplaces may not release people early during some emergencies.
Unauthorized pickup may be refused.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume schools or employers will automatically notify your chosen out-of-area contact.
Do not leave caregivers without clear written emergency instructions.
redFlags: If you cannot locate a child or dependent person during an emergency, speak to site officials and call authorities if needed.
If a medically dependent person is stranded without care, contact emergency services.
preparednessTips: Save emergency hotline numbers for schools and workplaces in phones and on paper.
Review coordination details every year.

sourceReferences.title: State emergency family planning guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful coordination framework for families, schools, and caregivers.

sourceReferences.title: Municipal preparedness checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical pick-up and reunification planning ideas.

guides.title: Meeting Places and Reunion Planning

guideData.title: Meeting Places and Reunion Planning
guideData.summary: Choose and document meeting places near home, in the neighborhood, and outside your area so everyone knows where to go if separated.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before disasters that might separate family members, such as fires, floods, wildfires, evacuations, or area-wide communication failures.
guideData.preferredOption: Define at least three meeting places and share them clearly with everyone in the household.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no prior plan exists, send one clear meeting point by text as soon as you become aware of a safe gathering location.
steps: Choose a near-home meeting point for small incidents like house fires.
Choose a neighborhood location in case you cannot return home.
Choose an out-of-area meeting point for major evacuations.
Write the addresses and when each point should be used.
Practice walking key routes with children and caregivers.
Review and update the locations when hazards, roads, or home address changes.
warnings: Some locations may become unsafe during specific disasters; review them with hazard context in mind.
Official shelter or evacuation instructions may override your default reunion locations.
whatNotToDo: Do not choose vague places that children or visitors may misunderstand.
Do not assume everyone will pick the same place without documenting it.
redFlags: If someone fails to arrive at a meeting point in known danger conditions, contact authorities.
If a child is missing near an active hazard, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Mark meeting places on a printed map kept in kits.
Share reunion information with babysitters, schools, and close neighbors.

sourceReferences.title: Family Emergency Communication Plan Templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard household planning format used broadly.

sourceReferences.title: Get a Kit, Make a Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family planning guidance with contacts and meeting points.

guides.title: Basic Family Emergency Communication Plan

guideData.title: Basic Family Emergency Communication Plan
guideData.summary: Write down who to contact, how to reach them, and where to meet if phones, travel, or routines are disrupted during an emergency.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for all households, especially those with children, older adults, caregivers, or family members who spend the day in different places.
guideData.preferredOption: Create a simple printed family communication plan and give every household member a copy.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot print, handwrite the plan and store photos of it on phones plus one paper copy in a visible home location.
steps: List names, mobile numbers, emails, and any key medical needs.
Choose one out-of-area contact who can relay updates.
Write down multiple contact methods such as text, calls, and email.
Record emergency numbers and local official hotlines.
Create wallet cards with emergency contacts and meeting points.
Store copies at home, in kits, and on phones.
Review annually or after any move or number change.
warnings: Phone networks may be overloaded; texts may work when calls do not.
A single app or single device is not enough for family communication resilience.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume everyone remembers phone numbers from memory.
Do not keep the only copy of the plan on one phone.
redFlags: If family members are unreachable during an active life-threatening incident, contact authorities.
If a dependent person with urgent medical needs loses caregiver contact, seek emergency help.
preparednessTips: Teach children how to call emergency services and state their name and address.
Set ICE contacts on phones.

sourceReferences.title: Family Emergency Communication Plan Templates
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Standard household planning format used broadly.
