sourceReferences.whyUseful: Family planning guidance with contacts and meeting points.

guides.title: Kit Maintenance and Rotation Schedule

guideData.title: Kit Maintenance and Rotation Schedule
guideData.summary: Emergency kits only work if you check and refresh them regularly. Replace expired food, water, batteries, and medications on a schedule.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after creating kits and then every 6–12 months or before major risk seasons.
guideData.preferredOption: Set a recurring reminder and use a checklist to review every kit on a regular schedule.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot do a full check, do a quick scan for expired food, dead batteries, low medications, and outdated documents.
steps: Schedule a kit review every 6–12 months.
Discard and replace expired or damaged food and water.
Check emergency medications and refill backup supplies when possible.
Test flashlights, radios, smoke alarms, and battery-powered devices.
Update IDs, contact lists, insurance copies, and family-specific items.
Relabel or repack kits with the latest review date.
warnings: Expired medications, swollen cans, dead batteries, and stale water can make a kit unreliable at the worst moment.
Home safety systems that fail can leave you without warning during fast-moving emergencies.
whatNotToDo: Do not build kits and forget them for years.
Do not use kit items casually unless you have a strict replace-after-use habit.
redFlags: If a disaster is underway and critical medicines or home warning systems are missing or broken, seek replacement or safe alternate shelter urgently.
If smoke alarms or carbon monoxide alarms fail and cannot be restored, consider temporary safer lodging where appropriate.
preparednessTips: Use first-in, first-out rotation for stored food.
Keep a printed maintenance log with dates and notes.

sourceReferences.title: Preparedness Kit Maintenance
sourceReferences.organization: American Red Cross / municipal preparedness checklists
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common schedule-based kit maintenance guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Smoke Alarm Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for integrating alert-system maintenance with kit maintenance.

guides.title: Car / Work Mini Emergency Kit (24–72 Hours)

guideData.title: Car / Work Mini Emergency Kit (24–72 Hours)
guideData.summary: Keep a compact emergency kit in your car or workplace so you can manage delays, sheltering, or being stranded away from home.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for commuting, remote driving, workplace lockdowns, severe weather travel, or short disruptions that keep you away from home.
guideData.preferredOption: Build a compact separate kit for your vehicle and another for your workplace with water, food, light, first aid, and basic tools.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot keep a full kit, at least keep water, snacks, a flashlight, and a small first-aid kit available.
steps: Use a small bag or bin that fits in your trunk or under a desk.
Add bottled water, sealed snacks, and a basic first-aid kit.
Include a flashlight, spare batteries, and warmth items like a hat, gloves, socks, or emergency blanket where climate requires.
Pack a whistle, multitool, small duct tape, and a paper map.
Store printed key phone numbers and alternate route information.
Check food, water, and seasonal clothing twice a year.
warnings: Cars become dangerous in extreme heat or cold.
Heat can damage some stored items in vehicles.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on phone navigation and battery life.
Do not store dangerous heat-sensitive aerosol items in parked vehicles.
redFlags: If you are stranded in dangerous temperatures, injured, or your vehicle smells like fuel or is smoking, call for help early.
If you cannot safely remain with the vehicle, move only if conditions and official guidance support it.
preparednessTips: Keep at least half a tank of fuel during storm or wildfire seasons.
Leave a medical-condition note in the glove box if useful for responders.

sourceReferences.title: 72-Hour Kit / Preparedness guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common compact-kit structure for commuting and workplace use.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Kit Fact Sheets
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Helpful small-kit planning patterns for mobility.

guides.title: Personal Evacuation Go-Bag (Adult)

guideData.title: Personal Evacuation Go-Bag (Adult)
guideData.summary: Keep a small bag ready with water, food, light, communications, first aid, hygiene, documents, and basic tools to leave home quickly.
guideData.whenToUse: For evacuations, sudden disasters, or when you must leave home or work on short notice.
guideData.preferredOption: Follow official emergency kit guidance and customize for your household.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot assemble a full bag, gather the highest-priority items: medications, water, IDs, a flashlight, and a way to contact family.
steps: Pack non-perishable food and water for several days where feasible.
Add flashlight, batteries or hand-crank light, radio, phone charger, and power bank.
Include first aid, personal medications, and hygiene items.
Add copies of important documents and some cash in small bills.
Include seasonal clothing, an emergency blanket, and a whistle.
Store the bag in an accessible location and review it at least annually.
warnings: A heavy kit may be hard to carry; balance completeness with portability.
Medications expire and must be rotated regularly.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on last-minute packing during an evacuation.
Do not store the kit where it can be blocked in an emergency.
redFlags: If local authorities issue evacuation or shelter-in-place orders, follow them and bring your bag.
If a family member runs out of critical medications, seek medical or pharmacy support quickly.
preparednessTips: Follow local or national emergency preparedness websites for region-specific recommendations.
Include items for children, elders, and pets as needed.
Practice a quick grab-and-go drill with your household.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed official grab-and-go guidance for household evacuation readiness.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong official U.S. emergency kit guidance on water, food, documents, tools, and medicines.

guides.title: 72-Hour Home Survival Kit (Shelter at Home)

guideData.title: 72-Hour Home Survival Kit (Shelter at Home)
guideData.summary: Store at least three days of water, food, light, radio, first aid, sanitation, and key documents in one clearly labeled home kit.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before storms, earthquakes, or prolonged service disruptions when you may need to remain at home without safe water, power, or outside help.
guideData.preferredOption: Build one centralized kit sized for everyone in the household for at least 72 hours.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot fully stock 72 hours, start with water, flashlight, radio, medications, and basic food, then expand over time.
steps: Plan at least 1 gallon of water per person per day for 3 days.
Pack 3 days of shelf-stable, easy-to-eat food and a manual can opener.
Add flashlights, spare batteries, and a battery-powered or hand-crank radio.
Include first aid, prescription medications, and essential medical supplies.
Pack sanitation items, hygiene supplies, blankets, sturdy shoes, and copies of critical documents.
Add infant, child, pet, or special-needs items as required.
Review and refresh the kit every 6–12 months.
warnings: Utilities, tap water, and fuel may be unavailable after major disasters.
Temperature-sensitive medicines and devices need special planning during outages.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on cook-only foods that require large amounts of water or fuel.
Do not scatter supplies randomly around the house without one clearly marked core kit.
redFlags: Running out of life-sustaining medication, safe water, or basic shelter supplies during an active emergency needs urgent outside help or official support.
Chest pain, confusion, breathing trouble, or medication failure should be treated as urgent medical issues.
preparednessTips: Aim toward a 2-week home supply over time even if 72 hours is your baseline.
Keep a written inventory taped to or stored inside the kit.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Preparedness Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used household kit and go-bag guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit / Make A Plan materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Core all-hazards preparedness checklists used nationwide.

guides.title: Cleaning and Disinfection to Prevent Disease Spread

guideData.title: Cleaning and Disinfection to Prevent Disease Spread
guideData.summary: Clean visible dirt first, then disinfect high-touch and contaminated surfaces with an appropriate disinfectant or bleach solution made using safe water.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during and after emergencies when flooding, sewage issues, or illness increase contamination risk around the home or shelter.
guideData.preferredOption: Use safe water, soap or detergent, and an appropriate disinfectant on toilets, kitchens, handles, and visibly contaminated areas.
guideData.fallbackOption: If supplies are limited, prioritize toilets, food-prep areas, and high-touch surfaces first.
steps: Remove visible dirt with soap and water before disinfecting.
Prepare disinfectant safely according to instructions.
Disinfect toilets, sinks, counters, handles, and contaminated surfaces.
Allow the proper contact time before drying or wiping.
Use gloves and ventilation when using bleach or disinfectants.
warnings: Floodwater or sewage-contaminated items may need to be discarded if they cannot be cleaned effectively.
Mixing bleach with ammonia or acids creates toxic fumes.
whatNotToDo: Do not use contaminated water for cleaning food-contact surfaces.
Do not mix cleaning products together.
Do not use the same tools for toilet and kitchen cleaning without proper disinfection.
redFlags: Rashes, breathing issues, or eye irritation after chemical exposure.
Persistent sewage contamination after cleaning attempts.
preparednessTips: Store detergent, disinfectant, bleach, gloves, buckets, and cloths.
Keep printed dilution and safety instructions with your supplies.
Use separate cleaning tools for toilets versus kitchens.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency environmental cleaning guidance
sourceReferences.organization: CDC WASH and emergency hygiene materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports household cleaning and disinfection after contamination events.

guides.title: Hand Hygiene When Water or Soap Is Scarce

guideData.title: Hand Hygiene When Water or Soap Is Scarce
guideData.summary: Wash hands with safe water and soap when possible; otherwise use alcohol-based sanitizer on visibly clean hands and prioritize hygiene before eating, feeding children, or wound care.
guideData.whenToUse: Use in emergencies with limited safe water, damaged water systems, or crowded conditions that increase disease spread.
guideData.preferredOption: Wash with safe water and soap for at least 20 seconds after toilet use and before food or wound care.
guideData.fallbackOption: If safe water or soap is unavailable, use alcohol-based sanitizer on visibly clean hands after wiping away dirt if possible.
steps: Clean hands after toilet use, waste handling, food prep, feeding, and wound care.
Use safe water and soap when available.
If using sanitizer, cover all hand surfaces and rub until dry.
Set up hygiene stations near toilets and food prep if possible.
warnings: Sanitizer works poorly on visibly dirty or greasy hands.
Crowded shelters and disrupted sanitation increase disease spread.
whatNotToDo: Do not wash hands before food prep with contaminated water.
Do not wipe hands on visibly dirty cloths as a substitute for hand cleaning.
redFlags: Rising diarrhea, vomiting, or respiratory illness despite hygiene efforts in a household or shelter.
preparednessTips: Include soap, sanitizer, and disposable towels in emergency kits.
Plan dedicated handwashing or hygiene stations near toilet and kitchen areas.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency hygiene guidance
sourceReferences.organization: CDC WASH and IFRC / Red Cross
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports hand hygiene priorities under limited-water conditions.

guides.title: Emergency Toilet and Waste Management

guideData.title: Emergency Toilet and Waste Management
guideData.summary: Keep human waste contained, covered, and away from food, living spaces, and water sources using lined buckets, improvised toilets, or approved disposal methods.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when toilets do not flush or sewage systems are damaged, unavailable, or backing up during an emergency.
guideData.preferredOption: Use functioning toilets only while authorities say sewer systems are safe.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no toilet setup is possible, use a designated emergency latrine or pit only where local conditions and guidance make that safe.
steps: Stop flushing if sewage systems are not working and backup is possible.
Set up a sturdy bucket or toilet frame lined with heavy-duty bags.
Add absorbent material after each use.
Seal and store bags in a covered container until safe disposal.
Keep waste areas away from food, children, and pets.
Follow local disposal instructions if available.
warnings: Improper waste disposal can spread diarrheal disease and contaminate water sources.
Do not dispose of waste into open water or floodwater.
whatNotToDo: Do not mix emergency toilet waste with regular household trash unless authorities instruct it.
Do not reuse waste bags or containers without thorough disinfection.
Do not allow sewage backup to continue unchecked if you can safely isolate the problem.
redFlags: Community outbreak-like diarrhea after sanitation disruption.
Visible sewage backing into homes or communal living areas.
Leaking or unsealed waste storage near living spaces.
preparednessTips: Store heavy-duty bags, absorbent material, disinfectants, and a bucket or portable toilet.
Learn local emergency waste disposal guidance in advance.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH sanitation guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong basis for emergency toilet setup and contamination prevention.

guides.title: Maritime Electronic Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)

guideData.title: Maritime Electronic Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
guideData.summary: At sea, use VHF radio distress calling, DSC, EPIRBs, and other approved electronic systems as primary alerting tools, with visual signals as backup for localization.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for boating or offshore travel where marine radio and beacon systems are part of the emergency response system.
guideData.preferredOption: Use VHF marine radio, DSC, and EPIRBs or similar approved devices as your main distress systems.
guideData.fallbackOption: If marine systems are unavailable, use any phone, satellite device, or general PLB while staying with the vessel if safe.
steps: Program and test marine radio and distress features before the trip.
Register and maintain EPIRBs correctly.
In distress, send a DSC alert if available, make a Mayday call, and activate the EPIRB if needed.
Maintain watch for responses and continue visual signaling as rescuers approach.
warnings: Marine systems depend on correct setup, registration, and user familiarity.
Loss of vessel power can disable radios; self-powered beacons are a critical backup.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume a phone alone is enough offshore.
Do not turn off an activated beacon without clear rescue-resolution reason or instructions.
redFlags: Any major loss of vessel safety or serious medical emergency at sea warrants immediate distress communication.
preparednessTips: Practice distress procedures with crew.
Store handheld radios and beacons where they can be reached instantly in abandon-ship situations.

sourceReferences.title: Marine distress communications overview
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for Mayday, distress systems, and safety communication.

sourceReferences.title: Integrated maritime distress systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports role of DSC, EPIRB, and related systems.

guides.title: Maritime Visual Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)

guideData.title: Maritime Visual Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
guideData.summary: On water, use coast-guard-approved visual distress signals such as flares, orange distress flags, smoke, and approved electronic lights; treat land-based signaling patterns only as secondary aids.
guideData.whenToUse: Use at sea or on large lakes where maritime regulations and search-and-rescue systems apply.
guideData.preferredOption: Carry and use approved visual distress devices appropriate to your vessel and waters, supplemented by mirror, horn, and bright visible items.
guideData.fallbackOption: If approved devices are unavailable in a true emergency, use any visible and audible means you have while calling for help by radio or phone.
steps: Know and carry the distress equipment required for your vessel and waters.
In an emergency, use radio first if possible, then deploy the most suitable day or night visual signal.
Use mirrors, bright cloth, and arm motion only as secondary aids.
Continue signaling intermittently rather than wasting all devices at once.
warnings: Pyrotechnic flares and smoke devices can cause burns or fires if handled badly.
Not all bright LED devices meet legal distress-signal standards.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume land whistle or mirror conventions meet maritime legal requirements.
Do not bury distress gear where it cannot be reached quickly.
redFlags: Flooding, fire aboard, loss of propulsion in dangerous conditions, or a serious medical emergency at sea require immediate distress signaling.
If flares or lights get no response, continue with radio and other signaling methods.
preparednessTips: Inspect and replace flares or time-limited devices regularly.
Train all crew on where distress gear is stored and how to deploy it.

sourceReferences.title: Visual Distress Signals
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for maritime distress-signal requirements and legal distinctions.

sourceReferences.title: Visual distress signal effectiveness testing
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence for signal visibility in real-world conditions.

guides.title: Beacon and Locator Basics (PLB, EPIRB, Satellite Messengers)

guideData.title: Beacon and Locator Basics (PLB, EPIRB, Satellite Messengers)
guideData.summary: Use an approved emergency beacon or SOS-capable satellite messenger as your most reliable long-range distress tool, but still pair it with visual and audible signals.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for remote travel with limited or no cell service where rescue may be slow or difficult and you need a reliable emergency alerting tool.
guideData.preferredOption: For land travel, carry a registered PLB or SOS-capable satellite messenger.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack a beacon, rely on voice or text contact if possible, combined with traditional signaling and a clearly left trip plan.
steps: Register and test your beacon according to official guidance before the trip.
In a real emergency, activate SOS and keep the device where it has the clearest possible sky view.
If two-way communication is available, send details about injuries, terrain, and conditions.
Once activated, avoid unnecessary movement unless staying put is clearly unsafe.
Use whistle, light, and visible markers to help rescuers pinpoint you.
warnings: False activations can trigger major rescue responses; use only for real emergencies.
Dense canopy, deep canyons, and buildings can reduce signal quality or delay updates.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on phone apps or consumer navigation apps in remote areas.
Do not turn the beacon off once activated unless rescue is complete or you are clearly instructed to do so.
redFlags: Serious injury, severe weather exposure, or terrain trapping with no straightforward self-rescue route.
Loss of safe mobility or survival risk increasing faster than you can self-evacuate.
preparednessTips: Treat a beacon as core safety gear for regular remote trips.
Carry it on your person rather than buried in luggage or pack.
Keep emergency contacts and registration details current.

sourceReferences.title: Beacon registration and use guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for registration and owner information requirements.

sourceReferences.title: Marine distress and signaling systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for EPIRB, distress systems, and rescue integration.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency communication planning guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports pairing beacon use with visible rescue signals.

guides.title: Burns That Need Emergency Care

guideData.title: Burns That Need Emergency Care
guideData.summary: Large, deep, or critical-area burns, or burns from chemicals, electricity, or inhalation need urgent medical attention.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for burns that are more than minor superficial injuries or if the burn affects breathing, critical areas, or large surface area.
guideData.preferredOption: Begin basic burn first aid while arranging urgent medical care or evacuation.
guideData.fallbackOption: If evacuation is delayed, continue gentle cooling if appropriate and keep the area clean and loosely covered while monitoring airway and circulation.
steps: Assess size, depth, and location.
Call emergency services or arrange urgent transport.
Stop the burning source.
Cool with cool water for up to 20 minutes if still hot.
Remove tight items if not stuck.
Cover loosely and monitor breathing.
warnings: Serious burns can cause fluid loss, shock, and airway compromise.
Deep burns may hurt less but still be more dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply home remedies or thick creams to severe burns.
Do not delay transport after basic first aid is done.
redFlags: Deep burns, face/airway burns, breathing difficulty, large burns, electrical, lightning, or chemical burns require urgent care.
preparednessTips: Know where the nearest emergency care or burn-capable hospital is.
Teach quick burn classification by size and location.

sourceReferences.title: Burn First Aid and Burn Center Referral
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specialist guidance for escalation criteria.

sourceReferences.title: Burns Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-facing burn first aid and when to seek help.

guides.title: First Aid for Burns (Minor)

guideData.title: First Aid for Burns (Minor)
guideData.summary: Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 20 minutes, remove tight items, and cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for small, superficial or superficial-partial burns that do not involve critical areas extensively.
guideData.preferredOption: Cool with cool running water for at least 20 minutes.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no tap water is available, use clean cool water or repeated cool wet cloths.
steps: Stop the burning source.
Cool the area with cool water for 20 minutes.
Remove jewelry and tight items if not stuck.
Cover loosely with a clean dressing or plastic wrap.
Monitor for worsening or infection.
warnings: Even minor burns can worsen over time.
Children and older adults may be more vulnerable to complications.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply ice directly.
Do not use butter, oils, or thick ointments that trap heat.
Do not intentionally break blisters.
redFlags: Burns larger than the person’s palm, burns to face/hands/feet/genitals/major joints, electrical or chemical burns, or infection signs need urgent care.
preparednessTips: Keep clean water access, non-stick dressings, and plastic wrap in the kit.
Teach the 20-minute cooling rule.

sourceReferences.title: Burn First Aid
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Specialist burn first-aid guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Burns: How to Help
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Public-friendly burn-care guidance.

guides.title: Wound Infection: Red Flags

guideData.title: Wound Infection: Red Flags
guideData.summary: Watch for spreading redness, swelling, warmth, worsening pain, pus, red streaks, or fever, and seek care early if they appear.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when monitoring any wound over hours to days, especially dirty wounds, bites, punctures, or wounds in people with chronic illness.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep the wound clean and dry and inspect it daily.
guideData.fallbackOption: If care is delayed, continue gentle cleaning and dry dressings while seeking the earliest possible medical review.
steps: Inspect the wound daily in good light.
Look for spreading redness or swelling.
Check for warmth, worsening pain, or throbbing.
Look for pus or foul-smelling drainage.
Monitor for fever or feeling unwell.
Seek medical care if symptoms worsen or spread.
warnings: Infections near joints, face, or in people with diabetes or immune problems can worsen quickly.
whatNotToDo: Do not tightly seal an infected or draining wound.
Do not start leftover antibiotics without professional advice.
redFlags: Rapidly spreading redness, red streaks, high fever, severe pain, or inability to move the area normally require urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep a thermometer and spare dressings in your kit.
Use notes or photos to monitor changes over time.

sourceReferences.title: Wound Infection Warning Signs
sourceReferences.organization: Public health ministry / standard wound care guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common red-flag descriptions aligned with public first-aid practice.

guides.title: After Landslide

guideData.title: After Landslide
guideData.summary: Stay away from unstable ground and damaged structures until authorities or qualified experts say it is safe.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after the slide has stopped and you are deciding whether to return, search, or inspect damage.
guideData.preferredOption: Keep clear of the affected area.
guideData.fallbackOption: If re-entry is delayed, use safer shelter and wait for official or expert advice.
steps: Check for trapped or injured people only if it is safe.
Report utility damage if possible.
Listen for emergency updates.
Do not enter visibly unstable areas.
warnings: Additional slides may follow the first event.
Unstable buildings, road edges, and buried utilities remain hazardous.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter unstable structures.
Do not assume the slope is stable because movement has stopped once.
redFlags: Structural cracks, trapped people, gas leaks, downed lines, or flooding require urgent emergency help.
preparednessTips: Consult geotechnical or local hazard resources if you live in a landslide-prone area.

sourceReferences.title: Post-Landslide Safety Guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Reasonable public-safety principles, but source base should be reviewed and strengthened.

guides.title: Tornado Safety in a Mobile Home

guideData.title: Tornado Safety in a Mobile Home
guideData.summary: Leave mobile homes early for a sturdy shelter when tornado warnings are issued. Mobile homes are not safe tornado shelters.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you are in or near a mobile home during a tornado watch or warning.
guideData.preferredOption: Go to a sturdy building or designated shelter immediately.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot reach sturdy shelter in time, move to the safest available low area away from trees and vehicles if that is your only option.
steps: Monitor alerts closely.
Leave for shelter early rather than waiting to confirm the tornado visually.
Protect your head and neck once in shelter.
warnings: Mobile homes can overturn or fail even in weaker tornadoes and straight-line winds.
whatNotToDo: Do not stay inside a mobile home during a tornado warning.
Do not wait until debris is already visible if a warning is active.
redFlags: If someone is trapped, injured, or shelter cannot be reached safely, call emergency services as soon as possible.
preparednessTips: Know your nearest sturdy shelter and route in advance.
Keep a go-bag and weather radio ready.

sourceReferences.title: Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public guidance that mobile homes are unsafe shelters.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness and shelter decision guidance.

guides.title: Tornado Safety in a Vehicle

guideData.title: Tornado Safety in a Vehicle
guideData.summary: If possible, drive to a sturdy shelter immediately. Do not try to shelter under an overpass.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when a tornado warning occurs while you are in a car or on the road.
guideData.preferredOption: Drive to the nearest sturdy building if there is time and a safe route.
guideData.fallbackOption: If escape is impossible and conditions demand immediate protection, move to the lowest safe place available away from vehicles and debris if you can do so safely.
steps: Look for the nearest sturdy building.
Do not stop under bridges or overpasses.
If you can reach shelter safely, do so immediately.
If not, protect your head and neck and follow local emergency guidance.
warnings: Vehicles are poor tornado shelters.
Road visibility and debris may change very quickly.
whatNotToDo: Do not try to outrun a tornado if traffic, storm movement, or visibility make it unsafe.
Do not shelter under overpasses.
redFlags: If the vehicle is damaged, overturned, or you are injured, call emergency services when possible.
preparednessTips: Plan frequent weather checks during tornado season.
Know sturdy shelter options along regular routes.

sourceReferences.title: Tornado Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Scenario-specific vehicle guidance from the core authority.

sourceReferences.title: Tornadoes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Preparedness and shelter guidance.

guides.title: Driving in Flood Conditions

guideData.title: Driving in Flood Conditions
guideData.summary: Never drive through flooded roads. Turn around, find another route, and move to higher ground if needed.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when roads are flooded, rainfall is intense, or visibility and drainage conditions are poor.
guideData.preferredOption: Turn around and find a safer route.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot safely leave the vehicle and water is not yet rising into it, call for help and monitor conditions closely.
steps: Slow down and assess conditions before entering low areas.
Do not cross water-covered roads.
If your car stalls in rising water, get out if safe and move to higher ground.
Avoid bridges or roads that look damaged.
warnings: Moving water can sweep vehicles away in surprisingly shallow depths.
Road damage may be hidden beneath floodwater.
whatNotToDo: Do not drive around barricades.
Do not assume you can judge depth or road condition from the driver seat.
redFlags: If trapped in rising water, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Know alternate routes.
Keep a charged phone and emergency kit in the car during flood season.

sourceReferences.title: Turn Around Don't Drown
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Best single-source public rule for flood driving safety.

guides.title: Sanitation and Waste Handling Basics

guideData.title: Sanitation and Waste Handling Basics
guideData.summary: Clean water, hand hygiene, and waste handling basics during disruption.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when toilets, water service, or sewer service are disrupted.
warnings: Improper waste disposal near water sources can contaminate drinking water.
Poor hand hygiene during disruptions leads to rapid spread of gastrointestinal illness.
redFlags: Signs of waterborne illness: diarrhea, vomiting, or fever after poor hygiene conditions.
Wounds showing signs of infection in a low-hygiene environment.

sourceReferences.title: Sanitation and Hygiene After a Disaster

guides.title: Communication Outage Basics

guideData.title: Communication Outage Basics
guideData.summary: Fallback communication habits when networks are overloaded or unavailable.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when cell, internet, or local communication networks fail during emergencies.
warnings: Overloaded networks may give false impression that calls went through.
Draining your phone battery attempting calls on an overloaded network reduces emergency use time.
redFlags: Inability to reach emergency services by any available method.
Worsening medical or safety emergency with no way to call for help.

guides.title: Power Outage Basics

guideData.title: Power Outage Basics
guideData.summary: Basic lighting, food, refrigeration, charging, and safety actions during blackouts.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during unplanned loss of electricity at home or work.
warnings: Using candles, charcoal, or fuel-burning devices indoors during outages creates fire and carbon monoxide risk.
Medication that requires refrigeration may become unsafe.
redFlags: Gas smell, CO alarm, structural damage, or fire related to the outage.
Medication requiring refrigeration becoming unavailable.
Medical device failure due to power loss.

sourceReferences.title: Power Outages

sourceReferences.title: Food Safety During and After an Emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Federal guidance on safe food handling without refrigeration.

guides.title: Shelter in Place Basics

guideData.title: Shelter in Place Basics
guideData.summary: Sometimes staying inside is safer than evacuating. Prepare to remain indoors with supplies and information.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for storms, hazardous air, unrest, utility disruptions, or other cases where official guidance says staying inside is safer.
guideData.preferredOption: Move everyone into the safest indoor area available and maintain communication, supplies, and situational awareness.
steps: Bring people, pets, and essential supplies inside.
Close doors, windows, and vents if the hazard involves outdoor contamination or smoke.
Use official updates to know when conditions change.
Conserve water, power, and phone battery if disruption may last.
warnings: Shelter in place is not appropriate if there is a gas leak, fire, or structural damage in the building.
Sealed rooms have limited air supply; do not shelter in place for extended periods without air monitoring guidance.
redFlags: Gas smell, fire, or structural damage inside the shelter.
Official order to evacuate issued while sheltering in place.
CO alarm activation.

guides.title: Evacuation Go-Bag Checklist

guideData.title: Evacuation Go-Bag Checklist
guideData.summary: Keep a fast-grab bag ready for evacuations so you are not scrambling during a crisis.
guideData.whenToUse: Use where sudden evacuation is possible due to fire, flood, earthquake damage, storms, or conflict.
guideData.preferredOption: Pack a portable bag with essentials you can carry quickly.
steps: Pack medicines, documents, chargers, water, snacks, basic hygiene items, flashlight, and cash.
Include clothing layers and protective footwear if space allows.
Add child, pet, or disability-specific items if needed.
Store the bag near an exit and review it regularly.
warnings: Bags too heavy to carry quickly may delay evacuation.
Do not delay evacuation waiting to pack more items.
redFlags: Evacuation order issued with no bag ready.
Go-bag contents include medications that have expired or are now missing.

guides.title: Make a Family Emergency Plan

guideData.title: Make a Family Emergency Plan
guideData.summary: A good emergency plan tells everyone how to reconnect, where to meet, and what to do if communication fails.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for household preparedness before disasters or trips.
guideData.preferredOption: Choose meeting points, assign roles, and pick an out-of-area contact.
steps: Discuss likely local hazards and what each person should do.
Choose household and neighborhood meeting points.
Pick an out-of-area contact who can relay information.
Write down key phone numbers and addresses.
Practice the plan periodically.
warnings: Plans not practiced regularly are forgotten under stress.
Plans that do not account for all household members, including pets and those with medical needs, may fail during real emergencies.
redFlags: Cannot locate a household member during an emergency with no agreed meeting point or contact plan.

guides.title: Build a 72-Hour Emergency Kit

guideData.title: Build a 72-Hour Emergency Kit
guideData.summary: Build a simple grab-and-stay emergency kit with water, food, light, first aid basics, medicines, documents, and communication tools.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before disasters such as earthquakes, floods, wildfire, storms, or outages.
guideData.preferredOption: Build a ready-to-grab kit with enough core supplies for at least several days.
steps: Store water and non-perishable food.
Add lighting, batteries, radio, first aid basics, medicines, hygiene items, and a whistle.
Keep copies of important documents and emergency contacts.
Include local maps, phone charging options, and basic tools.
Check the kit regularly and replace expired items.
warnings: Kits not maintained or rotated regularly may contain expired food, medications, or depleted batteries when needed.
redFlags: You are forced to evacuate immediately with no kit available.
Kit contents are expired or unusable.

guides.title: Emergency Water Storage

guideData.title: Emergency Water Storage
guideData.summary: Store emergency water before disasters so you are not forced to improvise under pressure.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for home, vehicle, workplace, or travel preparedness planning.
guideData.preferredOption: Store a multi-day supply of clean water in safe containers and rotate it regularly.
steps: Use food-grade containers or trusted sealed water supplies.
Label and date stored water.
Keep water in a cool, protected location.
Rotate or refresh stored supplies according to trusted guidance.
warnings: Water containers must be food-grade; do not reuse containers that held toxic substances.
Stored water needs to be rotated and refreshed periodically.
redFlags: Signs of severe dehydration if stored supply runs out.
Water supply smells, looks discolored, or was stored in improper containers.
preparednessTips: Plan extra water for hot climates, children, medical needs, and pets.

guides.title: When a Wound Needs Urgent Medical Care

guideData.title: When a Wound Needs Urgent Medical Care
guideData.summary: Some wounds need urgent professional care even if bleeding is partly controlled.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when deciding whether a cut, puncture, or wound can be managed simply or needs urgent care.
guideData.preferredOption: Seek urgent medical care for deep, jagged, heavily contaminated, numb, or infected wounds.
steps: Assess depth, contamination, bleeding, pain, and function.
Look for numbness, loss of movement, exposed tissue, or severe contamination.
Watch for infection signs such as spreading redness, swelling, pus, or fever.
Get medical help if these signs are present or if you are unsure.
warnings: Delay can worsen infection, tissue damage, and healing.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt invasive field closure or advanced wound procedures on your own.
redFlags: Exposed bone or fat, persistent bleeding, numbness, severe contamination, spreading infection, or systemic illness require urgent care.

guides.title: Severe Bleeding: Get Help Fast

guideData.title: Severe Bleeding: Get Help Fast
guideData.summary: Severe bleeding is an emergency. Apply direct pressure immediately and get emergency help.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when blood is spurting, soaking through dressings rapidly, or the person shows signs of shock or collapse.
guideData.preferredOption: Call emergency services or get urgent help while someone applies firm direct pressure.
steps: Call for emergency help or direct someone to do it.
Apply firm direct pressure immediately.
Keep the person lying down if possible and monitor breathing and responsiveness.
Continue pressure until advanced help takes over or bleeding is clearly controlled.
warnings: Uncontrolled severe bleeding can be fatal in minutes.
whatNotToDo: Do not delay emergency help while searching for perfect supplies.
redFlags: Loss of consciousness, trouble breathing, severe weakness, or ongoing uncontrolled bleeding are immediate emergencies.

guides.title: Minor Cuts and Scrapes: Clean and Cover

guideData.title: Minor Cuts and Scrapes: Clean and Cover
guideData.summary: Rinse the wound with clean water, remove visible dirt, cover it with a clean dressing, and watch for infection.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for shallow cuts and scrapes with slow, controllable bleeding and no deep tissue or large gap visible.
guideData.preferredOption: Gently clean with clean running water.
guideData.fallbackOption: If clean running water is not available, use the cleanest bottled or saline water you have and gently remove obvious dirt.
steps: Wash or sanitize your hands if possible.
Apply gentle pressure if minor bleeding continues.
Rinse under clean water.
Clean around the wound with mild soap.
Pat the surrounding skin dry.
Cover with a clean dressing.
Change the dressing daily or when wet or dirty.
warnings: Dirty wounds and high-risk contamination increase infection risk.
People with diabetes or poor circulation may need earlier medical review.
whatNotToDo: Do not repeatedly use full-strength hydrogen peroxide, alcohol, or iodine inside the wound.
Do not close deep or heavily contaminated wounds in the field.
redFlags: Gaping wounds, bites, embedded objects, visible bone/tendon/fat, uncontrollable bleeding, or high-risk contamination need urgent medical advice.
preparednessTips: Keep dressings, saline or clean water, and mild soap in your kit.
Keep tetanus vaccination up to date.

sourceReferences.title: Cuts and Puncture Wounds

guides.title: Cold Weather Prevention and Layering
guides.parentTopic: Cold Weather: Prevention and Field Care

guideData.title: Cold Weather Prevention and Layering
guideData.parentTopic: Cold Weather: Prevention and Field Care
guideData.summary: Stay dry, layer clothing, protect extremities, and limit exposure during cold, wet, or windy conditions.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before winter travel, outdoor work, trekking, storms, or prolonged cold-weather exposure.
guideData.preferredOption: Use layered clothing with moisture management and keep backup dry gear available.
steps: Wear layered clothing with moisture-wicking and insulating materials when possible.
Keep spare dry socks, gloves, and a hat available.
Protect from wind and wet conditions early.
Limit time exposed in severe cold or wind chill.
Check companions frequently for early symptoms.
warnings: Cold injuries can develop faster in wind, wet conditions, exhaustion, or poor nutrition.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on cotton alone in wet, cold conditions if better options are available.
redFlags: Uncontrollable shivering, confusion, slurred speech, or extreme fatigue in cold.
Skin that is hard, white, or has lost sensation.

guides.title: After the Tornado

guideData.title: After the Tornado
guideData.summary: After winds stop, watch for debris, injuries, gas leaks, and downed power lines before moving through damaged areas.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after the tornado passes and you are deciding whether it is safe to leave shelter or help others.
guideData.preferredOption: Check injuries and immediate hazards first.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the structure is unsafe, move carefully to safer shelter and follow emergency instructions.
steps: Use shoes and gloves if available.
Avoid sharp debris and unstable buildings.
Report utility problems if possible.
Monitor radio or official alerts for updates.
warnings: Additional tornadoes may follow the first.
Debris, dust, gas leaks, and damaged wiring remain dangerous after the storm.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter heavily damaged buildings alone.
Do not touch downed lines or damaged electrical systems.
redFlags: Stopped breathing, severe bleeding, trapped people, gas smell, or collapse hazards require emergency help.
preparednessTips: Keep dust masks, flashlights, and sturdy footwear in your shelter kit.

guides.title: Tornado Safety: If You Are in a Vehicle or Mobile Home

guideData.title: Tornado Safety: If You Are in a Vehicle or Mobile Home
guideData.summary: A sturdy building is safer than a vehicle or mobile home. Leave for shelter if you can do so early and safely.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during a tornado warning when you are in a car, truck, RV, or mobile home.
guideData.preferredOption: Get to a sturdy building before the tornado reaches you if there is time and a safe route.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no sturdy shelter is reachable, get low in a safer nearby low area only if conditions make that safer than remaining exposed.
steps: Monitor official alerts and decide early.
Leave mobile homes for sturdy shelter as soon as warnings are issued.
If driving and a sturdy building is nearby, go there promptly.
If no shelter is reachable, protect your head and neck and get as low as practical.
warnings: Vehicles and mobile homes can overturn or be struck by debris.
Low areas may also flood in some storms.
whatNotToDo: Do not try to outrun a close tornado in traffic.
Do not shelter under bridges or overpasses.
redFlags: Tornado visible or sirens active with no solid shelter available.
Vehicle lifted, rocked, or struck by debris.

guides.title: Tornado Safety: Take Shelter Immediately

guideData.title: Tornado Safety: Take Shelter Immediately
guideData.summary: Get to the lowest interior room of a sturdy building and protect your head and neck immediately.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during a tornado warning, when a tornado is sighted, or when there are clear tornado danger signs.
guideData.preferredOption: Go to a basement, storm shelter, or lowest interior room.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no basement is available, use a small interior room such as a closet, hallway, or bathroom on the lowest floor.
steps: Move quickly to the safest room.
Get low under sturdy furniture if available.
Cover your head and neck with your arms, a helmet, or a mattress/blanket if available.
Stay there until the all-clear.
warnings: Flying debris causes many tornado injuries.
Mobile homes are especially dangerous.
whatNotToDo: Do not shelter under overpasses.
Do not stay near windows.
Do not wait to visually confirm the tornado if an official warning is issued.
redFlags: Severe injuries, collapse, or trapped people require emergency help as soon as safe.
preparednessTips: Plan a safe room in advance.
Practice tornado drills twice yearly.
Keep a weather radio and helmets or head protection in your shelter area.

guides.title: Returning Home After Flooding

guideData.title: Returning Home After Flooding
guideData.summary: Avoid floodwater and damaged structures until hazards are checked. Treat all floodwater as contaminated.
guideData.whenToUse: Use after floodwater recedes or when returning to a flooded home, neighborhood, or road.
guideData.preferredOption: Stay out until officials say it is safe where possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If return is delayed or the structure is unsafe, use temporary shelter and wait for official guidance.
steps: Wear protective gear if returning.
Do not touch downed power lines or wet electrical systems.
Clean wounds exposed to floodwater promptly.
Use safe drinking water only; boil or treat if advised.
Photograph damage if needed for records once safe.
warnings: Floodwater may contain sewage, chemicals, sharp debris, and electrical hazards.
Hidden structural damage may cause collapse or falls.
whatNotToDo: Do not enter buildings with major visible damage alone.
Do not touch wet electrical equipment.
Do not assume water or food is safe after flooding.
redFlags: Electrical shocks, serious injuries, infected wounds, gas leaks, or unstable buildings need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep waterproof storage for documents and supplies.
Store gloves, boots, flashlights, and cleaning supplies in your emergency kit.

guides.title: Floodwater Safety

guideData.title: Floodwater Safety
guideData.summary: Treat all floodwater as contaminated and dangerous. Avoid direct contact whenever possible.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when walking near, cleaning after, or being exposed to floodwater.
guideData.preferredOption: Avoid floodwater whenever possible.
guideData.fallbackOption: If exposure is unavoidable, use boots, gloves, and the safest route possible, then clean up immediately afterward.
steps: Stay out of floodwater when possible.
If contact occurs, wash with clean water and soap as soon as possible.
Cover open wounds before exposure if you can.
Discard or disinfect contaminated items according to public-health guidance.
warnings: Floodwater may contain sewage, chemicals, sharps, and hidden electrical risks.
whatNotToDo: Do not let children play in floodwater.
Do not assume clear-looking water is safe.
redFlags: Seek urgent help for infected wounds, electrical exposure, chemical burns, or severe illness after exposure.
preparednessTips: Keep protective gloves, boots, and wound-care supplies available during flood season.

sourceReferences.title: Floodwater Safety
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary health guidance on contamination and cleanup after floods.

guides.title: Flood Survival: Get to Higher Ground

guideData.title: Flood Survival: Get to Higher Ground
guideData.summary: Move to higher ground immediately and follow evacuation orders. Never walk or drive through floodwater.
guideData.whenToUse: Use during flood warnings, flash floods, fast-rising water, or when water is entering roads, buildings, or low areas.
guideData.preferredOption: Go to higher ground immediately.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot evacuate safely, move to the highest safe level of the building, but avoid attics without a way out.
steps: Monitor alerts by radio or phone.
If safe, shut off utilities.
Take essentials and leave early.
Avoid flooded roads and low crossings.
Move to higher ground or official shelter.
warnings: Floodwater rises quickly and may hide strong currents, contamination, and electrical hazards.
Six inches of moving water can knock a person down; deeper water can move vehicles.
whatNotToDo: Do not walk, swim, or drive through floodwater.
Do not ignore barriers or evacuation orders.
redFlags: If trapped, water is entering the home, or someone is swept away or injured, call emergency services immediately.
preparednessTips: Know evacuation routes.
Store a 3-day kit with water, medications, and documents.

guides.title: Earthquake Preparedness Checklist

guideData.title: Earthquake Preparedness Checklist
guideData.summary: Prepare before a quake by securing heavy items, practicing drills, and keeping essential supplies and utility knowledge ready.
guideData.whenToUse: Use before earthquakes as part of household, workplace, or school readiness planning.
guideData.preferredOption: Secure heavy furniture and appliances.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot do everything, start with securing the most dangerous heavy items and building a simple family plan.
steps: Anchor bookshelves and cabinets.
Move heavy items to lower shelves.
Know how to turn off gas, electricity, and water if safe and trained.
Build and refresh an emergency kit.
Practice where to shelter in each room.
warnings: Unsecured furniture and glass are major indoor injury hazards.
Aftershocks can injure people even after the first quake has passed.
whatNotToDo: Do not wait until after an earthquake to create your family plan.
Do not store all emergency supplies in one hard-to-reach place.
redFlags: If your home shows major structural weakness or known earthquake risk issues, seek professional safety advice.
preparednessTips: Keep local maps and emergency contacts accessible.
Include supplies for children, older adults, pets, and medications.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: Tsunami Risk After a Quake

guideData.title: Earthquake Safety: Tsunami Risk After a Quake
guideData.summary: If you are near the coast and strong or long shaking occurs, move to higher ground or inland as soon as it is safe.
guideData.whenToUse: Use in coastal or tsunami-risk areas after strong or long earthquake shaking.
guideData.preferredOption: Evacuate on foot to higher ground or inland as soon as the shaking stops.
steps: Protect yourself during shaking first.
As soon as it is safe, go inland or to higher ground if strong or long shaking occurred.
Avoid beaches, shorelines, and low coastal roads.
Stay away until officials say it is safe.
warnings: Tsunami waves can arrive quickly after coastal earthquakes.
Multiple waves may follow.
whatNotToDo: Do not go to the shore to watch the water.
Do not return after the first wave.
redFlags: Coastal earthquake followed by rapid ocean withdrawal.
Official tsunami warning or alarm.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: Right After Shaking

guideData.title: Earthquake Safety: Right After Shaking
guideData.summary: Once shaking stops, check for injuries and hazards before moving or re-entering any damaged area.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when the shaking has stopped and you need to decide whether to stay, leave, or seek help.
guideData.preferredOption: Check yourself and others for injuries.
guideData.fallbackOption: If the building looks unsafe, stay out and move to a safer open area away from structures and power lines.
steps: Treat urgent injuries if you can do so safely.
If you smell gas or see fire, evacuate immediately.
Do not use candles if gas leaks are possible.
Use a flashlight for dark areas.
Be ready for aftershocks and repeat Drop, Cover, and Hold On if needed.
warnings: Aftershocks can cause additional collapses.
Damaged wiring, fuel leaks, and unstable structures remain dangerous after the main shaking.
whatNotToDo: Do not re-enter heavily damaged buildings.
Do not use open flames near possible gas leaks.
Do not ignore official evacuation or coastal tsunami guidance.
redFlags: Gas leaks, fire, collapse risk, downed power lines, or serious injuries need urgent help.
preparednessTips: Keep sturdy shoes, a flashlight, and emergency supplies accessible.
Have a family meeting point and an out-of-area contact.

guides.title: Earthquake Safety: During Shaking

guideData.title: Earthquake Safety: During Shaking
guideData.summary: Drop, Cover, and Hold On under sturdy furniture or next to an interior wall away from glass and falling objects.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when you feel shaking, receive an earthquake alert, or experience a sudden jolt that makes standing difficult.
guideData.preferredOption: Drop to your hands and knees.
guideData.fallbackOption: If no sturdy furniture is available, cover your head and neck and get low beside an interior wall away from windows and heavy objects.
steps: Drop where you are.
Get under sturdy furniture if available.
Cover your head and neck.
Hold on until all shaking stops.
Stay inside until the shaking ends unless you are already in a clear outdoor area.
warnings: Expect aftershocks.
Falling glass, shelves, and fixtures cause many injuries.
whatNotToDo: Do not run outside while the building is shaking.
Do not use elevators.
Do not stand in doorways as your main strategy.
redFlags: Serious injuries, heavy bleeding, major structural damage, or gas smell require urgent emergency help.
If near the coast and shaking was strong or long, be alert for tsunami evacuation needs as soon as you can move safely.
preparednessTips: Practice earthquake drills.
Secure heavy furniture and appliances.
Know how to turn off utilities if trained and safe to do so.

guides.title: Land Navigation

guideData.title: Land Navigation
guideData.summary: Use map and compass if you have them; otherwise use only broad terrain awareness for cautious short decisions, and avoid ambitious travel when lost.
guideData.whenToUse: Use when GPS has failed, battery is low, or accuracy is poor and you need a fallback strategy on land.
guideData.preferredOption: Use map and compass together to orient yourself and plan short safe movement along obvious terrain handrails like roads, trails, ridges, or major valleys.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you lack tools or skill, use the sun and major terrain only for broad orientation, not precise navigation, and prefer staying put over long uncertain travel.
steps: If you have a map and compass, orient the map to north and identify obvious terrain features around you.
Match roads, rivers, ridges, valleys, and lakes to the map.
Plan short, conservative travel legs along clear handrails.
If visibility is poor or your location estimate is unstable, stop traveling and shelter instead.
If you have no tools, use the sun only for general direction and avoid relying on folk methods as precise navigation.
warnings: Many natural-navigation folklore tricks are unreliable.
Fog, storms, darkness, and whiteout conditions can defeat even experienced navigators.
Streams and valleys do not automatically lead safely to help.
whatNotToDo: Do not over-trust smartphone navigation if battery is low or the device is cold.
Do not commit to long dead-reckoning travel in unfamiliar terrain.
Do not treat moss, plant growth, or similar folklore as reliable directional tools.
redFlags: You repeatedly cannot match terrain to your map or keep changing your estimated position.
Visibility drops to the point that safe travel is no longer realistic.
You are becoming exhausted, hypothermic, or heat-stressed while trying to navigate.
preparednessTips: Carry a paper map and simple baseplate compass even if using GPS.
Practice map and compass skills in easy terrain before relying on them remotely.
Learn local terrain features and hazards before your trip.

guideData.title: First Aid
guideData.summary: Emergency medical treatment before professional help.
guideData.whenToUse: Use for deeper browsing, educational questions, and as supporting reference under action cards.
warnings: First aid is not a substitute for professional medical care.
Do not attempt procedures beyond your training.
redFlags: Wounds that are large, deep, gaping, or involve the face, hands, genitals, or major joints.
Signs of shock: pale, clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion, or fainting.

guides.title: Knot Tying
guides.parentTopic: Cordage, Knots, and Safe Tool Use

guideData.title: Knot Tying
guideData.parentTopic: Cordage, Knots, and Safe Tool Use
guideData.summary: Use adjustable knots that can tension and re-tension guy lines to keep tarps and bivouacs stable.
guideData.whenToUse: Any time you need to tension lines for tarps, tents, or improvised shelters.
guideData.preferredOption: Use a taut-line hitch on guy lines to create an adjustable loop that slides when slack and grips when loaded.
guideData.fallbackOption: Use simple wraps around trees and multiple half hitches if you do not know the named knots, understanding they are less adjustable.
steps: Wrap cord around a stake or tree and create an adjustable loop in the standing line.
For a taut-line hitch, make two wraps inside the loop toward the anchor and one wrap outside, then tighten.
Slide the knot to adjust tension on the tarp as needed.
For a trucker’s hitch, create a loop in the standing line, run the working end through an anchor point and back through the loop, then pull to tension and secure with half hitches.
warnings: Incorrectly tied knots can slip and cause shelter failure in bad weather.
High loads on thin cord can cut into bark or damage anchors.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload light cordage with heavy loads such as body weight.
Do not rely on camping knots for technical rescue or climbing.
redFlags: Shelter repeatedly collapsing in severe weather when you cannot maintain warmth and dryness.
preparednessTips: Practice a very small set of knots at home until you can tie them under stress.
Carry several meters of strong cord or paracord in your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Knots for Survival and Rescue
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used visual reference for basic, non-technical knots, though it also includes advanced content the app should exclude.

guides.title: Fire Making

guideData.title: Fire Making
guideData.summary: Prepare a safe fire site, build a small structure of tinder, kindling, and fuel, then light the tinder and add wood gradually.
guideData.whenToUse: Outdoor situations where a fire is allowed and safe, for warmth, signaling, or boiling water.
guideData.preferredOption: Use dry fine tinder, small kindling, and then larger fuel, protecting the flame from wind and keeping the fire small.
guideData.fallbackOption: If matches or a lighter are weak, use multiple small tinder bundles and shield them carefully while lighting.
steps: Confirm local rules and safety before building a fire.
Clear the fire area down to mineral soil and away from overhanging branches, tents, and flammables.
Gather three fuel sizes: tinder, kindling, and larger fuel wood.
Arrange tinder in a small bundle and build kindling around it with space for air.
Light the tinder from the windward side and add more kindling, then larger wood, as the fire strengthens.
warnings: Even small fires can spread quickly in dry or windy conditions; embers can ignite nearby vegetation.
whatNotToDo: Do not use gasoline or unknown flammable liquids.
Do not leave a fire unattended or build it against roots, logs, or peat soils.
redFlags: Flames spreading beyond your cleared area or wind pushing sparks toward structures or vegetation.
preparednessTips: Carry waterproof matches or a lighter and reliable tinder in a waterproof container when traveling outdoors.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor fire-building guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common safety-focused outdoor fire-starting practice, adapted to local rules.

guides.title: Morse Code Alphabet and Numbers

guideData.title: Morse Code Alphabet and Numbers
guideData.summary: The complete International Morse Code character table (A–Z and 0–9) as standardized by ITU-R M.1677-1, with timing rules and tips for using Morse code to signal in an emergency.
guideData.whenToUse: When you need to look up a specific letter or number to encode a message in Morse code
guideData.preferredOption: Use the SOS pattern (· · · − − − · · ·) as your default distress signal — it is universally recognized and does not require the receiver to know Morse code.
guideData.fallbackOption: If you cannot remember SOS, three of anything repeated (three flashes, three blasts, three fires, three rocks) is a recognized distress signal in many rescue contexts.
warnings: Sending incorrect Morse signals may confuse rescuers or obscure your distress message.
Spending excessive time encoding messages instead of seeking shelter, warmth, or water can worsen your survival situation.
Morse code is only useful if someone is watching or listening — make sure rescuers are in range before prolonged signaling.
In low-light conditions, a flashlight battery drains quickly; use short, efficient SOS bursts rather than extended transmissions.
whatNotToDo: Do not insert pauses between the S, O, and S groups when sending SOS — it is one continuous prosign, not three separate letters.
Do not use random flashing or tapping without a pattern — rescuers may not recognize it as a distress signal.
Do not attempt to encode long sentences in an emergency — stick to SOS or very short messages (HELP, HERE).
redFlags: Inability to signal due to darkness, injury, or equipment failure with no rescue team in sight.
Battery depletion before rescue arrives with no alternative signaling method available.
preparednessTips: ALPHABET (ITU-R M.1677-1):
A = · −  (dit-dah)
B = − · · ·  (dah-dit-dit-dit)
C = − · − ·  (dah-dit-dah-dit)
D = − · ·  (dah-dit-dit)
E = ·  (dit)
F = · · − ·  (dit-dit-dah-dit)
G = − − ·  (dah-dah-dit)
H = · · · ·  (dit-dit-dit-dit)
I = · ·  (dit-dit)
J = · − − −  (dit-dah-dah-dah)
K = − · −  (dah-dit-dah)
L = · − · ·  (dit-dah-dit-dit)
M = − −  (dah-dah)
N = − ·  (dah-dit)
O = − − −  (dah-dah-dah)
P = · − − ·  (dit-dah-dah-dit)
Q = − − · −  (dah-dah-dit-dah)
R = · − ·  (dit-dah-dit)
S = · · ·  (dit-dit-dit)
T = −  (dah)
U = · · −  (dit-dit-dah)
V = · · · −  (dit-dit-dit-dah)
W = · − −  (dit-dah-dah)
X = − · · −  (dah-dit-dit-dah)
Y = − · − −  (dah-dit-dah-dah)
Z = − − · ·  (dah-dah-dit-dit)
NUMBERS (0–9):
0 = − − − − −  (five dahs)
1 = · − − − −  (dit-dah-dah-dah-dah)
2 = · · − − −  (dit-dit-dah-dah-dah)
3 = · · · − −  (dit-dit-dit-dah-dah)
4 = · · · · −  (dit-dit-dit-dit-dah)
5 = · · · · ·  (five dits)
6 = − · · · ·  (dah-dit-dit-dit-dit)
7 = − − · · ·  (dah-dah-dit-dit-dit)
8 = − − − · ·  (dah-dah-dah-dit-dit)
9 = − − − − ·  (dah-dah-dah-dah-dit)
TIMING RULES (ITU-R M.1677-1):
A dash (−) lasts three times as long as a dot (·).
Space between signals within the same letter = 1 dot duration.
Space between letters = 3 dot durations.
Space between words = 7 dot durations.

MEMORIZATION TIP: Frequency-weighted design means common letters have short codes. E = · (one dot), T = − (one dash), I = · · (two dots), A = · − (two signals). Start by memorizing S (· · ·), O (− − −), and E (·).
PRINT AND LAMINATE: Keep a printed copy of this chart in your emergency kit, go-bag, or vehicle glove box — you may need it when you cannot access this app.

sourceReferences.title: Recommendation ITU-R M.1677-1: International Morse Code
sourceReferences.whyUseful: The definitive international standard defining every Morse code character and timing rule — primary source for the full A–Z and 0–9 tables.

sourceReferences.title: Army Field Manual FM 21-76: Survival
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms Morse code as a core field signaling skill and identifies SOS as the primary distress call for survivor signaling in field conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Morse Code
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Comprehensive overview of Morse code history, design principles, and the frequency-weighted alphabet structure useful for memorization guidance.
